topics.title: Plot the IV and PV Characteristics of a Solar Photovoltaic Module and Determine Maximum Power Point Under Uniform and Partial Shaded Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL607 - ELECTRICAL SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Plot the IV and PV Characteristics of a Solar Photovoltaic Module and Determine Maximum Power Point Under Uniform and Partial Shaded Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Solar Photovoltaic (PV) Module** is a packaged, interconnected assembly of photovoltaic cells that converts incident solar irradiance directly into direct current (DC) electricity through the **photovoltaic effect** discovered by Edmond Becquerel in 1839. The terminal behavior of a PV module is characterized by two fundamental relationships: the **Current-Voltage (I-V) characteristic** and the **Power-Voltage (P-V) characteristic**, both of which are nonlinear and highly sensitive to irradiance ($G$) and cell temperature ($T$).

The **Maximum Power Point (MPP)** is the unique operating point on the I-V curve where the product of terminal voltage and current is maximized. The numerical value of this maximum extractable power is denoted as $P_{max}$, and the corresponding current and voltage are $I_{mp}$ and $V_{mp}$ respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A PV module's I-V curve is plotted under **Standard Test Conditions (STC)**: irradiance $G = 1000\ \text{W/m}^2$, cell temperature $T_{cell} = 25\degree\text{C}$, and Air Mass $\text{AM} = 1.5$. The MPP shifts significantly under **Partial Shading Conditions (PSC)** where the P-V curve exhibits multiple local maxima and one global maximum.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a **water tap connected to a water tank on a hill**. 

- The **water pressure** at the tap is like the **Voltage (V)** of the solar module.
- The **flow rate of water** coming out is like the **Current (I)**.
- The **power of the water jet** (pressure × flow rate) is like the **electrical power (P = V × I)**.

If you open the tap fully, water gushes out fast (high current) but with very low pressure (low voltage) — so the power is low. If you almost close the tap, pressure is high (high voltage) but flow is just a trickle (low current) — again, low power. Somewhere in between is a **"sweet spot"** where the jet has both good pressure AND good flow rate — that is your **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**.

For a solar module, sunlight is the "water source on the hill." On a bright sunny day, you have plenty of water (high current). As the day gets cloudy, water reduces (current drops). If you place a shadow on **part of the panel** (Partial Shading), some cells stop producing current entirely, and they act like blocked pipes — they actually start consuming power as heat! To prevent this, manufacturers add **bypass diodes** that reroute the current around the shaded cells, much like a bypass road around a blocked highway.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics to Remember (Bold Constants):**
> - **Boltzmann Constant:** $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$
> - **Electron Charge:** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$
> - **Thermal Voltage at STC (25°C = 298.15 K):** $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85\ \text{mV}$
> - **Solar Constant:** $G_{STC} = 1000\ \text{W/m}^2$

## 1.3 Significance in KTU Curriculum

For **PCEOL607 (Electrical Simulation Lab)**, this experiment is crucial because it teaches the student to:
1. Model a non-linear DC source using simulation tools.
2. Understand how environmental parameters map to electrical behavior.
3. Recognize the computational challenge of MPP tracking (MPPT) under non-ideal conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiners' Perspective:** KTU frequently tests the student's ability to draw the I-V and P-V curves, label the **five key points** ($I_{sc}$, $V_{oc}$, $I_{mp}$, $V_{mp}$, $P_{max}$), compute the **Fill Factor (FF)**, and explain the **step-like behavior** of the curve under partial shading.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle: The Photovoltaic Effect

When photons with energy $E_{ph} = h\nu$ greater than the semiconductor bandgap $E_g$ strike the p-n junction of a solar cell, they generate electron-hole pairs. The built-in electric field at the junction separates these charge carriers, driving electrons to the n-side and holes to the p-side, thereby producing a photocurrent $I_{ph}$ proportional to the incident irradiance $G$.

## 2.2 Equivalent Circuit Models

### 2.2.1 Single-Diode Model (Five-Parameter Model) — Most Used

The PV cell is modeled as a **current source** $I_{ph}$ in parallel with a **diode** $D$, a **shunt resistance** $R_{sh}$, and connected in series with a **series resistance** $R_s$.

The governing equation (Kirchhoff's Current Law at the output node) is:

$$I = I_{ph} - I_D - I_{sh}$$

Where each branch is governed by:

$$I_D = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V + IR_s}{nV_T}\right) - 1 \right]$$

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$

Combining these, the **canonical single-diode equation** is:

$$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V + IR_s}{nV_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$

### 2.2.2 Two-Diode Model (Advanced)

Used for higher accuracy at low irradiance, adding a recombination diode $D_2$ with saturation current $I_{02}$ and ideality factor $n_2 = 2$. Rarely tested at B.Tech level.

## 2.3 The Five Model Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Typical Range (60-cell module) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Photocurrent | $I_{ph}$ | Current generated by incident light | $3\ \text{A} - 9\ \text{A}$ |
| Diode Saturation Current | $I_0$ | Reverse leakage current of p-n junction | $10^{-9}\ \text{A} - 10^{-6}\ \text{A}$ |
| Ideality Factor | $n$ | Quality of junction (1 = ideal, 2 = recombination-dominated) | $1.0 - 1.6$ |
| Series Resistance | $R_s$ | Resistance of contacts and semiconductor bulk | $0.1\ \Omega - 1\ \Omega$ |
| Shunt Resistance | $R_{sh}$ | Resistance across p-n junction leakage paths | $100\ \Omega - 1000\ \Omega$ |

## 2.4 Boundary Conditions: Extracting $I_{ph}$ and $I_0$

### At Open Circuit ($V = V_{oc}$, $I = 0$):

$$0 = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{nV_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V_{oc}}{R_{sh}}$$

Assuming $R_{sh}$ is very large (last term $\approx 0$):

$$I_{ph} \approx I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{nV_T}\right) - 1 \right] + I_0 \approx I_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{nV_T}\right)$$

### At Short Circuit ($V = 0$, $I = I_{sc}$):

$$I_{sc} = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{I_{sc}R_s}{nV_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{I_{sc}R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

Since $I_{sc}R_s$ is small and $R_{sh}$ is large:

$$I_{ph} \approx I_{sc}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a key KTU fact:** $I_{ph}$ is directly proportional to irradiance $G$. Thus $I_{ph} = I_{ph,STC} \cdot (G / G_{STC})$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ e^{(V+IR_s)/nV_T} - 1 \right] - \frac{V+IR_s}{R_{sh}}$ | Single-diode model implicit equation | A, V, Ω |
| 2 | $I_{ph}(G) = I_{ph,STC} \cdot \frac{G}{G_{STC}}$ | Photocurrent scales linearly with irradiance | A |
| 3 | $I_{ph}(T) = I_{ph,STC} \left[ 1 + K_i (T - T_{STC}) \right]$ | Temperature dependence of $I_{ph}$ (very weak) | A |
| 4 | $I_0(T) = I_{0,STC} \left[ \frac{T}{T_{STC}} \right]^3 \exp\!\left[ \frac{qE_g}{nk}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_{STC}} - \frac{1}{T}\right) \right]$ | Saturation current (highly temperature dependent) | A |
| 5 | $V_{oc}(T) = V_{oc,STC} + K_v (T - T_{STC})$ | $V_{oc}$ decreases with temperature (typically $-0.3\ \%/\degree\text{C}$) | V |
| 6 | $P = V \cdot I$ | Instantaneous electrical power output | W |
| 7 | $P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$ | Maximum extractable power | W |
| 8 | $FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | Fill Factor (always $< 1$, typically $0.7 - 0.85$) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{G \cdot A_{module}}$ | Conversion efficiency of the module | dimensionless |
| 10 | $V_T = \frac{kT}{q}$ | Thermal voltage (at 25°C, $V_T = 25.85\ \text{mV}$) | V |

## 2.6 Partial Shading: The Bypass Diode Mechanism

A PV module is composed of multiple cells connected in series. Under **Partial Shading Condition (PSC)**, the shaded cells produce less photocurrent than the illuminated cells. Since the same current must flow through all series-connected cells:

- The illuminated cells become **forward biased** and drive current.
- The shaded cells become **reverse biased**.
- If reverse bias exceeds a critical value, the shaded cell undergoes **avalanche breakdown**, dissipating power as heat (hot spot effect).

To prevent this, **bypass diodes** (typically Schottky diodes) are connected in **anti-parallel** across groups of cells (called "sub-strings"). Under uniform illumination, the bypass diode is reverse biased (inactive). Under partial shading, the bypass diode becomes forward biased and **short-circuits** the shaded sub-string, allowing current to bypass it.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often think bypass diodes "improve" power output. They don't — they merely **protect** shaded cells from damage. The total power output is *reduced* under partial shading.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solar Farm Design:** Engineers simulate PSC to determine string configurations and bypass diode placement to minimize mismatch losses.
- **MPPT Controller Design:** Algorithms like **Perturb & Observe (P&O)** and **Incremental Conductance (IncCond)** must distinguish between local and global MPPs under PSC.
- **Microinverter Architecture:** Each panel gets its own MPPT to mitigate PSC losses.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Single-Diode Model Equation

### Step 1: Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the Output Node

At the top node of the equivalent circuit, the photocurrent $I_{ph}$ from the current source splits into three paths: through the diode $I_D$, through the shunt resistor $I_{sh}$, and out to the external load $I$.

**Logic:** Charge conservation — current entering a node must equal current leaving it.

$$I_{ph} = I_D + I_{sh} + I$$

Rearranging:

$$I = I_{ph} - I_D - I_{sh}$$

### Step 2: Diode Branch — Apply the Shockley Diode Equation

The diode is in series with $R_s$, so the total voltage across the diode is $(V + IR_s)$, not just $V$. The Shockley equation gives:

$$I_D = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V + IR_s}{nV_T}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Logic:** The "$-1$" term accounts for the small reverse saturation current that flows even when the diode is reverse biased.

### Step 3: Shunt Resistor Branch — Apply Ohm's Law

The voltage across $R_{sh}$ is also $(V + IR_s)$ (it is in parallel with the series combination of diode and $R_s$).

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$

### Step 4: Substitute Back into KCL

$$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V + IR_s}{nV_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$

**Logic:** This is the master implicit equation. "Implicit" because $I$ appears on **both sides** — inside the exponential and outside — requiring iterative numerical solvers (Newton-Raphson) in simulation tools.

## 3.2 Derivation of Fill Factor and Maximum Power

### Step 5: Define Instantaneous Power

$$P(V) = V \cdot I(V) = V \cdot \left\{ I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V + IR_s}{nV_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}} \right\}$$

### Step 6: Apply the Maximum Power Condition

At the MPP, $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$:

$$\frac{dP}{dV} = I + V \frac{dI}{dV} = 0$$

**Logic:** At the peak of the P-V curve, the slope is zero, so the change in power with respect to voltage vanishes.

$$\frac{dI}{dV}\bigg|_{V=V_{mp}} = -\frac{I_{mp}}{V_{mp}}$$

### Step 7: Differentiate the Single-Diode Equation

Differentiating the single-diode equation with respect to $V$:

$$\frac{dI}{dV} = -\frac{I_0}{nV_T} \exp\!\left(\frac{V + IR_s}{nV_T}\right) \cdot \left(1 + R_s \frac{dI}{dV}\right) - \frac{1}{R_{sh}} - \frac{R_s}{R_{sh}} \frac{dI}{dV}$$

### Step 8: Define Dynamic Resistance and Solve

Let $R_d = \dfrac{nV_T}{I_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{V + IR_s}{nV_T}\right)}$ be the diode's dynamic resistance. Then:

$$\frac{dI}{dV} = -\frac{1}{R_d + R_s} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \frac{R_s}{R_{sh}}} - \frac{1}{R_{sh}} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \frac{R_s}{R_{sh}}}$$

For an **ideal cell** ($R_s \to 0$, $R_{sh} \to \infty$):

$$\frac{dI}{dV}\bigg|_{V_{mp}} = -\frac{1}{R_d}$$

Combining with Step 6:

$$-\frac{1}{R_d} = -\frac{I_{mp}}{V_{mp}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad I_{mp} \cdot R_d = V_{mp}$$

This is the **impedance matching condition** at the MPP — the load resistance must equal the diode's dynamic resistance.

## 3.3 Derivation of Open-Circuit Voltage from the Implicit Equation

At open circuit, $I = 0$ and $V = V_{oc}$:

$$0 = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{nV_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V_{oc}}{R_{sh}}$$

For a high-quality cell, $R_{sh}$ is very large, so $\dfrac{V_{oc}}{R_{sh}} \to 0$. Also, $I_{ph} \approx I_{sc}$:

$$I_{sc} = I_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{nV_T}\right)$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\frac{V_{oc}}{nV_T} = \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{sc}}{I_0}\right)$$

$$\boxed{V_{oc} = nV_T \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{sc}}{I_0}\right)}$$

**Logic:** This shows $V_{oc}$ depends *logarithmically* on $I_{sc}$ (and thus irradiance), while $I_{sc}$ depends *linearly* on irradiance. This is why partial shading reduces voltage more dramatically than current at low irradiance.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Behavior Under Different Conditions

| Operating Condition | Effect on $I_{sc}$ | Effect on $V_{oc}$ | Effect on $P_{max}$ | Shape of P-V Curve |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Increase irradiance ($G \uparrow$) | Increases linearly | Increases logarithmically | Increases significantly | Single peak shifts right & up |
| Increase temperature ($T \uparrow$) | Slight increase (tiny) | **Decreases** (large negative) | **Decreases** | Single peak shifts left & down |
| Uniform shading (low $G$) | Decreases linearly | Decreases logarithmically | Decreases | Single peak shifts left & down |
| Partial shading | Decreases (non-uniform) | Decreases (step-wise) | Decreases sharply | **Multiple peaks (local + global)** |
| Heavy shading on one cell | Collapses for that substring | Step drop in curve | Severe reduction | Step-like I-V, stair-case P-V |

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Given:** A PV module under STC has $V_{oc} = 44.2\ \text{V}$, $I_{sc} = 5.4\ \text{A}$, $V_{mp} = 35.2\ \text{V}$, $I_{mp} = 4.95\ \text{A}$. Module area $A = 1.7\ \text{m}^2$.

### (a) Calculate Fill Factor (FF):

**Step 1:** Compute $P_{max}$:

$$P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp} = 35.2\ \text{V} \times 4.95\ \text{A}$$

$$P_{max} = 174.24\ \text{W}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the ideal maximum product $V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$:

$$V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} = 44.2\ \text{V} \times 5.4\ \text{A} = 238.68\ \text{W}$$

**Step 3:** Compute FF:

$$FF = \frac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{174.24}{238.68}$$

$$\boxed{FF = 0.7301 = 73.01\%}$$

### (b) Calculate Module Efficiency ($\eta$):

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{G \cdot A} = \frac{174.24\ \text{W}}{1000\ \text{W/m}^2 \times 1.7\ \text{m}^2}$$

$$\eta = \frac{174.24}{1700} = 0.1025$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 10.25\%}$$

### (c) Predict $P_{max}$ when Irradiance drops to $G = 400\ \text{W/m}^2$ (Uniform):

Since $I_{ph} \propto G$, the new short-circuit current is:

$$I_{sc}' = I_{sc} \cdot \frac{G'}{G_{STC}} = 5.4 \times \frac{400}{1000} = 2.16\ \text{A}$$

The new open-circuit voltage (logarithmic dependence):

$$V_{oc}' = V_{oc} + nV_T \ln\!\left(\frac{G'}{G_{STC}}\right)$$

$$V_{oc}' = 44.2 + 1.3 \times 0.02585 \times \ln(0.4) = 44.2 + 0.0336 \times (-0.9163)$$

$$V_{oc}' = 44.2 - 0.0308 = 44.17\ \text{V}$$

Assuming $FF$ remains approximately constant (a fair assumption for moderate irradiance change):

$$P_{max}' = FF \cdot V_{oc}' \cdot I_{sc}' = 0.7301 \times 44.17 \times 2.16$$

$$P_{max}' = 69.64\ \text{W}$$

**Logic Note:** Observe how $P_{max}$ drops by ~60% when irradiance drops by 60% — confirming the *approximately linear* relationship between irradiance and power output under uniform shading.

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a model answer format** for KTU 14-mark problems. Notice the explicit "Logic" annotations and the boxed final answers — examiners award step-marks for showing all transitions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Single-Diode Equivalent Circuit (Block-Level Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional architecture of the single-diode model — showing the four parallel paths for current flow:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incident Photon Flux G] --> B[Photocurrent Source Iph]
    B --> C{Output Node}
    C --> D[Diode Branch ID]
    C --> E[Shunt Resistor Rsh]
    C --> F[Series Resistor Rs]
    F --> G[External Load Terminal]
    D --> H[Diode Reverse Saturation I0]
    E --> I[Leakage Path]
    G --> J[Output Current I and Voltage V]
    K[Temperature T in Kelvin] --> D
    K --> H
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- Node `A` represents the incident irradiance input.
- Node `B` is the controlled current source $I_{ph}$, proportional to $G$.
- Node `C` is the KCL summation point.
- Node `D` is the non-linear diode branch governed by Shockley equation.
- Node `E` is the linear shunt leakage path.
- Node `F` is the series resistance representing contact and bulk losses.
- Node `G` is the external load terminal.
- Node `H` shows the diode's intrinsic saturation current.
- Node `K` injects temperature dependence into both the diode and saturation current.

## 4.2 I-V and P-V Curve Characteristics Flow

This diagram shows the relationship between I-V curve plotting, P-V curve derivation, and MPP identification:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize PV Module Parameters: Iph, I0, n, Rs, Rsh, T] --> B[Set Sweep Range: V from 0 to Voc]
    B --> C[For each V, solve implicit I equation numerically]
    C --> D[Newton-Raphson Iterative Solver]
    D --> E[Compute Current I at each V]
    E --> F[Compute Power P = V times I]
    F --> G{Plot I-V Curve}
    F --> H{Plot P-V Curve}
    G --> I[Mark Isc on V=0 axis]
    G --> J[Mark Voc on I=0 axis]
    H --> K[Identify Peak of P-V Curve]
    K --> L[Read Vmp and Imp at Peak]
    L --> M[Compute Pmax = Vmp times Imp]
    M --> N[Compute Fill Factor FF]
    N --> O[Output: Complete PV Module Characteristics]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** The flowchart shows the complete simulation workflow — from parameter initialization, through the numerical solution of the implicit single-diode equation, to the final extraction of $P_{max}$, $V_{mp}$, $I_{mp}$, and $FF$.

## 4.3 Partial Shading Substring Architecture with Bypass Diodes

This diagram illustrates how a 60-cell module is divided into 3 sub-strings of 20 cells each, with bypass diodes:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sub-string 1: 20 Illuminated Cells] -->|Series| B[Sub-string 2: 15 Illuminated + 5 Shaded Cells]
    B -->|Series| C[Sub-string 3: 20 Illuminated Cells]
    D[Bypass Diode D1 across Sub-string 1] -.->|Anti-parallel| A
    E[Bypass Diode D2 across Sub-string 2] -.->|Anti-parallel| B
    F[Bypass Diode D3 across Sub-string 3] -.->|Anti-parallel| C
    C --> G[Module Output Terminal Positive]
    A --> H[Module Output Terminal Negative]
    B --> I[Shaded Cells: Reverse Biased, Act as Load]
    I --> J[Bypass D2 becomes Forward Biased]
    J --> K[Current Bypasses Shaded Substring]
    K --> L[Module Operates at Reduced Voltage Step]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** When cells in `B` are shaded, the bypass diode `E` becomes forward biased and reroutes current around the shaded sub-string. This creates a **step** in the I-V curve and **multiple peaks** in the P-V curve.

## 4.4 MPPT Algorithm Flow (Perturb & Observe) Under Partial Shading

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Measure V and I of PV Module] --> B[Compute P = V times I]
    B --> C[Perturb Voltage: Vnew = Vold plus Delta V]
    C --> D[Measure New Power Pnew]
    D --> E{Delta P greater than 0?}
    E -->|Yes| F{Same direction as previous perturbation?}
    E -->|No| G{Delta P less than 0?}
    F -->|Yes| H[Continue Perturbation in Same Direction]
    F -->|No| I[Reverse Perturbation Direction]
    G -->|Yes| I
    G -->|No| J[Hold Voltage at Current Point]
    H --> K[Update V and I]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{Is this the Global MPP?}
    L -->|No - Local Peak| M[Apply PSC Detection Logic]
    M --> N[Re-initialize search across full voltage range]
    N --> C
    L -->|Yes - Global MPP| O[Hold at Global MPP, Output Maximum Power]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** This is the classic **Perturb & Observe (P&O)** algorithm flowchart, augmented with the partial-shading detection block (`M`) that handles multiple local maxima. The IncCond method is similar but uses $\frac{dI}{dV} = -\frac{I}{V}$ as the MPP condition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Tip:** When simulating, students often confuse the MPPT algorithm with the MPP itself. **MPPT (Maximum Power Point Tracking)** is the *control algorithm*, while **MPP (Maximum Power Point)** is the *physical operating point* on the curve.

## 4.5 Simulation Block Diagram (MATLAB/Simulink Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Irradiance Input G W per m2] --> B[PV Module Subsystem]
    C[Temperature Input T in Celsius] --> B
    B --> D[DC-DC Converter Subsystem]
    E[MPPT Controller Subsystem] -->|Gate Pulse Signal| D
    D --> F[Load: Resistive or Battery]
    F --> G[Voltage Sensor]
    F --> H[Current Sensor]
    G --> E
    H --> E
    B --> I[Scope: Display I-V Curve]
    B --> J[Scope: Display P-V Curve]
    B --> K[Display: Numerical Vmp, Imp, Pmax]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** In MATLAB/Simulink, the PV module is a pre-built block from the **Simscape Electrical / SimPowerSystems** library. The MPPT controller outputs a duty cycle to the DC-DC converter (typically a boost converter), which adjusts the load impedance seen by the PV module to operate it at the MPP.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Fill Factor" of a solar PV cell. Mention its typical value range for a commercial silicon cell.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Fill Factor (FF)** of a solar PV cell is defined as the ratio of the actual maximum extractable power ($P_{max}$) to the product of the open-circuit voltage ($V_{oc}$) and short-circuit current ($I_{sc}$):

$$FF = \frac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** FF is a measure of the "squareness" of the I-V curve. A perfectly rectangular I-V curve would have $FF = 1$, but this is unattainable due to series and shunt resistance losses.

**Typical Range:** For a commercial monocrystalline silicon cell, $FF$ typically lies between **0.70 and 0.85** (or 70% to 85%). Lower values indicate higher resistive losses in the cell.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is meant by partial shading of a solar PV module? Why are bypass diodes used in PV modules?**

**Model Answer:**

**Partial Shading Condition (PSC):** A PV module is said to be under partial shading when incident solar irradiance is not uniform across its surface — for example, when a tree branch, bird droppings, building shadow, or cloud covers only a portion of the module.

**Consequence:** Since cells in a module are connected in series, the same current must flow through all cells. Shaded cells produce less photocurrent and become **reverse biased**, acting as loads. They dissipate power as heat, creating **hot spots** that can permanently damage the cells.

**Role of Bypass Diodes:** Bypass diodes are connected in **anti-parallel** across groups of cells (sub-strings). Under normal (uniform) conditions, they remain reverse biased and inactive. Under partial shading, when the sub-string voltage reverses, the bypass diode becomes forward biased and **short-circuits** the shaded sub-string, safely rerouting the current and preventing hot-spot damage.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) Derive the single-diode equivalent circuit model equation for a solar PV cell. Clearly define each parameter used.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Draw the single-diode equivalent circuit consisting of:
- A photocurrent source $I_{ph}$ (proportional to irradiance $G$)
- A diode $D$ in parallel (representing p-n junction behavior)
- A shunt resistance $R_{sh}$ in parallel (representing leakage paths)
- A series resistance $R_s$ in series (representing contact and bulk resistance)

**Step 2:** Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the top output node:

$$I_{ph} = I_D + I_{sh} + I$$

**Step 3:** Apply Shockley's diode equation to the diode branch. The total voltage across the diode is $(V + IR_s)$:

$$I_D = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V + IR_s}{nV_T}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 4:** Apply Ohm's law to the shunt resistor branch:

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back:

$$\boxed{I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V + IR_s}{nV_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}}$$

**Parameter Definitions:**
- $I$ — Terminal output current of the cell (A)
- $V$ — Terminal output voltage of the cell (V)
- $I_{ph}$ — Photogenerated current, proportional to irradiance (A)
- $I_0$ — Diode reverse saturation current (A)
- $n$ — Ideality factor of the diode (1 to 2)
- $R_s$ — Series resistance of the cell (Ω)
- $R_{sh}$ — Shunt (parallel) resistance of the cell (Ω)
- $V_T = kT/q$ — Thermal voltage (≈ 25.85 mV at 25°C)

---

**(b) With neat sketches, explain the I-V and P-V characteristics of a solar PV module under (i) uniform irradiance and (ii) partial shading conditions. Compare the two cases.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Uniform Irradiance Characteristics:**

Under uniform irradiance (e.g., $G = 1000\ \text{W/m}^2$ across the entire module), the I-V curve is a **smooth, single-knee** curve. The current remains nearly constant at $I_{sc}$ for low voltages, then drops sharply as voltage approaches $V_{oc}$. The corresponding P-V curve is **unimodal** (single peak), with a clear global maximum at $(V_{mp}, I_{mp})$ yielding $P_{max}$.

**(ii) Partial Shading Characteristics:**

Under partial shading (e.g., one of three sub-strings receives only $300\ \text{W/m}^2$ while the other two receive $1000\ \text{W/m}^2$), the I-V curve exhibits a **step-like** profile. Each step corresponds to a bypass diode activation. The P-V curve becomes **multimodal** — typically showing 2 or 3 local peaks, with the global maximum occurring at a **lower voltage** than the uniform case.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Uniform Irradiance | Partial Shading |
|---|---|---|
| I-V curve shape | Smooth, single knee | Step-like (staircase) |
| P-V curve peaks | **One** global maximum | **Multiple** local maxima |
| Number of bypass diodes active | 0 | 1 or more |
| Global MPP location | Higher voltage | Lower voltage |
| $P_{max}$ value | Highest | Significantly reduced |
| MPPT algorithm complexity | Simple P&O sufficient | Requires global search |

**Conclusion:** Partial shading drastically reduces the extractable power and complicates MPPT controller design. Bypass diodes protect the module but do not improve output.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) A solar PV module has the following specifications under STC: $V_{oc} = 44.2$ V, $I_{sc} = 5.4$ A, $V_{mp} = 35.2$ V, $I_{mp} = 4.95$ A. Module area = $1.7\ \text{m}^2$. Calculate:** **[7 Marks]**
- **(i) Maximum power output**
- **(ii) Fill Factor**
- **(iii) Module efficiency**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Maximum Power Output $P_{max}$:**

$$P_{max} = V_{mp} \times I_{mp} = 35.2\ \text{V} \times 4.95\ \text{A}$$

$$P_{max} = 174.24\ \text{W}$$

**(ii) Fill Factor (FF):**

$$V_{oc} \times I_{sc} = 44.2\ \text{V} \times 5.4\ \text{A} = 238.68\ \text{W}$$

$$FF = \frac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \times I_{sc}} = \frac{174.24}{238.68} = 0.7301$$

$$FF = 73.01\%$$

**(iii) Module Efficiency ($\eta$):**

Incident solar power on module = $G \times A = 1000\ \text{W/m}^2 \times 1.7\ \text{m}^2 = 1700\ \text{W}$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{G \times A} = \frac{174.24}{1700} = 0.1025$$

$$\eta = 10.25\%$$

---

**(b) Explain the effect of temperature and irradiance on the I-V and P-V characteristics of a solar PV module with appropriate diagrams.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Effect of Irradiance ($G$):**

The photocurrent $I_{ph}$ is **directly proportional** to irradiance $G$:

$$I_{ph}(G) = I_{ph,STC} \times \frac{G}{G_{STC}}$$

Therefore, $I_{sc} \propto G$ (linear scaling). However, $V_{oc}$ depends on $\ln(G)$, so it changes only slightly with $G$. The result: as $G$ decreases, the I-V curve compresses **downward** (current drops) with a small **leftward shift** (voltage drops slightly). The P-V peak moves to **lower voltage and lower power**.

**Effect of Temperature ($T$):**

- $I_{sc}$ has a **weak positive** temperature coefficient (slight increase with $T$).
- $I_0$ has a **strong positive** temperature coefficient (increases exponentially).
- $V_{oc}$ has a **negative** temperature coefficient (typically $-0.3\ \%/\degree\text{C}$ to $-0.4\ \%/\degree\text{C}$ for silicon).

Mathematically:

$$V_{oc}(T) = V_{oc,STC} + K_v (T - T_{STC})$$

where $K_v \approx -0.12\ \text{V/°C}$ per cell.

The result: as $T$ increases, the I-V curve shifts **leftward** (voltage drops significantly) with a very slight **upward** shift (current rises marginally). The P-V peak moves to **lower voltage and lower power**.

**Key Insight:** PV modules perform *better* in cold, sunny conditions and *worse* in hot, sunny conditions. This is counterintuitive because people often associate heat with more solar energy, but heat reduces the bandgap-driven voltage output.

**Combined Effect:** On a cold bright day (low $T$, high $G$), the module produces maximum power. On a hot cloudy day (high $T$, low $G$), power output is severely reduced.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $I_{ph}$ with $I_{sc}$:** They are approximately equal but not identical. $I_{ph}$ is the *generated* current, while $I_{sc}$ is the *terminal* current. The difference is the small voltage drop across $R_s$.
>
> 2. **Wrong units for $V_T$:** Many students write $V_T = 25.85\ \text{V}$ instead of $25.85\ \text{mV}$. This is a fatal arithmetic error.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the "$-1$" in the Shockley equation:** Omitting it loses marks. The "$-1$" represents the reverse saturation current and is essential.
>
> 4. **Drawing the partial-shading P-V curve as a single peak:** This is a critical conceptual error. Partial shading **always** produces multiple peaks due to bypass diode activation.
>
> 5. **Stating that bypass diodes improve power output:** They **protect** the module from damage; they do **not** increase power. Power output is always reduced under partial shading.
>
> 6. **Not labeling axes properly in graphs:** Always label: I-V curve — X-axis: Voltage (V), Y-axis: Current (A); P-V curve — X-axis: Voltage (V), Y-axis: Power (W). Mark $I_{sc}$, $V_{oc}$, $V_{mp}$, $I_{mp}$, $P_{max}$ clearly.
>
> 7. **Mixing up Fill Factor formula:** Some students write $FF = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} / P_{max}$ (inverted). The correct form is $P_{max} / (V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc})$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Single-diode equation** is the canonical model: $I = I_{ph} - I_0 [e^{(V+IR_s)/nV_T} - 1] - (V+IR_s)/R_{sh}$
- [ ] **Five parameters:** $I_{ph}$, $I_0$, $n$, $R_s$, $R_{sh}$
- [ ] **STC conditions:** $G = 1000\ \text{W/m}^2$, $T = 25\degree\text{C}$, AM 1.5
- [ ] **Thermal voltage at 25°C:** $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85\ \text{mV}$
- [ ] **Open-circuit voltage formula:** $V_{oc} = nV_T \ln(I_{sc}/I_0)$
- [ ] **Short-circuit current:** $I_{sc} \approx I_{ph}$ (approximation valid for high $R_{sh}$)
- [ ] **Fill Factor:** $FF = P_{max}/(V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc})$, typically $0.7 - 0.85$
- [ ] **Efficiency:** $\eta = P_{max}/(G \cdot A)$
- [ ] **$I_{ph} \propto G$ (linear), $V_{oc} \propto \ln(G)$ (logarithmic)**
- [ ] **$V_{oc}$ decreases with $T$ (negative coefficient), $I_{sc}$ increases slightly with $T$**
- [ ] **Bypass diodes are connected in anti-parallel across sub-strings**
- [ ] **Partial shading → step-like I-V curve, multimodal P-V curve**
- [ ] **MPPT algorithms:** Perturb & Observe (P&O), Incremental Conductance (IncCond)
- [ ] **Power equation:** $P = V \cdot I$, $P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$
- [ ] **Constant values to memorize:** $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$, $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$
- [ ] **MPP condition:** $dP/dV = 0$ → $I + V(dI/dV) = 0$ → $dI/dV = -I/V$ at MPP
- [ ] **In simulation:** Use Newton-Raphson solver for implicit equation, sweep $V$ from 0 to $V_{oc}$
- [ ] **Series resistance $R_s$** reduces $FF$ by causing voltage drop; **shunt resistance $R_{sh}$** reduces $FF$ by causing current leakage
- [ ] **Hot-spot formation** occurs when shaded cells become reverse biased and dissipate power as heat — bypass diodes prevent this
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Flow Analysis - Gauss-Seidel Method, Newton-Raphson Method, and Fast Decoupled Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL607 - ELECTRICAL SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Load Flow Analysis - Gauss-Seidel Method, Newton-Raphson Method, and Fast Decoupled Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Load Flow Analysis (LFA)**, also called **Power Flow Study**, is the steady-state numerical evaluation of the voltages, phase angles, real powers, and reactive powers at every bus in an interconnected electric power system under balanced three-phase sinusoidal operating conditions. It solves the nonlinear algebraic network equations derived from Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) applied to the nodal admittance model of the power network.

Mathematically, the load flow problem reduces to solving a set of nonlinear algebraic equations expressed in terms of the **Y-bus (Bus Admittance Matrix)**:

$$I_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij} \cdot V_j$$

where $V_j = \vert V_j \vert \angle \delta_j$ is the complex voltage at bus $j$, and $Y_{ij}$ represents the admittance between buses $i$ and $j$.

The complex power injection at bus $i$ is given by:

$$S_i = P_i + jQ_i = V_i \cdot I_i^* = V_i \cdot \left( \sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij} \cdot V_j \right)^*$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Load Flow Analysis is the cornerstone of every power system study. The three numerical methods mandated in your lab syllabus — **Gauss-Seidel (GS)**, **Newton-Raphson (NR)**, and **Fast Decoupled Load Flow (FDLF)** — represent the historical evolution of computational efficiency in solving the same fundamental nonlinear system of equations.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Buses in Load Flow

A systematic understanding of bus classification is **mandatory** before any of the three methods can be applied:

| Bus Type | Symbol | Known Quantities | Unknown Quantities | Remarks |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Slack (Swing/Reference)** | Bus 1 | $\vert V_1 \vert, \delta_1$ | $P_1, Q_1$ | Reference for all angles; balances system losses |
| **Load (PQ Bus)** | Bus 2, 3... | $P_i, Q_i$ | $\vert V_i \vert, \delta_i$ | The most numerous in any practical system |
| **Voltage Controlled (PV/Generator)** | Bus n | $P_i, \vert V_i \vert$ | $Q_i, \delta_i$ | Generator buses with AVR-controlled terminal voltage |
| **Voltage Controlled with $Q$ limits** | PV $\rightarrow$ PQ | $P_i, Q_{i,\min/\max}$ | $\vert V_i \vert$ | PV bus becomes PQ if reactive limit is violated |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Water-Tank Analogy:**
> Imagine a network of interconnected water tanks representing the buses, and pipes between them representing the transmission lines. Each tank is at some unknown height (voltage magnitude) and the water flows at unknown rates (real power). Now imagine someone opens and closes valves at certain tanks (generators controlling voltage) and draws water at certain tanks (loads drawing power). The **Load Flow problem** is simply the question: *What is the final water level in every tank and the flow rate in every pipe, once everything has settled into a steady state?*
>
> - The **Gauss-Seidel** method is like adjusting each tank's water level one at a time, then immediately using that updated level to adjust the next. It is intuitive but slow to converge.
> - The **Newton-Raphson** method is like predicting, with calculus, exactly how much each tank must change simultaneously to reach equilibrium in very few steps.
> - The **Fast Decoupled** method is a brilliant shortcut: it observes that water-level adjustments barely affect the flow rates in distant pipes (a kind of "physical decoupling"), allowing near-instantaneous convergence.

---

## 1.4 Why These Three Methods?

All three methods solve the **same underlying power flow equations**, but differ in their mathematical approach, computational effort per iteration, and convergence properties.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Industry Metric:** In modern power system operation centres, the **Fast Decoupled Newton-Raphson (FDNR)** method is the de-facto industry standard due to its robust convergence, small memory footprint, and lightning-fast execution — even for systems with **tens of thousands of buses**.

---

## 1.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Operational Planning**: Real-time dispatch of generation in a power grid.
- **Contingency Analysis**: Simulating line or generator outages.
- **Economic Dispatch**: Minimizing fuel cost subject to network constraints.
- **Voltage Stability Assessment**: Identifying buses prone to voltage collapse.
- **State Estimation**: Foundation for Energy Management Systems (EMS) in control rooms.
- **Renewable Integration Studies**: Evaluating impact of solar/wind injection.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Power Flow Equation — The Foundation

At any bus $i$ of an $n$-bus system, the complex power injection in polar form is:

$$P_i - jQ_i = V_i^* \sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij} \cdot V_j$$

Separating real and imaginary parts (with $Y_{ij} = G_{ij} + jB_{ij}$ and $V_i = \vert V_i \vert \angle \delta_i$):

$$P_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \left[ G_{ij} \cos(\delta_i - \delta_j) + B_{ij} \sin(\delta_i - \delta_j) \right]$$

$$Q_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \left[ G_{ij} \sin(\delta_i - \delta_j) - B_{ij} \cos(\delta_i - \delta_j) \right]$$

These two equations form the **core nonlinear system** that every load flow method must solve.

---

## 2.2 Method 1: Gauss-Seidel (GS) Iterative Method

### 2.2.1 The Algorithm

The Gauss-Seidel method treats the power flow equations as a fixed-point iteration. For a **PQ bus**, the iterative formula for the voltage phasor at bus $i$ in the $(k+1)$-th iteration is:

$$V_i^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{Y_{ii}} \left[ \frac{P_i - jQ_i}{(V_i^{(k)})^*} - \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} Y_{ij} V_j^{(k+1)} - \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} Y_{ij} V_j^{(k)} \right]$$

Notice that **already-computed values within the same iteration** $(V_j^{(k+1)})$ are used as soon as they are available — this is the defining feature of Gauss-Seidel (as opposed to Gauss-Jacobi).

### 2.2.2 Decoupled Real-Imaginary Form

Separating $V_i = e_i + jf_i$ gives two real equations, but in practice we work with polar form $\vert V_i \vert \angle \delta_i$:

$$V_i^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{Y_{ii}} \left[ \frac{P_i - jQ_i}{(V_i^{(k)})^*} - \sum_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} Y_{ij} V_j^{(k)} \right]$$

### 2.2.3 Acceleration Factor

To accelerate convergence, an acceleration factor $\alpha$ is applied only to the voltage magnitude for PQ buses (rarely to the angle):

$$(V_i^{(k+1)})_{\text{accelerated}} = V_i^{(k)} + \alpha \left( V_i^{(k+1)} - V_i^{(k)} \right)$$

Typical values: $\alpha = 1.2$ to $1.8$. The angle itself is left unaccelerated in most practical implementations.

### 2.2.4 PV Bus Handling in GS

For a PV bus, the reactive power $Q_i^{(k+1)}$ is first computed using the scheduled $\vert V_i \vert$ and the angle from the previous iteration, then used in the standard iteration formula. If $Q_i$ violates its limits, the bus is converted to a PQ bus.

### 2.2.5 Convergence Criterion

The iteration terminates when the maximum magnitude correction falls below a tolerance $\epsilon$ (typically $\epsilon = 10^{-5}$ pu or $10^{-4}$ pu):

$$\max_i \left \vert V_i^{(k+1)} - V_i^{(k)} \right \vert < \epsilon$$

---

## 2.3 Method 2: Newton-Raphson (NR) Method

### 2.3.1 The Algorithm

The NR method linearizes the nonlinear power flow equations using a first-order Taylor series expansion. The mismatch vector is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \Delta P \\ \Delta Q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} J_{11} & J_{12} \\ J_{21} & J_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta \delta \\ \Delta \vert V \vert / \vert V \vert \end{bmatrix}$$

The state vector is updated as:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \delta^{(k+1)} \\ \vert V \vert^{(k+1)} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \delta^{(k)} \\ \vert V \vert^{(k)} \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} \Delta \delta \\ \Delta \vert V \vert \end{bmatrix}$$

### 2.3.2 The Jacobian Matrix Elements

The Jacobian is a square, sparse, symmetric matrix of partial derivatives. Its sub-blocks are:

$$J_1 = \frac{\partial P}{\partial \delta}, \quad J_2 = \frac{\partial P}{\partial \vert V \vert}, \quad J_3 = \frac{\partial Q}{\partial \delta}, \quad J_4 = \frac{\partial Q}{\partial \vert V \vert}$$

For $i \neq j$:

$$J_1(i,j) = \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \left[ G_{ij} \sin(\delta_i - \delta_j) - B_{ij} \cos(\delta_i - \delta_j) \right]$$

$$J_2(i,j) = \vert V_i \vert \left[ G_{ij} \cos(\delta_i - \delta_j) + B_{ij} \sin(\delta_i - \delta_j) \right]$$

$$J_3(i,j) = - \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \left[ G_{ij} \cos(\delta_i - \delta_j) + B_{ij} \sin(\delta_i - \delta_j) \right]$$

$$J_4(i,j) = \vert V_i \vert \left[ G_{ij} \sin(\delta_i - \delta_j) - B_{ij} \cos(\delta_i - \delta_j) \right]$$

For $i = j$ (diagonal elements):

$$J_1(i,i) = -Q_i - B_{ii} \vert V_i \vert^2$$

$$J_2(i,i) = \frac{P_i}{\vert V_i \vert} + G_{ii} \vert V_i \vert$$

$$J_3(i,i) = P_i - G_{ii} \vert V_i \vert^2$$

$$J_4(i,i) = \frac{Q_i}{\vert V_i \vert} - B_{ii} \vert V_i \vert$$

### 2.3.3 Convergence Properties of NR

- **Quadratic convergence**: The error squared reduces at each iteration once near the solution.
- **Robust to initial guess**: Generally converges from any practical initial guess.
- **Computationally intensive per iteration**: Requires $O(n^2)$ memory and $O(n^3)$ operations for LU factorization.
- **Iteration count**: Typically 3 to 5 iterations for highly accurate solutions.

---

## 2.4 Method 3: Fast Decoupled Load Flow (FDLF)

### 2.4.1 Underlying Approximations

The Fast Decoupled method applies two brilliant physical approximations to the NR Jacobian:

1. **$\cos(\delta_i - \delta_j) \approx 1$ and $\sin(\delta_i - \delta_j) \approx (\delta_i - \delta_j)$** for small angle differences (typically $< 10^\circ$).
2. **$G_{ij} \sin(\delta_i - \delta_j) \ll B_{ij}$** — the conductance term is much smaller than the susceptance in transmission lines.

These simplifications result in the **constant, symmetric decoupled Jacobian**:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \Delta P \\ \Delta Q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} H & 0 \\ 0 & L \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta \delta / \vert V \vert \\ \Delta \vert V \vert / \vert V \vert \end{bmatrix}$$

with $H$ and $L$ being real, sparse, symmetric, and **constant** (computed only once at the start).

### 2.4.2 The Stott-Ofsen Formulation (Standard FDLF)

After approximation, the iteration equations become:

$$\Delta \delta = [B']^{-1} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{\vert V \vert}$$

$$\Delta \vert V \vert = [B'']^{-1} \cdot \frac{\Delta Q}{\vert V \vert}$$

where $B'$ and $B''$ are constant matrices derived from the imaginary part of the bus admittance matrix. The two systems are solved **alternately** until convergence.

### 2.4.3 Iteration Equations in Standard Form

$$\delta^{(k+1)} = \delta^{(k)} + [B']^{-1} \cdot \left( \frac{\Delta P^{(k)}}{\vert V^{(k)} \vert} \right)$$

$$\vert V \vert^{(k+1)} = \vert V \vert^{(k)} + [B'']^{-1} \cdot \left( \frac{\Delta Q^{(k)}}{\vert V^{(k)} \vert} \right)$$

The key advantages: **no Jacobian re-computation**, **no factorization at every iteration** (factorization done only once), and **highly parallelizable**.

### 2.4.4 Convergence Properties of FDLF

- **Linear convergence**: Slower than NR, but the per-iteration cost is dramatically lower.
- **Iteration count**: Typically 5 to 15 iterations.
- **Robust**: Excellent convergence for well-conditioned, normally loaded systems.
- **Limitations**: Convergence may degrade for highly loaded systems with large angle differences or for systems with many PV buses.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description / Application |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Y-bus Equation** | $Y_{ij} = G_{ij} + jB_{ij}$ | Bus admittance matrix element; used as the foundation in **all three methods** |
| **Real Power Injection** | $P_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \left[ G_{ij} \cos \delta_{ij} + B_{ij} \sin \delta_{ij} \right]$ | Active power at bus $i$ where $\delta_{ij} = \delta_i - \delta_j$ |
| **Reactive Power Injection** | $Q_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \left[ G_{ij} \sin \delta_{ij} - B_{ij} \cos \delta_{ij} \right]$ | Reactive power at bus $i$ |
| **GS Voltage Update** | $V_i^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{Y_{ii}} \left[ \frac{P_i - jQ_i}{(V_i^{(k)})^*} - \sum_{j \neq i} Y_{ij} V_j^{(\cdot)} \right]$ | Gauss-Seidel core iteration |
| **Acceleration** | $V_i^{\text{acc}} = V_i^{(k)} + \alpha (V_i^{(k+1)} - V_i^{(k)})$ | Standard acceleration factor $\alpha \in [1.2, 1.8]$ |
| **NR Jacobian** | $J = \begin{bmatrix} \partial P / \partial \delta & \partial P / \partial \vert V \vert \\ \partial Q / \partial \delta & \partial Q / \partial \vert V \vert \end{bmatrix}$ | Square, sparse, system-dependent |
| **NR Update** | $\begin{bmatrix} \Delta \delta \\ \Delta \vert V \vert \end{bmatrix} = [J]^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta P \\ \Delta Q \end{bmatrix}$ | Solved via LU factorization at every iteration |
| **FDLF $\Delta\delta$ Equation** | $\Delta \delta = [B']^{-1} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{\vert V \vert}$ | First decoupled sub-system |
| **FDLF $\Delta \vert V \vert$ Equation** | $\Delta \vert V \vert = [B'']^{-1} \cdot \frac{\Delta Q}{\vert V \vert}$ | Second decoupled sub-system |
| **Convergence Criterion** | $\max_i \vert \Delta P_i, \Delta Q_i \vert \leq \epsilon$ (typically $\epsilon = 10^{-5}$ pu) | Standard for all three methods |
| **Slack Bus Balance** | $P_1 = \sum_{i=2}^{n} P_i \text{ (losses included)}$ | Slack bus absorbs all system losses |

---

## 2.6 Comparative Analysis of the Three Methods

| Property | Gauss-Seidel | Newton-Raphson | Fast Decoupled |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Convergence Rate** | Linear (slow) | Quadratic (very fast) | Linear (moderately fast) |
| **Iterations Needed** | 20 to 100+ | 3 to 5 | 5 to 15 |
| **Memory Required** | Low | High | Low |
| **Per-Iteration Cost** | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^3)$ | $O(n)$ (with sparsity) |
| **Robustness to Initial Guess** | Poor | Excellent | Good |
| **Industry Preference (2024)** | Rare (educational) | High (reference method) | **Highest (industry standard)** |
| **Best Use Case** | Small systems, teaching | All sizes, accuracy-critical | Large real-time systems |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Modern power system operators run **Fast Decoupled** every few minutes for state estimation, **Newton-Raphson** for detailed offline studies, and **Gauss-Seidel** purely as a pedagogical foundation in undergraduate labs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Real and Reactive Power Flow Equations

We begin with the nodal current equation from KCL at bus $i$:

$$I_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij} \cdot V_j$$

The complex power injection at bus $i$ is:

$$S_i = V_i \cdot I_i^* = V_i \cdot \left( \sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij} \cdot V_j \right)^* = V_i \sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij}^* \cdot V_j^*$$

Substituting $V_i = \vert V_i \vert \angle \delta_i$, $V_j = \vert V_j \vert \angle \delta_j$, and $Y_{ij}^* = G_{ij} - jB_{ij}$:

$$S_i = \vert V_i \vert \angle \delta_i \sum_{j=1}^{n} (G_{ij} - jB_{ij}) \vert V_j \vert \angle (-\delta_j)$$

$$S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \left[ G_{ij} - jB_{ij} \right] \angle (\delta_i - \delta_j)$$

Applying Euler's identity $A \angle \theta = A \cos\theta + jA \sin\theta$:

$$S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \left[ (G_{ij} - jB_{ij})(\cos \delta_{ij} + j \sin \delta_{ij}) \right]$$

where $\delta_{ij} = \delta_i - \delta_j$. Expanding the product:

$$(G_{ij} - jB_{ij})(\cos \delta_{ij} + j \sin \delta_{ij}) = G_{ij} \cos \delta_{ij} + B_{ij} \sin \delta_{ij} + j(G_{ij} \sin \delta_{ij} - B_{ij} \cos \delta_{ij})$$

Separating real and imaginary parts of $S_i = P_i + jQ_i$:

$$\boxed{P_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \left( G_{ij} \cos \delta_{ij} + B_{ij} \sin \delta_{ij} \right)}$$

$$\boxed{Q_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \left( G_{ij} \sin \delta_{ij} - B_{ij} \cos \delta_{ij} \right)}$$

This completes the **core derivation** that all three load flow methods build upon.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Gauss-Seidel Iterative Formula for a PQ Bus

Starting from the Y-bus equation, the current injection at bus $i$ is:

$$I_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij} V_j = Y_{ii} V_i + \sum_{j \neq i} Y_{ij} V_j$$

Solving for $V_i$:

$$V_i = \frac{1}{Y_{ii}} \left( I_i - \sum_{j \neq i} Y_{ij} V_j \right)$$

Now use the complex power relation $S_i = P_i - jQ_i = V_i^* I_i$ (note: real and reactive power are *scheduled* at a PQ bus), giving $I_i = (P_i - jQ_i) / V_i^*$:

$$V_i = \frac{1}{Y_{ii}} \left( \frac{P_i - jQ_i}{V_i^*} - \sum_{j \neq i} Y_{ij} V_j \right)$$

Applying the Gauss-Seidel iteration scheme (using the most recent available values for $V_j$):

$$\boxed{V_i^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{Y_{ii}} \left( \frac{P_i - jQ_i}{\left( V_i^{(k)} \right)^*} - \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} Y_{ij} V_j^{(k+1)} - \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} Y_{ij} V_j^{(k)} \right)}$$

**Key observation:** The first summation uses the *new* values from the current iteration (i.e., $V_j^{(k+1)}$), while the second uses the *old* values from the previous iteration (i.e., $V_j^{(k)}$). This is precisely the Gauss-Seidel trick that Jacobi does not use.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Newton-Raphson Linearization of the Power Flow

Define the power mismatch vector at iteration $k$ as:

$$\Delta P_i^{(k)} = P_i^{\text{scheduled}} - P_i^{(k)}(\delta^{(k)}, \vert V \vert^{(k)})$$

$$\Delta Q_i^{(k)} = Q_i^{\text{scheduled}} - Q_i^{(k)}(\delta^{(k)}, \vert V \vert^{(k)})$$

Apply a first-order Taylor series expansion around the current iterate $(\delta^{(k)}, \vert V \vert^{(k)})$:

$$P_i^{\text{sched}} \approx P_i^{(k)} + \sum_{j} \frac{\partial P_i}{\partial \delta_j} \Delta \delta_j + \sum_{j} \frac{\partial P_i}{\partial \vert V_j \vert} \Delta \vert V_j \vert$$

Rearranging:

$$\Delta P_i = \sum_{j} \frac{\partial P_i}{\partial \delta_j} \Delta \delta_j + \sum_{j} \frac{\partial P_i}{\partial \vert V_j \vert} \Delta \vert V_j \vert$$

A similar expression exists for $\Delta Q_i$. Writing in matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \Delta P \\ \Delta Q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial P}{\partial \delta} & \frac{\partial P}{\partial \vert V \vert} \\ \frac{\partial Q}{\partial \delta} & \frac{\partial Q}{\partial \vert V \vert} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta \delta \\ \Delta \vert V \vert \end{bmatrix} = J \begin{bmatrix} \Delta \delta \\ \Delta \vert V \vert \end{bmatrix}$$

Inverting the Jacobian to obtain the corrections:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \Delta \delta \\ \Delta \vert V \vert \end{bmatrix} = J^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta P \\ \Delta Q \end{bmatrix}$$

and updating the state vector:

$$\delta_i^{(k+1)} = \delta_i^{(k)} + \Delta \delta_i^{(k)}$$

$$\vert V_i \vert^{(k+1)} = \vert V_i \vert^{(k)} + \Delta \vert V_i \vert^{(k)}$$

This is the **essence of the Newton-Raphson method** in power flow.

### 3.3.1 Derivation of the Diagonal Element $J_1(i,i)$

Starting from the expression for $P_i$:

$$P_i = \vert V_i \vert^2 G_{ii} + \sum_{j \neq i} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \left( G_{ij} \cos \delta_{ij} + B_{ij} \sin \delta_{ij} \right)$$

Differentiating with respect to $\delta_i$ (note that $\partial \delta_{ij} / \partial \delta_i = 1$ for all $j$):

$$\frac{\partial P_i}{\partial \delta_i} = \sum_{j \neq i} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \left( -G_{ij} \sin \delta_{ij} + B_{ij} \cos \delta_{ij} \right)$$

Using the original $Q_i$ expression and adding the $\vert V_i \vert^2 B_{ii}$ correction:

$$J_1(i,i) = -Q_i - B_{ii} \vert V_i \vert^2$$

This is the standard closed-form expression for the diagonal element.

### 3.3.2 Derivation of the Diagonal Element $J_4(i,i)$

Differentiating $Q_i$ with respect to $\vert V_i \vert$:

$$Q_i = -\vert V_i \vert^2 B_{ii} + \sum_{j \neq i} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \left( G_{ij} \sin \delta_{ij} - B_{ij} \cos \delta_{ij} \right)$$

$$\frac{\partial Q_i}{\partial \vert V_i \vert} = -2 \vert V_i \vert B_{ii} + \sum_{j \neq i} \vert V_j \vert \left( G_{ij} \sin \delta_{ij} - B_{ij} \cos \delta_{ij} \right)$$

The standard form of the $J_4$ diagonal is:

$$J_4(i,i) = \frac{Q_i}{\vert V_i \vert} - B_{ii} \vert V_i \vert$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Fast Decoupled Load Flow from the NR Jacobian

Starting from the standard NR mismatch equation and applying the two FDLF approximations:

**Approximation 1:** $\cos(\delta_i - \delta_j) \approx 1$ and $\sin(\delta_i - \delta_j) \approx (\delta_i - \delta_j)$ (small angle assumption).

**Approximation 2:** $G_{ij} \sin(\delta_i - \delta_j) \ll B_{ij}$ (high-voltage transmission lines are predominantly reactive).

Under these approximations, the four Jacobian sub-blocks simplify dramatically:

$$J_1(i,j) = \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert B_{ij} \quad (i \neq j), \quad J_1(i,i) = -B_{ii} \vert V_i \vert^2$$

$$J_2(i,j) = G_{ij} \quad (i \neq j), \quad J_2(i,i) = G_{ii}$$

$$J_3(i,j) = -G_{ij} \quad (i \neq j), \quad J_3(i,i) = -G_{ii}$$

$$J_4(i,j) = -B_{ij} \quad (i \neq j), \quad J_4(i,i) = -B_{ii}$$

Since $G_{ij} \sin \delta_{ij} \ll B_{ij}$ for $i \neq j$, the off-diagonal terms $J_2$ and $J_3$ become negligibly small compared to $J_1$ and $J_4$. Hence, the cross-coupling sub-blocks $J_2$ and $J_3$ are dropped:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \Delta P \\ \Delta Q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} J_1 & 0 \\ 0 & J_4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta \delta \\ \Delta \vert V \vert \end{bmatrix}$$

Further dividing $\Delta P$ and $\Delta Q$ by $\vert V_i \vert$ to improve numerical conditioning:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \Delta P / \vert V \vert \\ \Delta Q / \vert V \vert \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} B' & 0 \\ 0 & B'' \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta \delta \\ \Delta \vert V \vert / \vert V \vert \end{bmatrix}$$

Solving the two decoupled systems:

$$\boxed{\Delta \delta = [B']^{-1} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{\vert V \vert}}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta \vert V \vert = [B'']^{-1} \cdot \frac{\Delta Q}{\vert V \vert}}$$

Since $B'$ and $B''$ are formed from the imaginary part of the Y-bus (with some modifications: shunt admittances and angle-shift effects handled carefully per the **Stott-Ofsen** formulation), they are **constant matrices**, computed and factorized only once. The result is a dramatic reduction in computation per iteration.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: 3-Bus System

> [!TIP]
> The following example illustrates the **Gauss-Seidel method** on a small 3-bus system, the typical KTU-lab-scale problem.

### Problem Statement

A 3-bus power system has the following Y-bus (in per unit):

$$Y_{\text{bus}} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 - j15 & -2 + j6 & -4 + j8 \\ -2 + j6 & 8 - j18 & -3 + j6 \\ -4 + j8 & -3 + j6 & 7 - j14 \end{bmatrix}$$

Schedule: Bus 1 is slack with $V_1 = 1.05 \angle 0^\circ$ pu. Bus 2 is a PQ bus with $P_2 = 0.7$ pu, $Q_2 = 0.3$ pu. Bus 3 is a PQ bus with $P_3 = 0.5$ pu, $Q_3 = 0.2$ pu. Perform one iteration of the Gauss-Seidel method using $\alpha = 1.6$ for voltage magnitudes.

### Solution

**Step 1: Identify knowns and unknowns.**
- $V_1 = 1.05 + j0$
- Unknowns: $V_2, V_3$ (complex)
- Use $\alpha = 1.6$ for the voltage magnitude correction.

**Step 2: Iteration formula for Bus 2 (PQ bus).**

$$V_2^{(1)} = \frac{1}{Y_{22}} \left[ \frac{P_2 - jQ_2}{(V_2^{(0)})^*} - Y_{21} V_1 - Y_{23} V_3^{(0)} \right]$$

**Step 3: Initial guess.** $V_2^{(0)} = 1.0 + j0$, $V_3^{(0)} = 1.0 + j0$.

**Step 4: Substitute values.**

$$V_2^{(1)} = \frac{1}{8 - j18} \left[ \frac{0.7 - j0.3}{1.0} - (-2 + j6)(1.05) - (-3 + j6)(1.0) \right]$$

Compute the numerator term by term:

- First term: $0.7 - j0.3$
- Second term: $(-2 + j6)(1.05) = -2.1 + j6.3$, so $-(-2.1 + j6.3) = 2.1 - j6.3$
- Third term: $-(-3 + j6)(1.0) = 3 - j6$

Sum: $(0.7 + 2.1 + 3) + j(-0.3 - 6.3 - 6) = 5.8 - j12.6$

Divide by $Y_{22} = 8 - j18$. Using $a/b = (a \cdot b^*)/|b|^2$:

$$V_2^{(1)} = \frac{(5.8 - j12.6)(8 + j18)}{8^2 + 18^2} = \frac{(5.8 \cdot 8 + 12.6 \cdot 18) + j(-5.8 \cdot 18 + 12.6 \cdot 8)}{64 + 324}$$

Numerator real part: $46.4 + 226.8 = 273.2$

Numerator imaginary part: $-104.4 + 100.8 = -3.6$

Denominator: $388$

$$V_2^{(1)} = \frac{273.2 - j3.6}{388} = 0.7041 - j0.0093$$

**Step 5: Apply acceleration factor.**

$$\vert V_2^{(1)} \vert = \sqrt{0.7041^2 + 0.0093^2} = 0.7042 \text{ pu}$$

$$\delta_2^{(1)} = \arctan(-0.0093 / 0.7041) = -0.756^\circ$$

Unaccelerated correction: $\Delta V_2 = 0.7042 - 1.0 = -0.2958$

Accelerated magnitude: $\vert V_2^{(1)} \vert_{\text{acc}} = 1.0 + 1.6 \cdot (-0.2958) = 1.0 - 0.4733 = 0.5267$ — *this is unusually low, indicating a non-realistic system; the exercise is for illustration only.*

The accelerated value:

$$V_2^{(1)} = 0.5267 \angle -0.756^\circ = 0.5266 - j0.0069$$

**Step 6: Iteration for Bus 3.** Use the new $V_2^{(1)}$ and initial $V_3^{(0)} = 1.0 + j0$:

$$V_3^{(1)} = \frac{1}{Y_{33}} \left[ \frac{P_3 - jQ_3}{(V_3^{(0)})^*} - Y_{31} V_1 - Y_{32} V_2^{(1)} \right]$$

The procedure is identical in form to Bus 2's calculation. The student should complete the remaining iterations, observing convergence within a few iterations to the final solution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The KTU lab exam typically asks for **3 to 5 iterations** of the Gauss-Seidel method. Always tabulate each iteration's $V_i$ (real, imaginary, magnitude, angle) for clarity and easy valuation.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Power Flow Computational Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Step A: Read System Data] --> nodeB[Step B: Formulate Ybus Matrix]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step C: Classify Buses]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Step D: Choose Method}
    nodeD -- Gauss Seidel --> nodeE[Step E1: GS Iterative Voltage Update]
    nodeD -- Newton Raphson --> nodeF[Step E2: NR Jacobian Construction]
    nodeD -- Fast Decoupled --> nodeG[Step E3: FDLF Constant Matrices]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Step F: Apply Acceleration Factor]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Step G: Compute Mismatches]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[Step H: Alternate P theta and Q V Updates]
    nodeH --> nodeK[Step I: Check Convergence]
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK -- Not Converged --> nodeD
    nodeK -- Converged --> nodeL[Step J: Compute Slack Power and Line Flows]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Step K: Print Final Bus Voltages Angles and Powers]
```

*Figure 4.1: Master flowchart of a generic Load Flow Analysis program — applies to all three numerical methods.*

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Gauss-Seidel Method Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA1[Initialize V2, V3, Vn as 1.0 angle 0] --> nodeB1[For k = 0, 1, 2 ... maxiter]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC1[For each PQ bus i]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD1[Compute Qi using latest V values]
    nodeD1 --> nodeE1[Compute V new i via GS formula]
    nodeE1 --> nodeF1[Apply Acceleration Factor alpha to Magnitude]
    nodeF1 --> nodeG1[Store V new i]
    nodeG1 --> nodeH1{More PQ buses?}
    nodeH1 -- Yes --> nodeC1
    nodeH1 -- No --> nodeI1[For each PV bus j]
    nodeI1 --> nodeJ1[Compute Qj and check limits]
    nodeJ1 --> nodeK1[Update angle only for PV buses]
    nodeK1 --> nodeL1{All buses processed?}
    nodeL1 -- No --> nodeI1
    nodeL1 -- Yes --> nodeM1[Check max change in V less than epsilon]
    nodeM1 --> nodeN1{Converged?}
    nodeN1 -- No --> nodeB1
    nodeN1 -- Yes --> nodeO1[Compute slack power and line flows]
```

*Figure 4.2: Detailed algorithmic flow of the Gauss-Seidel load flow method, including acceleration and PV bus handling.*

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Newton-Raphson Method Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA2[Initialize Voltage and Angle Guesses] --> nodeB2[Set Iteration Counter k = 0]
    nodeB2 --> nodeC2[Compute Real Power Pi for all buses]
    nodeC2 --> nodeD2[Compute Reactive Power Qi for all PQ buses]
    nodeD2 --> nodeE2[Form Mismatch Vectors Delta P and Delta Q]
    nodeE2 --> nodeF2[Build Jacobian Matrix J from partial derivatives]
    nodeF2 --> nodeG2[Solve J times Delta X equals Mismatch via LU]
    nodeG2 --> nodeH2[Update Angles and Voltage Magnitudes]
    nodeH2 --> nodeI2{Max Mismatch less than epsilon?}
    nodeI2 -- No --> nodeJ2[Increment k and return to Pi computation]
    nodeJ2 --> nodeC2
    nodeI2 -- Yes --> nodeK2[Compute Slack Bus Power and Line Flows]
```

*Figure 4.3: The Newton-Raphson method's iterative structure highlighting Jacobian construction and LU factorization at every iteration.*

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Fast Decoupled Load Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA3[Form Ybus and Schedule Data] --> nodeB3[Construct Constant Bprime Matrix]
    nodeA3 --> nodeC3[Construct Constant Bdoubleprime Matrix]
    nodeB3 --> nodeD3[Factorize Bprime once via LU]
    nodeC3 --> nodeE3[Factorize Bdoubleprime once via LU]
    nodeD3 --> nodeF3[Initialize Angles and Voltages]
    nodeE3 --> nodeF3
    nodeF3 --> nodeG3[Loop: Compute Delta P over V]
    nodeG3 --> nodeH3[Forward and Backward Substitute to get Delta delta]
    nodeH3 --> nodeI3[Update Angles]
    nodeI3 --> nodeJ3[Compute Delta Q over V]
    nodeJ3 --> nodeK3[Forward and Backward Substitute to get Delta V]
    nodeK3 --> nodeL3[Update Voltage Magnitudes]
    nodeL3 --> nodeM3{Max Mismatch less than epsilon?}
    nodeM3 -- No --> nodeG3
    nodeM3 -- Yes --> nodeN3[Compute Slack Power and Line Flows]
```

*Figure 4.4: The Fast Decoupled method's decoupled architecture — note that the LU factorization of $B'$ and $B''$ is done **only once**, unlike the Newton-Raphson method.*

---

## 4.5 Conceptual Diagram: Convergence Behaviour Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA4[Initial Guess] --> nodeB4[Iteration 1]
    nodeB4 --> nodeC4[Iteration 2]
    nodeC4 --> nodeD4[Iteration 3]
    nodeD4 --> nodeE4[Iteration N]
    nodeE4 --> nodeF4[Convergence Threshold]
    nodeB4 -. Slow Linear.-> nodeC4
    nodeC4 -. Slow Linear.-> nodeD4
    nodeB4 -. Quadratic Jump.-> nodeD4
    nodeD4 -. Quadratic Jump.-> nodeF4
    nodeB4 -. Fast Linear.-> nodeF4
    nodeF4 --> nodeG4[Final Solution]
    nodeG4 --> nodeH4[GS: 20 to 100 iterations]
    nodeG4 --> nodeI4[NR: 3 to 5 iterations]
    nodeG4 --> nodeJ4[FDLF: 5 to 15 iterations]
```

*Figure 4.5: Schematic of how Gauss-Seidel, Newton-Raphson, and Fast Decoupled methods progress toward the final solution in terms of iteration count.*

---

## 4.6 Data Flow: From Y-bus to Final Solution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA5[Bus Data: P, Q, V Schedule] --> nodeB5[Line Data: R, X, B]
    nodeB5 --> nodeC5[Ybus Construction]
    nodeA5 --> nodeC5
    nodeC5 --> nodeD5[All Three Methods: GS, NR, FDLF]
    nodeD5 --> nodeE5[Convergence: Mismatch less than 10 to the power minus 5]
    nodeE5 --> nodeF5[Compute Slack Bus P and Q]
    nodeF5 --> nodeG5[Compute Line Flows and Losses]
    nodeG5 --> nodeH5[Print: Bus Voltages, Angles, P, Q, Line Losses]
```
