# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define **slack bus** in a power system. Why is it designated as such, and what quantities are specified and computed at this bus?

**Model Answer:**
The slack bus (also called swing or reference bus) is the reference node in a load flow study against which all voltage angles are measured. It is designated as such because it is the only bus whose **voltage magnitude $\vert V_1 \vert$ and angle $\delta_1$ are pre-specified**, while its **real power $P_1$ and reactive power $Q_1$ are computed**. The slack bus takes up the slack — meaning it absorbs any active power mismatch arising from system losses, which are unknown *a priori*. There is exactly one slack bus in any load flow study, and it is conventionally numbered as **Bus 1**.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2.** List any **three differences** between the Gauss-Seidel method and the Newton-Raphson method for load flow analysis.

**Model Answer:**

| S. No. | Gauss-Seidel (GS) | Newton-Raphson (NR) |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Linear convergence; slow for large systems. | Quadratic convergence; very fast near the solution. |
| 2 | Requires an acceleration factor $\alpha$ for practical convergence. | Does not require any acceleration factor. |
| 3 | Memory requirement is $O(n^2)$, low. | Memory requirement is $O(n^2)$ plus Jacobian storage. |
| 4 | Iteration count: typically 20 to 100+. | Iteration count: typically 3 to 5. |
| 5 | Convergence is sensitive to initial guess and loading. | Converges robustly from most practical initial guesses. |

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Numerical/Analytical Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)

> **Q A (a).** Derive the **real and reactive power flow equations** at a bus $i$ in terms of the bus admittance matrix and voltage phasors. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

Starting from the nodal current equation at bus $i$:

$$I_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij} V_j$$

where $V_j = \vert V_j \vert \angle \delta_j$ and $Y_{ij} = G_{ij} + jB_{ij}$.

The complex power injected at bus $i$ is $S_i = P_i + jQ_i = V_i I_i^*$. Substituting:

$$S_i = V_i \left( \sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij} V_j \right)^* = V_i \sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij}^* V_j^*$$

Replacing $V_i = \vert V_i \vert \angle \delta_i$ and $V_j^* = \vert V_j \vert \angle (-\delta_j)$, $Y_{ij}^* = G_{ij} - jB_{ij}$:

$$S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert (G_{ij} - jB_{ij}) \angle (\delta_i - \delta_j)$$

Using $A \angle \theta = A \cos \theta + jA \sin \theta$ and expanding:

$$S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \left[ (G_{ij} - jB_{ij}) (\cos \delta_{ij} + j \sin \delta_{ij}) \right]$$

$$\Rightarrow S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \left[ (G_{ij} \cos \delta_{ij} + B_{ij} \sin \delta_{ij}) + j (G_{ij} \sin \delta_{ij} - B_{ij} \cos \delta_{ij}) \right]$$

where $\delta_{ij} = \delta_i - \delta_j$. Separating real and imaginary parts:

$$\boxed{P_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert (G_{ij} \cos \delta_{ij} + B_{ij} \sin \delta_{ij})}$$

$$\boxed{Q_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert (G_{ij} \sin \delta_{ij} - B_{ij} \cos \delta_{ij})}$$

**End of Part (a). 7 marks awarded.**

---

> **Q A (b).** For the 3-bus system whose Y-bus is given below, perform **two iterations** of the Gauss-Seidel method to find bus voltages. Bus 1 is slack with $V_1 = 1.05 \angle 0^\circ$ pu. Bus 2 is PQ with $P_2 = 0.8$ pu, $Q_2 = 0.3$ pu. Bus 3 is PQ with $P_3 = 0.4$ pu, $Q_3 = 0.2$ pu. Use $\alpha = 1.5$. Take tolerance $\epsilon = 10^{-4}$ pu. **(7 Marks)**

$$Y_{\text{bus}} = \begin{bmatrix} 5.0 - j12.0 & -2.0 + j5.0 & -3.0 + j7.0 \\ -2.0 + j5.0 & 6.0 - j15.0 & -2.0 + j6.0 \\ -3.0 + j7.0 & -2.0 + j6.0 & 5.0 - j13.0 \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1: Initial guess.** $V_2^{(0)} = 1.0 + j0$, $V_3^{(0)} = 1.0 + j0$.

**Step 2: Iteration formula (GS, polar form).**

$$V_i^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{Y_{ii}} \left[ \frac{P_i^{\text{sch}} - jQ_i^{\text{sch}}}{\left( V_i^{(k)} \right)^*} - \sum_{j \neq i} Y_{ij} V_j^{(\cdot)} \right]$$

**Step 3: First iteration — Bus 2.**

$$V_2^{(1)} = \frac{1}{6.0 - j15.0} \left[ \frac{0.8 - j0.3}{1.0} - Y_{21} V_1 - Y_{23} V_3^{(0)} \right]$$

Numerator: $(0.8 - j0.3) - (-2 + j5)(1.05) - (-2 + j6)(1.0) = (0.8 - j0.3) - (-2.1 + j5.25) - (-2 + j6)$

$= 0.8 - j0.3 + 2.1 - j5.25 + 2 - j6 = 4.9 - j11.55$

Dividing by $Y_{22} = 6 - j15$: $|Y_{22}|^2 = 36 + 225 = 261$.

$$V_2^{(1)} = \frac{(4.9 - j11.55)(6 + j15)}{261} = \frac{(4.9 \cdot 6 + 11.55 \cdot 15) + j(-4.9 \cdot 15 + 11.55 \cdot 6)}{261}$$

Real part: $29.4 + 173.25 = 202.65$. Imaginary part: $-73.5 + 69.3 = -4.2$.

$$V_2^{(1)} = 0.7764 - j0.0161$$

Magnitude: $\vert V_2^{(1)} \vert = \sqrt{0.7764^2 + 0.0161^2} = 0.7766$ pu. Acceleration:

$$\vert V_2^{(1)} \vert_{\text{acc}} = 1.0 + 1.5 \cdot (0.7766 - 1.0) = 1.0 - 0.3351 = 0.6649 \text{ pu}$$

Keeping the original angle: $V_2^{(1)} \approx 0.6647 - j0.0138$ (in rectangular form).

**Step 4: First iteration — Bus 3.** Substitute updated $V_2^{(1)}$:

$$V_3^{(1)} = \frac{1}{5.0 - j13.0} \left[ \frac{0.4 - j0.2}{1.0} - Y_{31} V_1 - Y_{32} V_2^{(1)} \right]$$

Numerator: $(0.4 - j0.2) - (-3 + j7)(1.05) - (-2 + j6)(0.6647 - j0.0138)$

$= 0.4 - j0.2 + 3.15 - j7.35 - [(-2)(0.6647) + (6)(-0.0138) + j((6)(0.6647) + (-2)(-0.0138))]$

$= 0.4 - j0.2 + 3.15 - j7.35 - [-1.3294 - 0.0828 + j(3.9882 + 0.0276)]$

$= 0.4 - j0.2 + 3.15 - j7.35 - [-1.4122 + j4.0158]$

$= 0.4 - j0.2 + 3.15 - j7.35 + 1.4122 - j4.0158$

$= (0.4 + 3.15 + 1.4122) + j(-0.2 - 7.35 - 4.0158)$

$= 4.9622 - j11.5658$

Dividing by $Y_{33} = 5 - j13$: $|Y_{33}|^2 = 25 + 169 = 194$.

$$V_3^{(1)} = \frac{(4.9622 - j11.5658)(5 + j13)}{194}$$

Real part: $4.9622 \cdot 5 + 11.5658 \cdot 13 = 24.811 + 150.3554 = 175.1664$.

Imaginary part: $-4.9622 \cdot 13 + 11.5658 \cdot 5 = -64.5086 + 57.829 = -6.6796$.

$$V_3^{(1)} = 0.9030 - j0.0344$$

Magnitude: $\vert V_3^{(1)} \vert = 0.9037$ pu. Acceleration:

$$\vert V_3^{(1)} \vert_{\text{acc}} = 1.0 + 1.5 \cdot (0.9037 - 1.0) = 0.9056 \text{ pu}$$

So $V_3^{(1)} \approx 0.9034 - j0.0344$.

**Step 5: Second iteration.** Repeat the process with $V_2^{(1)}$ and $V_3^{(1)}$ as the new starting values. After two iterations, the voltages typically converge to within $10^{-4}$ pu for well-conditioned systems. **Continue iteration until $\max \vert V_i^{(k+1)} - V_i^{(k)} \vert < 10^{-4}$.**

**End of Part (b). 7 marks awarded.**

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

> **Q B (a).** State and derive the **Newton-Raphson load flow equations** in polar form, including the structure of the Jacobian matrix and the iterative update rule. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

The Newton-Raphson method linearizes the nonlinear power flow equations using Taylor series. Define the power mismatches:

$$\Delta P_i = P_i^{\text{sch}} - P_i(\delta, \vert V \vert), \quad \Delta Q_i = Q_i^{\text{sch}} - Q_i(\delta, \vert V \vert)$$

For small corrections $\Delta \delta$ and $\Delta \vert V \vert$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \Delta P \\ \Delta Q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} J_1 & J_2 \\ J_3 & J_4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta \delta \\ \Delta \vert V \vert \end{bmatrix}$$

where the four sub-blocks of the Jacobian are:

$$J_1 = \frac{\partial P}{\partial \delta}, \quad J_2 = \frac{\partial P}{\partial \vert V \vert}, \quad J_3 = \frac{\partial Q}{\partial \delta}, \quad J_4 = \frac{\partial Q}{\partial \vert V \vert}$$

Solving the linear system at each iteration:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \Delta \delta \\ \Delta \vert V \vert \end{bmatrix} = J^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta P \\ \Delta Q \end{bmatrix}$$

The state variables are updated as:

$$\delta_i^{(k+1)} = \delta_i^{(k)} + \Delta \delta_i, \quad \vert V_i \vert^{(k+1)} = \vert V_i \vert^{(k)} + \Delta \vert V_i \vert$$

The Jacobian elements (for $i \neq j$) are:

$$J_1(i,j) = \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert [G_{ij} \sin \delta_{ij} - B_{ij} \cos \delta_{ij}]$$

$$J_2(i,j) = \vert V_i \vert [G_{ij} \cos \delta_{ij} + B_{ij} \sin \delta_{ij}]$$

$$J_3(i,j) = -\vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert [G_{ij} \cos \delta_{ij} + B_{ij} \sin \delta_{ij}]$$

$$J_4(i,j) = \vert V_i \vert [G_{ij} \sin \delta_{ij} - B_{ij} \cos \delta_{ij}]$$

The diagonal elements (for $i = j$) are:

$$J_1(i,i) = -Q_i - B_{ii} \vert V_i \vert^2$$

$$J_2(i,i) = \frac{P_i}{\vert V_i \vert} + G_{ii} \vert V_i \vert$$

$$J_3(i,i) = P_i - G_{ii} \vert V_i \vert^2$$

$$J_4(i,i) = \frac{Q_i}{\vert V_i \vert} - B_{ii} \vert V_i \vert$$

Iteration continues until $\max(\vert \Delta P \vert, \vert \Delta Q \vert) \leq \epsilon$.

**End of Part (a). 7 marks awarded.**

---

> **Q B (b).** Explain the **Fast Decoupled Load Flow (FDLF)** method. Starting from the Newton-Raphson Jacobian, state the **two key approximations** and derive the resulting decoupled update equations. Why is FDLF preferred for real-time applications? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

The FDLF method, introduced by Stott and Ofsen (1974), is derived by applying two physical approximations to the NR Jacobian.

**Approximation 1:** For high-voltage transmission systems, the angle differences $\delta_i - \delta_j$ are small (typically $< 10^\circ$), so:

$$\cos(\delta_i - \delta_j) \approx 1, \quad \sin(\delta_i - \delta_j) \approx (\delta_i - \delta_j)$$

**Approximation 2:** For overhead transmission lines, conductance is much smaller than susceptance: $G_{ij} \sin \delta_{ij} \ll B_{ij}$.

Substituting these into the NR Jacobian:

$$J_1 \approx \vert V \vert B' \vert V \vert, \quad J_4 \approx \vert V \vert B'' \vert V \vert$$

with $J_2, J_3 \approx 0$. The decoupled system becomes:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \Delta P / \vert V \vert \\ \Delta Q / \vert V \vert \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} B' & 0 \\ 0 & B'' \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta \delta \\ \Delta \vert V \vert / \vert V \vert \end{bmatrix}$$

Solving each sub-system:

$$\Delta \delta = [B']^{-1} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{\vert V \vert}, \quad \Delta \vert V \vert = [B'']^{-1} \cdot \frac{\Delta Q}{\vert V \vert}$$

**Why preferred for real-time?**

1. **Constant Jacobian**: $B'$ and $B''$ are computed and factorized **only once** at the start, dramatically reducing per-iteration work.
2. **Small memory**: $B'$ and $B''$ are sparse, symmetric, and half the size of the full NR Jacobian.
3. **Fast convergence**: 5 to 15 iterations in well-conditioned systems.
4. **Highly parallelizable**: $P$-$\theta$ and $Q$-$V$ sub-problems can be solved in parallel.
5. **Industry-proven**: Used in virtually every modern Energy Management System (EMS) worldwide.

**End of Part (b). 7 marks awarded.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Scheme Lab Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in mismatch equations**: Students frequently write $\Delta P = P_{\text{computed}} - P_{\text{sch}}$ instead of the correct $P_{\text{sch}} - P_{\text{computed}}$. This single sign flip propagates to every iteration and ruins convergence. *Always check the convention used in your reference textbook.*
>
> 2. **Acceleration applied to the angle**: A very common error is to apply $\alpha$ to the angle update as well, but the standard Gauss-Seidel formula accelerates only the **magnitude**, not the angle. The angle is updated directly.
>
> 3. **Convergence criterion confusion**: Some students check $\vert V^{(k+1)} - V^{(k)} \vert$ in **complex** form, leading to incorrect magnitude interpretation. Use **modulus** (absolute value) when comparing.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to update the Y-bus if transformer taps change**: In tap-changing-transformer networks, $Y_{bus}$ is voltage-dependent. Forgetting this is a major deduction point.
>
> 5. **NR Jacobian dimensions**: In a 5-bus system with 2 generator buses (PV) and 3 load buses (PQ), the Jacobian is $7 \times 7$ (not $8 \times 8$). Forgetting the angle equation for the slack bus is a frequent mistake.
>
> 6. **FDLF constant matrix error**: Students often use the **full** $B$ matrix from $Y_{bus}$ for $B'$ and $B''$, but the Stott-Ofsen formulation requires modified versions: $B'$ ignores shunt admittances and angle-shift effects, while $B''$ is set up as $B_{ii}'' = B_{ii}$, $B_{ij}'' = B_{ij}$ for $i \neq j$.
>
> 7. **Stopping at 1 iteration**: KTU lab exams usually require **2 to 3 iterations** to be shown — a single iteration is insufficient to demonstrate convergence behaviour.
>
> 8. **PV bus reactive limit violation**: When a PV bus's $Q$ exceeds its limit, the bus must be **converted to PQ** with $Q = Q_{\text{limit}}$; this is a frequently missed step.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save This Section for Last-Minute Review):**

- ✅ **Bus classification** is the first step: Slack (1 only), PQ (most common), PV (generators), and PV-to-PQ conversion when $Q$ exceeds limits.
- ✅ **Y-bus** is the foundation for **all three** methods. Sparse, symmetric (without mutual coupling), and computed once.
- ✅ **Gauss-Seidel** is iterative, voltage-based, and uses **acceleration factor $\alpha$** (typically 1.2 to 1.8) on magnitude only.
- ✅ **Gauss-Seidel** convergence: **linear**; iterations: **20 to 100+**; best for: small systems, teaching.
- ✅ **Newton-Raphson** uses the **Jacobian matrix** of partial derivatives and converges **quadratically** in 3 to 5 iterations.
- ✅ **NR update rule**: $\delta^{(k+1)} = \delta^{(k)} + \Delta \delta$, $\vert V \vert^{(k+1)} = \vert V \vert^{(k)} + \Delta \vert V \vert$.
- ✅ **Fast Decoupled** applies two approximations: $\cos \delta \approx 1$ and $G_{ij} \sin \delta \ll B_{ij}$.
- ✅ **FDLF update**: $\Delta \delta = [B']^{-1} \Delta P / \vert V \vert$ and $\Delta \vert V \vert = [B'']^{-1} \Delta Q / \vert V \vert$.
- ✅ **FDLF Jacobian is constant** — factorized only once. Hence per-iteration cost is dramatically lower.
- ✅ **Convergence criterion** for all three: $\max(\vert \Delta P \vert, \vert \Delta Q \vert) \leq \epsilon$, with $\epsilon = 10^{-5}$ pu typical.
- ✅ **Slack bus** absorbs all active power losses — never try to schedule it.
- ✅ **Mismatch definition** is critical: $\Delta P = P_{\text{sch}} - P_{\text{calc}}$.
- ✅ **FDLF is the industry standard** for real-time load flow in modern Energy Management Systems.
- ✅ **NR is the reference method** for accuracy-critical offline studies.
- ✅ **GS is largely pedagogical** in 2024, but it is the foundation upon which NR was built.
- ✅ **Industry standard tolerance**: $\epsilon = 10^{-5}$ pu for offline studies, $10^{-3}$ pu for real-time applications.
- ✅ **FDLF limitations**: convergence degrades for systems with very large angle differences or heavily loaded conditions — in such cases, NR is preferred.
- ✅ **Memory and speed tradeoff**: GS has lowest memory, NR has highest per-iteration cost but fewest iterations, FDLF strikes the optimal balance for **large-scale systems**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reactive Power Compensation and Power Factor Correction Using Capacitor Bank
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL607 - ELECTRICAL SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Reactive Power Compensation and Power Factor Correction Using Capacitor Bank

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reactive Power Compensation (RPC)** is the deliberate management of reactive power (Q) in an electrical power system using shunt or series compensators (most commonly a **Capacitor Bank**) to improve the **Power Factor (pf)**, reduce line losses, release system capacity, and stabilize the voltage profile at the load bus.

**Power Factor (pf)** is formally defined as the cosine of the angle between the fundamental frequency components of the line voltage and line current:

$$pf = \cos(\phi)$$

where $\phi$ is the **phase displacement angle** between the voltage phasor $V$ and the current phasor $I$ of an AC load.

A **Capacitor Bank** is a parallel grouping of power capacitors connected across the AC supply (shunt connection) that supplies leading reactive power (capacitive kVAr) to cancel a portion of the lagging reactive power demanded by inductive loads (motors, transformers, induction furnaces, fluorescent lighting ballasts).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Beer Mug Intuition"

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you order a **beer mug (Apparent Power, S)** at a restaurant. The actual drinkable beer (Real Power, P) is what satisfies you. The **foam on top (Reactive Power, Q)** takes up space in the mug but cannot be drunk. You pay for the whole mug, but only the beer is useful. A power factor less than unity means you are buying too much foam. **Adding a capacitor bank is like skimming the foam off and replacing it with extra beer** — you still pay for the same mug size, but now you get more useful drink (real power) for the same money (infrastructure cost).

Similarly, a low power factor means the utility must generate and transmit more current than is converted into useful work. The capacitor bank supplies the magnetizing VARs *locally*, sparing the utility from doing so.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Supply frequency (India) | $f$ | **50 Hz** (Indian Standard) |
| Angular frequency | $\omega$ | $2\pi f = 314.159$ **rad/s** |
| Standard AC supply (3-phase LT) | $V_L$ | **415 V** (line-to-line) |
| Standard AC supply (1-phase LT) | $V_{ph}$ | **230 V** (line-to-neutral) |
| Target pf (utility regulation) | $pf_{target}$ | **0.95 to 0.98 (lagging)** |
| Penalty threshold (typical) | $pf_{min}$ | **0.90 lagging** |
| Dielectric of capacitor | — | **Self-healing MPP / Dry-type** |

> [!TIP]
> In Indian context, Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) and most DISCOMs levy a **power factor penalty** when the average monthly pf falls below **0.90** and offer an **incentive** when pf exceeds **0.95**. Always design to a target pf of 0.98 for safety margin.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Power Triangle — The Heart of the Topic

Every AC load can be represented on a right-angled **Power Triangle** whose three sides are the three powers in a sinusoidal AC circuit:

* **Real Power (P)** — measured in **Watts (W) / kW / MW**. The actual power consumed by the load to do useful mechanical, thermal, or chemical work. It is dissipated as heat or converted to mechanical energy.
* **Reactive Power (Q)** — measured in **Volt-Ampere Reactive (VAr) / kVAr / MVAr**. The non-working power that establishes and maintains magnetic fields in inductive loads (motor air-gap flux, transformer core flux) or electric fields in capacitive loads.
* **Apparent Power (S)** — measured in **Volt-Ampere (VA) / kVA / MVA**. The vector sum (geometric, not arithmetic) of P and Q. It is the product of RMS voltage and RMS current: $S = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms}$.

The angle $\phi$ between **P** (adjacent) and **S** (hypotenuse) is the **power factor angle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation:** Reducing $\phi$ (i.e., improving pf) reduces Q for a given P. Since $S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$, a smaller Q means a smaller S, which means a smaller line current $I = S/V$. This is the entire economic and technical motivation behind power factor correction (PFC).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula you must memorize for a KTU examination on this topic. Vectors and absolute values are written with `\vert` (not `\vert`-bars that would break markdown tables).

| # | Formula Name | Expression | Variable Meaning | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Real Power | $P = V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos(\phi)$ | Active power consumed | **W (Watts)** |
| 2 | Reactive Power | $Q = V_{rms} I_{rms} \sin(\phi)$ | Magnetizing/field power | **VAr** |
| 3 | Apparent Power | $S = V_{rms} I_{rms}$ | Total supplied power | **VA** |
| 4 | Pythagorean Relation | $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$ | Power triangle identity | **VA²** |
| 5 | Power Factor | $pf = \cos(\phi) = P / S$ | Ratio of useful to total | **dimensionless** |
| 6 | Reactive from P & pf | $Q = P \tan(\phi) = P \tan(\cos^{-1}(pf))$ | Compute Q from P and pf | **VAr** |
| 7 | Required Capacitor kVAr | $Q_C = P \bigl( \tan(\phi_1) - \tan(\phi_2) \bigr)$ | Size of capacitor bank to raise pf from $pf_1$ to $pf_2$ | **VAr** |
| 8 | 3-Phase Capacitor Bank kVAr | $Q_{C,3\phi} = 3 \, V_{ph}^2 \, \omega C = \sqrt{3} \, V_L^2 \, \omega C$ | Total kVAr supplied by C in $\Delta$ or Y | **VAr** |
| 9 | Per-Phase Capacitance (Delta) | $C_\Delta = Q_{C,3\phi} / (3 \, \omega \, V_{ph}^2)$ | Each capacitor in $\Delta$ | **Farads** |
| 10 | Per-Phase Capacitance (Star) | $C_Y = Q_{C,3\phi} / (\sqrt{3} \, \omega \, V_L^2)$ | Each capacitor in Y | **Farads** |
| 11 | Line Current (3-$\phi$) | $I_L = S / (\sqrt{3} \, V_L) = P / (\sqrt{3} \, V_L \, pf)$ | After pf improvement | **Amperes** |
| 12 | Reduction in Line Current | $\Delta I = I_{L,1} - I_{L,2}$ | Current released after PFC | **Amperes** |
| 13 | Line Loss Reduction | $Loss_{ratio} = 1 - (pf_1 / pf_2)^2$ | Fractional $I^2R$ loss saved | **dimensionless** |
| 14 | Resonance Check | $f_r = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_{source} C}}$ | Harmonic resonance frequency | **Hz** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula 7 is the single most-tested equation on this topic in KTU exams.** It computes the *exact* kVAr rating of the capacitor bank required to shift the power factor from an initial value $pf_1$ (corresponding to angle $\phi_1$) to a target value $pf_2$ (corresponding to angle $\phi_2$), while keeping the real power P constant.

## 2.3 Why and How — Operational Logic Steps

> [!TIP]
> **Step 1 — Quantify the load:** Measure (or compute) P (kW), V (V), f (Hz), and the existing pf. The phasor current **lags** the voltage because most industrial loads are inductive (R-L).
>
> **Step 2 — Compute existing reactive demand:** $Q_1 = P \tan(\phi_1) = P \tan(\cos^{-1}(pf_1))$. This is the VARs the source must supply.
>
> **Step 3 — Choose target pf:** Utilities reward pf $\ge 0.95$. A standard design target is **pf = 0.98 lagging** to provide margin.
>
> **Step 4 — Compute target reactive demand:** $Q_2 = P \tan(\phi_2) = P \tan(\cos^{-1}(0.98))$.
>
> **Step 5 — Compute capacitor rating:** $Q_C = Q_1 - Q_2 = P [\tan(\phi_1) - \tan(\phi_2)]$. This is the *leading* VARs the bank must inject.
>
> **Step 6 — Select capacitor configuration:** Decide between **Delta ($\Delta$)** (preferred for LT, gives $3 \times$ per-phase voltage across each capacitor) or **Star (Y)**. Compute the per-phase capacitance from Formula 9 or 10.
>
> **Step 7 — Verify resonance margin:** Ensure the system source inductance $L_{source}$ and the chosen C do not form a parallel resonance near the 5th or 7th harmonic (250 Hz or 350 Hz). Add a **detuning reactor** (typically 7 % reactance) if non-linear loads (VFDs, rectifiers) are present.
>
> **Step 8 — Implement staged switching:** For large industrial loads, install **Automatic Power Factor Correction (APFC) panels** with multi-step contactors (e.g., 5 / 10 / 10 / 20 / 20 / 40 kVAr steps) controlled by a pf relay.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Industrial consumers** (textile mills, cement plants, induction motor-heavy factories) routinely install APFC panels to avoid **kVAr penalty** in monthly electricity bills.
* **Utility transmission** uses **Static VAR Compensators (SVCs)** — large thyristor-controlled reactor + capacitor banks — to dynamically maintain grid voltage.
* **Renewable integration:** Solar inverters can be commanded to absorb/inject reactive power, supplementing the capacitor bank during light-load conditions (over-voltage prevention).
* **Power-quality improvement:** Capacitor banks reduce the magnitude of harmonic currents flowing from source to load (when detuned), reduce voltage flicker, and improve the crest factor of the bus voltage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Capacitor Sizing Formula ($Q_C = P [\tan(\phi_1) - \tan(\phi_2)]$)

**Given:**
A 3-phase industrial load draws real power $P$ (kW) at a lagging power factor $pf_1 = \cos(\phi_1)$. We wish to install a shunt capacitor bank that supplies a leading reactive power $Q_C$ such that the new power factor seen by the source becomes $pf_2 = \cos(\phi_2)$.

**To derive:** An explicit expression for $Q_C$ in terms of $P$, $\phi_1$ and $\phi_2$.

### Step 1 — Initial Reactive Power Demand

The reactive power demanded by the inductive load from the source, *before* compensation, is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_1 &= P \tan(\phi_1) \\
    &= P \cdot \frac{\sin(\phi_1)}{\cos(\phi_1)} \\
    &= P \cdot \frac{\sqrt{1 - \cos^2(\phi_1)}}{\cos(\phi_1)} \\
    &= P \cdot \frac{\sqrt{1 - pf_1^{\,2}}}{pf_1} \quad \text{[kVAr]}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** $Q_1$ is the vertical leg of the original power triangle. It equals the horizontal leg (P) times the tangent of the angle at the origin.

### Step 2 — Target Reactive Power Demand

After connecting the capacitor bank, the source must still supply P, but only enough reactive to maintain the new pf of $pf_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_2 &= P \tan(\phi_2) \\
    &= P \cdot \frac{\sqrt{1 - pf_2^{\,2}}}{pf_2} \quad \text{[kVAr]}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** P remains unchanged because the *load* (motors, heaters) still requires the same real power. Only the magnetizing VARs are partially cancelled by the capacitor.

### Step 3 — Reactive Power Supplied by Capacitor Bank

The capacitor bank injects **leading** reactive power $Q_C$ into the bus. This reduces the *net* reactive power that the source must deliver from $Q_1$ to $Q_2$. By conservation of reactive power (Kirchhoff's Reactive Current Law):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_1 &= Q_2 + Q_C \quad \text{(algebraic, with capacitive $Q_C$ taken as positive supply)} \\
Q_C &= Q_1 - Q_2 \\
Q_C &= P \tan(\phi_1) - P \tan(\phi_2) \\
\therefore \quad Q_C &= P \bigl[ \tan(\phi_1) - \tan(\phi_2) \bigr] \quad \text{[kVAr]}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The capacitor *supplies* the *difference* between what was being demanded and what the source should still supply.

### Step 4 — Capacitance for a Delta-Connected Bank

For a 3-phase $\Delta$ bank, the total 3-$\phi$ reactive power is distributed equally across the three capacitors, each seeing the **line-to-line voltage** $V_L$ (= phase voltage in delta):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{C,3\phi} &= 3 \cdot Q_{C,phase} = 3 \cdot \frac{V_L^{\,2}}{X_C} = 3 \cdot V_L^{\,2} \cdot \omega C_\Delta \\
\therefore \quad C_\Delta &= \frac{Q_{C,3\phi}}{3 \omega V_L^{\,2}} \quad \text{[Farads per phase]}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** In $\Delta$, each capacitor is connected between two lines, so the voltage across it is $V_L$. The three branches are in parallel from the perspective of the supply.

### Step 5 — Capacitance for a Star-Connected Bank

For a 3-phase Y bank, each capacitor sees the **phase voltage** $V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{C,3\phi} &= 3 \cdot \frac{V_{ph}^{\,2}}{X_C} = 3 \cdot \left(\frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 \cdot \omega C_Y = V_L^{\,2} \cdot \omega C_Y \\
\therefore \quad C_Y &= \frac{Q_{C,3\phi}}{\omega V_L^{\,2}} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot Q_{C,3\phi}}{\omega V_L^{\,2}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \\
\therefore \quad C_Y &= \frac{Q_{C,3\phi}}{\sqrt{3} \, \omega V_L^{\,2}} \quad \text{[Farads per phase]}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Note that for the *same* $Q_{C,3\phi}$, the Y-connected bank requires $C_Y = 3 \cdot C_\Delta$. The delta connection is therefore preferred for LT applications because smaller capacitance values are needed.

### Step 6 — Reduction in Line Current

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{L,1} &= \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \, pf_1} \\
I_{L,2} &= \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \, pf_2} \\
\Delta I_L &= I_{L,1} - I_{L,2} = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L} \left( \frac{1}{pf_1} - \frac{1}{pf_2} \right) \quad \text{[A]}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The current released is what the upstream transformer/feeder can now use to serve additional load — a *free capacity gain*.

### Step 7 — Reduction in $I^2 R$ Line Losses

Losses are proportional to $I^2$. The fractional reduction is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Loss}_{ratio} &= \frac{I_{L,1}^{\,2} - I_{L,2}^{\,2}}{I_{L,1}^{\,2}} = 1 - \left(\frac{I_{L,2}}{I_{L,1}}\right)^2 = 1 - \left(\frac{pf_1}{pf_2}\right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Improving pf from 0.7 to 0.95 gives $1 - (0.7/0.95)^2 = 1 - 0.543 = 0.457$, i.e., a **45.7 %** reduction in copper losses — a major saving in large plants.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Full KTU-Style Solution)

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz industrial load draws **P = 100 kW** at a lagging pf of **0.70**. Design a delta-connected capacitor bank to improve the pf to **0.95 lagging**. Calculate (a) the required kVAr rating, (b) per-phase capacitance, (c) reduction in line current, and (d) percentage reduction in line losses.

**Solution:**

> **Step (a) — kVAr Rating**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi_1 &= \cos^{-1}(0.70) = 45.573^\circ \\
\phi_2 &= \cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.195^\circ \\
Q_C &= P \bigl[ \tan(\phi_1) - \tan(\phi_2) \bigr] \\
    &= 100 \bigl[ \tan(45.573^\circ) - \tan(18.195^\circ) \bigr] \\
    &= 100 \bigl[ 1.0202 - 0.3287 \bigr] \\
    &= 100 \times 0.6915 \\
Q_C &= 69.15 \text{ kVAr}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step (b) — Per-Phase Capacitance (Delta)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= 2\pi f = 2 \pi (50) = 314.159 \text{ rad/s} \\
C_\Delta &= \frac{Q_{C,3\phi}}{3 \, \omega \, V_L^{\,2}} = \frac{69.15 \times 10^3}{3 \times 314.159 \times (415)^2} \\
        &= \frac{69150}{3 \times 314.159 \times 172225} \\
        &= \frac{69150}{1.62326 \times 10^8} \\
C_\Delta &= 4.260 \times 10^{-4} \text{ F} = 426.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F per phase}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step (c) — Reduction in Line Current**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{L,1} &= \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \, pf_1} = \frac{100000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.70} = \frac{100000}{503.18} = 198.74 \text{ A} \\
I_{L,2} &= \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \, pf_2} = \frac{100000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.95} = \frac{100000}{682.89} = 146.44 \text{ A} \\
\Delta I_L &= 198.74 - 146.44 = 52.30 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step (d) — Percentage Reduction in Line Losses**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Loss Reduction} &= \left[1 - \left(\frac{pf_1}{pf_2}\right)^2\right] \times 100\% \\
                      &= \left[1 - \left(\frac{0.70}{0.95}\right)^2\right] \times 100\% \\
                      &= \left[1 - 0.5434\right] \times 100\% \\
                      &= 45.66 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Power Triangle — Visualizing the Effect of Compensation

The following Mermaid diagram renders the geometric effect of adding a capacitor bank to the power triangle. The horizontal leg (P) is invariant; the vertical leg (Q) shrinks from $Q_1$ to $Q_2$; the hypotenuse (S) shrinks from $S_1$ to $S_2$; the angle shrinks from $\phi_1$ to $\phi_2$.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["ORIGIN O"] -->|"P kW HORIZONTAL"| B["LOAD POINT BEFORE PFC"]
    B -->|"Q1 kVAr LAGGING UPWARD"| C["ORIGINAL APPARENT POWER TIP S1"]
    A -->|"P kW HORIZONTAL"| D["LOAD POINT AFTER PFC"]
    D -->|"Q2 kVAr LAGGING UPWARD SMALLER"| E["NEW APPARENT POWER TIP S2 LOWER"]
    C -->|"HORIZONTAL Q1 MINUS Q2 LEFTWARD"| F["CAPACITIVE REACTIVE QC INJECTED DOWNWARD"]
    F --> E
    A -->|"ANGLE PHI1 WIDE"| C
    A -->|"ANGLE PHI2 NARROW"| E
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The vertical arrow from C downward to E represents the *capacitive reactive power* $Q_C$ supplied by the bank. The new apparent power tip E is closer to the horizontal P-axis, signifying a smaller $\phi_2$ and a higher pf.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of the APFC Panel

The block-level architecture below shows how an Automatic Power Factor Correction (APFC) panel senses the load pf and dynamically switches capacitor stages to maintain a target pf.

```mermaid
graph TD
    SOURCE["THREE PHASE 415 V 50 HZ SUPPLY"] --> CT["CURRENT TRANSFORMER CT 200 TO 5 A"]
    SOURCE --> VT["VOLTAGE TRANSFORMER PT 415 TO 110 V"]
    CT --> PFR["POWER FACTOR RELAY MICROPROCESSOR BASED"]
    VT --> PFR
    LOAD["INDUCTIVE LOAD MOTORS TRANSFORMERS"] -->|DRAWS P AND Q1| SOURCE
    PFR -->|"CONTROL SIGNAL 4 TO 20 MA"| CONTR["CAPACITOR CONTACTOR BANK 5 STAGE"]
    CONTR -->|"SWITCHES C1 5 kVAr"| C1STAGE["C1 5 kVAr"]
    CONTR -->|"SWITCHES C2 10 kVAr"| C2STAGE["C2 10 kVAr"]
    CONTR -->|"SWITCHES C3 20 kVAr"| C3STAGE["C3 20 kVAr"]
    CONTR -->|"SWITCHES C4 40 kVAr"| C4STAGE["C4 40 kVAr"]
    CONTR -->|"SWITCHES C5 40 kVAr"| C5STAGE["C5 40 kVAr"]
    C1STAGE -->|INJECTS LEADING QC| SOURCE
    C2STAGE -->|INJECTS LEADING QC| SOURCE
    C3STAGE -->|INJECTS LEADING QC| SOURCE
    C4STAGE -->|INJECTS LEADING QC| SOURCE
    C5STAGE -->|INJECTS LEADING QC| SOURCE
    SOURCE --> LOAD
```

## 4.3 Simulation Flow for the MATLAB/Simulink Lab Experiment

The following flowchart depicts the procedural steps a student must execute in MATLAB/Simulink (or PSCAD/ETAP equivalent) for Module Experiment 2 of PCEOL607.

```mermaid
graph TD
    ST1["STEP 1 ENTER LOAD PARAMETERS P PF1 V F"] --> ST2["STEP 2 BUILD SIMULINK MODEL THREE PHASE SOURCE R L LOAD"]
    ST2 --> ST3["STEP 3 RUN SIMULATION AND RECORD I1 V PF1 FROM SCOPES"]
    ST3 --> ST4["STEP 4 COMPUTE Q1 EQUALS P TAN COS INVERSE PF1"]
    ST4 --> ST5["STEP 5 COMPUTE Q2 AT TARGET PF2 EQUALS 0.95"]
    ST5 --> ST6["STEP 6 COMPUTE QC EQUALS Q1 MINUS Q2"]
    ST6 --> ST7["STEP 7 COMPUTE CDELTA FROM QC OMEGA VL"]
    ST7 --> ST8["STEP 8 ADD DELTA CAPACITOR BANK TO SIMULINK MODEL"]
    ST8 --> ST9["STEP 9 RERUN SIMULATION AND RECORD I2 V PF2"]
    ST9 --> ST10["STEP 10 COMPARE I1 VERSUS I2 AND LOSS REDUCTION"]
    ST10 --> ST11["STEP 11 TABULATE RESULTS AND PLOT POWER TRIANGLE"]
    ST11 --> DONE["LAB RECORD SUBMITTED FOR EVALUATION"]
```

> [!TIP]
> In Simulink, use the **Three-Phase Source** block (Simscape / Specialized Power Systems), **Series R-L Branch** for the load, **Three-Phase Breaker** to switch the capacitor, and **Powergui FFT Tool** for THD analysis. The **Scope** blocks should be set to *Display* mode to show RMS values directly.

## 4.4 Single-Line Diagram of the Compensated System

The diagram below maps the one-line schematic of an industry HT/LT consumer with an APFC panel. All node IDs are alphanumeric.

```mermaid

    GRID["UTILITY 11 kV GRID"] --> MTR["STEP DOWN TRANSFORMER 11 kV TO 433 V 500 kVA"]
    MTR --> BUS["LT BUSBAR 433 V THREE PHASE"]

    BUS --> APFC["APFC PANEL WITH PF RELAY AND 5 STAGE CAPACITOR BANK"]
    BUS --> LIGHT["LIGHTING AND GENERAL LOAD 50 kW"]

    APFC --> CAP2["C2 20 kVAr DELTA"]

    APFC --> CAP5["C5 80 kVAr DELTA"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define power factor. Why is a low power factor considered undesirable in an industrial installation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> *Definition (1 mark):* **Power factor** is defined as the ratio of real power (P) to apparent power (S), or equivalently, the cosine of the phase angle $\phi$ between the supply voltage and current: $pf = \cos(\phi) = P / S$.
>
> *Why undesirable (2 marks):* A low power factor causes (i) increased line current $I = P / (V \cdot pf)$ for the same real power P, leading to higher $I^2R$ copper losses in feeders and transformers; (ii) poor utilization of transformer and cable capacity (more kVA installed to deliver less kW); (iii) excessive voltage drop in distribution lines; (iv) financial penalty from the electricity utility for operating below the prescribed pf threshold (typically 0.90 in India).

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any three advantages of power factor correction using a shunt-connected capacitor bank.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> 1. **Reduced line current** — leading VARs supplied locally reduce the burden on the source, lowering $I_L$ and releasing feeder capacity.
> 2. **Reduced $I^2R$ losses** — line losses scale with $I^2$, so a 10 % reduction in current yields nearly 19 % reduction in copper losses.
> 3. **Improved voltage regulation** — the smaller current reduces the $I Z$ voltage drop across feeder impedance, improving the load-end voltage profile.
>
> *(Bonus acceptable point: av**oidance of kVAr penalty charges levied by the DISCOM.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before Attempting!**
> 1. **Unit consistency:** Always convert kW ↔ W and kVAr ↔ VAr before plugging into formulas. Mixing units costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Angle conversion:** Compute $\phi = \cos^{-1}(pf)$ *first*, then take $\tan(\phi)$ *second*. Students frequently commit inverse-function errors.
> 3. **Three-phase vs. single-phase:** Clearly state whether the load and capacitor bank are 3-phase or 1-phase. The formula for $Q_C$ uses the *total* 3-phase P.
> 4. **Configuration matters:** Do not compute the delta capacitance and label it as star. Specify "per phase in delta".
> 5. **Final answer units:** Mention Farads (F), microfarads (µF), or kVAr explicitly. A bare number loses 0.5 mark.

### **Question B — Option A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

A 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz industrial plant has a connected load of **250 kW** operating at a **lagging power factor of 0.65**. The plant engineer wishes to install a delta-connected capacitor bank to improve the overall power factor to **0.95 lagging**.

**(a)** Calculate the required reactive power rating of the capacitor bank in kVAr. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Determine the capacitance of each capacitor in the delta bank and estimate the percentage reduction in line current and line losses after compensation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Part (a) — kVAr Rating (7 marks)**

Given: $P = 250$ kW, $V_L = 415$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $pf_1 = 0.65$, target $pf_2 = 0.95$.

Initial pf angle and target pf angle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi_1 &= \cos^{-1}(0.65) = 49.458^\circ \\
\phi_2 &= \cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.195^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

Required capacitor kVAr using the standard sizing formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_C &= P \bigl[ \tan(\phi_1) - \tan(\phi_2) \bigr] \\
    &= 250 \bigl[ \tan(49.458^\circ) - \tan(18.195^\circ) \bigr] \\
    &= 250 \bigl[ 1.1691 - 0.3287 \bigr] \\
    &= 250 \times 0.8404 \\
\therefore \quad Q_C &= 210.10 \text{ kVAr}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Mark split: angles — 2 marks; tangents — 2 marks; final kVAr — 3 marks.)*

> **Part (b) — Capacitance, Current and Loss Reduction (7 marks)**

Capacitance per phase (delta configuration):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= 2 \pi f = 2 \pi (50) = 314.159 \text{ rad/s} \\
C_\Delta &= \frac{Q_{C,3\phi}}{3 \, \omega \, V_L^{\,2}} = \frac{210.10 \times 10^3}{3 \times 314.159 \times (415)^2} \\
        &= \frac{210100}{3 \times 314.159 \times 172225} \\
        &= \frac{210100}{1.62326 \times 10^8} \\
C_\Delta &= 1.294 \times 10^{-3} \text{ F} = 1294 \text{ }\mu\text{F per phase}
\end{aligned}
$$

Line current before and after compensation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{L,1} &= \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \, pf_1} = \frac{250000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.65} = 534.10 \text{ A} \\
I_{L,2} &= \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \, pf_2} = \frac{250000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.95} = 365.45 \text{ A} \\
\text{Current reduction} &= \frac{534.10 - 365.45}{534.10} \times 100\% = 31.58\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Percentage reduction in $I^2R$ line losses:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Loss reduction} &= \left[1 - \left(\frac{pf_1}{pf_2}\right)^2\right] \times 100\% \\
                      &= \left[1 - \left(\frac{0.65}{0.95}\right)^2\right] \times 100\% \\
                      &= \left[1 - 0.4683\right] \times 100\% \\
                      &= 53.17\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Mark split: capacitance — 3 marks; currents & % reduction — 2 marks; loss reduction — 2 marks.)*

### **Question B — Option B (14 Marks — INTERNAL CHOICE)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

A 3-phase, 11 kV / 433 V step-down transformer supplies a star-connected inductive load of **800 kVA** at a **lagging power factor of 0.70**. A consulting engineer proposes a delta-connected capacitor bank at the LT bus to improve the pf to **0.92 lagging**.

**(a)** Compute the required kVAr rating of the bank and the per-phase capacitance in delta. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Calculate the line current drawn from the transformer LV side, before and after compensation. Comment on the transformer loading and percentage release of capacity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Part (a) — kVAr Rating and Capacitance (7 marks)**

Given: $S = 800$ kVA, $pf_1 = 0.70$, target $pf_2 = 0.92$, $V_L = 433$ V, $f = 50$ Hz.

Real power consumed by the load (constant before and after compensation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= S \cdot pf_1 = 800 \times 0.70 = 560 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

Angles:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi_1 &= \cos^{-1}(0.70) = 45.573^\circ \\
\phi_2 &= \cos^{-1}(0.92) = 23.074^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

Required capacitor kVAr:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_C &= P \bigl[ \tan(\phi_1) - \tan(\phi_2) \bigr] \\
    &= 560 \bigl[ \tan(45.573^\circ) - \tan(23.074^\circ) \bigr] \\
    &= 560 \bigl[ 1.0202 - 0.4251 \bigr] \\
    &= 560 \times 0.5951 \\
Q_C &= 333.26 \text{ kVAr}
\end{aligned}
$$

Per-phase capacitance in delta:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_\Delta &= \frac{Q_{C,3\phi}}{3 \, \omega \, V_L^{\,2}} = \frac{333.26 \times 10^3}{3 \times 314.159 \times (433)^2} \\
        &= \frac{333260}{3 \times 314.159 \times 187489} \\
        &= \frac{333260}{1.76679 \times 10^8} \\
C_\Delta &= 1.886 \times 10^{-3} \text{ F} \approx 1886 \text{ }\mu\text{F per phase}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Part (b) — Line Current, Loading & Released Capacity (7 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{L,1} &= \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L} = \frac{800000}{\sqrt{3} \times 433} = 1066.7 \text{ A} \\
I_{L,2} &= \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \, pf_2} = \frac{560000}{\sqrt{3} \times 433 \times 0.92} = 811.5 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

Transformer loading (nameplate 800 kVA):

* Before PFC: $I_{L,1} = 1066.7$ A ⇒ transformer is fully loaded at 100 % of rating.
* After PFC: apparent power seen by transformer:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_2 &= \frac{P}{pf_2} = \frac{560}{0.92} = 608.70 \text{ kVA}
\end{aligned}
$$

* New loading = $(608.70 / 800) \times 100\% = 76.09\%$.
* **Released capacity = 800 − 608.70 = 191.30 kVA**, i.e., the transformer can now serve an additional 191 kVA of load without being physically upgraded.

*(Mark split: line currents — 3 marks; apparent power & loading — 2 marks; released capacity interpretation — 2 marks.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must memorize for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE**
>
> * **Power Factor** is **$pf = \cos(\phi) = P / S$**, dimensionless, always between 0 and 1 for linear loads.
> * **P (real)** is in **kW**; **Q (reactive)** in **kVAr**; **S (apparent)** in **kVA**. The triangle obeys $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$.
> * **Lagging pf** = inductive load (current lags voltage) — needs capacitive compensation.
> * **Leading pf** = capacitive load (current leads voltage) — over-compensation is harmful (causes over-voltage at the bus).
> * **Master Formula — the one-line equation tested every year:** $\boxed{Q_C = P \bigl[ \tan(\phi_1) - \tan(\phi_2) \bigr]}$
> * **Delta connection is preferred for LT capacitor banks** because it requires only **one-third** the capacitance of a star bank for the same kVAr.
> * **Per-phase capacitance (delta):** $C_\Delta = Q_{C,3\phi} / (3 \omega V_L^{\,2})$.
> * **Per-phase capacitance (star):** $C_Y = Q_{C,3\phi} / (\sqrt{3} \, \omega V_L^{\,2})$.
> * **Current reduction:** $\Delta I_L = (P / \sqrt{3} V_L)(1/pf_1 - 1/pf_2)$.
> * **Loss reduction:** $\text{Loss saved} = 1 - (pf_1 / pf_2)^2$ (as a fraction).
> * **Indian utility target pf:** ≥ **0.95** (incentive zone), with **0.90** as the penalty threshold.
> * **Detuning reactor (typically 7 %):** mandatory when THD of voltage > 5 % or when large non-linear loads (VFDs, SMPS) are present, to avoid parallel resonance at 5th/7th harmonics.
> * **APFC panel** uses a microprocessor **pf relay** and multi-stage **capacitor contactors** to switch in 5/10/20/40 kVAr steps to maintain target pf dynamically.
> * **Simulation tip:** In MATLAB/Simulink, use **Three-Phase Source + Series R-L Load + Three-Phase Breaker (Switched Capacitor) + Scope**; set the *Powergui* FFT tool to validate THD after compensation.
> * **Caution against over-compensation:** Installing a capacitor bank larger than $Q_C$ pushes the system into the **leading pf region** (capacitive), which is also penalized and may cause self-excitation of synchronous generators or ferroresonance in transformers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Short Circuit Analysis - Symmetrical and Unsymmetrical Faults
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL607 - ELECTRICAL SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Short Circuit Analysis - Symmetrical and Unsymmetrical Faults

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Short Circuit Analysis** is the systematic process of determining the magnitudes of currents and voltages throughout an electrical power system immediately following the occurrence of a fault (abnormal connection of very low impedance between two points of different potentials or between a conductor and ground).

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme Power Systems syllabus**, short circuit analysis is fundamental for:
- **Selection of protective switchgear** (circuit breakers, relays)
- **Setting of relay coordination** parameters
- **Mechanical design** of busbars, support insulators, and conductors
- **Verification of equipment thermal and dynamic short-circuit withstand ratings**

### Classification of Faults

| S.No | Fault Type | Symmetry | Frequency of Occurrence |
|------|-----------|----------|------------------------|
| 1 | Three-Phase (L-L-L) | **Symmetrical** | 5% (Rare) |
| 2 | Single Line-to-Ground (L-G) | **Unsymmetrical** | 70% (Most Common) |
| 3 | Line-to-Line (L-L) | **Unsymmetrical** | 15% |
| 4 | Double Line-to-Ground (L-L-G) | **Unsymmetrical** | 10% |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Symmetrical Fault (3-Phase)** is rare but most severe. The **Single Line-to-Ground (L-G) Fault** is the most frequently occurring fault, typically caused by lightning, tree contact, or insulation failure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **multi-story water distribution system** where water flows through pipes under high pressure:
- **Normal Operation**: Water flows in a controlled, balanced manner through all three pipes (phases).
- **Short Circuit (Fault)**: Suddenly, a massive crack appears in one pipe, and water (current) gushes out uncontrollably.
  - If all three pipes crack equally → **Symmetrical Fault**
  - If only one pipe cracks or touches the building wall (ground) → **Unsymmetrical Fault**

**The "Fault Current" is like the water rushing through the crack** — it can be tens of thousands of Amperes, far exceeding normal operating current of hundreds of Amperes. We need to predict this worst-case flow to design **safety valves (circuit breakers)** that can shut off the system before pipes burst (equipment damage).

## 1.3 Key Standard Quantities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Base Values in Fault Analysis:**
> - **Base MVA (S_base)**: Typically **100 MVA** (standard reference)
> - **Base Voltage (V_base)**: Rated line-to-line voltage in **kV**
> - **Base Current (I_base)**: $\frac{S_{base}}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{base}}$ in **kA**
> - **Base Impedance (Z_base)**: $\frac{(V_{base})^2}{S_{base}}$ in **Ω**
> - **System Frequency**: **50 Hz** (India Standard - IS Standard)

## 1.4 The Per-Unit System

The **Per-Unit (pu) System** normalizes all system quantities to a common base, making calculations across different voltage levels extremely convenient.

$$ \text{Per-Unit Value} = \frac{\text{Actual Value}}{\text{Base Value}} $$

**Advantages of Per-Unit System:**
- Eliminates transformer voltage ratios in impedance calculations
- Direct comparison of impedances across different voltage levels
- Simplifies three-phase calculations into single-phase analysis
- Identical per-unit values on either side of a transformer
- Easier identification of abnormal operating conditions
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Symmetrical Components Theory (Fortescue's Theorem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fortescue's Theorem (1918)**: Any set of **three unbalanced phasors** (unsymmetrical fault currents or voltages) can be resolved into **three sets of balanced phasors** called **Symmetrical Components**.

The three components are:

| Component | Symbol | Description | Sequence |
|-----------|--------|-------------|----------|
| Positive Sequence | $I_{a1}, I_{b1}, I_{c1}$ | Three phasors equal magnitude, **120° apart** in phase sequence **a-b-c** | 1 |
| Negative Sequence | $I_{a2}, I_{b2}, I_{c2}$ | Three phasors equal magnitude, **120° apart** in phase sequence **a-c-b** | 2 |
| Zero Sequence | $I_{a0}, I_{b0}, I_{c0}$ | Three phasors equal magnitude, **0° apart** (in phase) | 0 |

### Mathematical Transformation

The relationship between phase quantities and symmetrical components is given by:

$$ \begin{bmatrix} I_a \\ I_b \\ I_c \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & a^2 & a \\ 1 & a & a^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_{a0} \\ I_{a1} \\ I_{a2} \end{bmatrix} $$

Where the operator $a = 1\angle 120° = -0.5 + j0.866$ and $a^2 = 1\angle 240° = -0.5 - j0.866$.

**Key Property**: $1 + a + a^2 = 0$ and $a^3 = 1$

## 2.2 Sequence Networks

Each sequence has its own Thevenin equivalent network seen from the fault point:

### Positive Sequence Network
- Represents balanced system under normal operation
- Includes generators, transformers, transmission lines
- All rotating machines contribute their **subtransient reactance** $X_d''$

### Negative Sequence Network
- Similar topology to positive sequence
- Generators contribute **negative sequence reactance** $X_2 \approx X_d''$
- No EMF sources (only impedances)

### Zero Sequence Network
- **Significantly different** from positive sequence network
- Returns through neutral and ground path
- Depends heavily on **transformer winding connections** (Star-Delta, Star-Star with neutral grounding)
- Generators contribute $X_0$ (typically much larger than $X_1$)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET — Master these equations for KTU examinations**

| S.No | Formula | Description | Units |
|------|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $I_{base} = \frac{S_{base}}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{base}}$ | Base current calculation | kA |
| 2 | $Z_{base} = \frac{(V_{base,L-L})^2}{S_{base}}$ | Base impedance (line-to-line) | Ω |
| 3 | $Z_{pu} = \frac{Z_{actual}}{Z_{base}}$ | Per-unit conversion | pu |
| 4 | $Z_{pu,new} = Z_{pu,old} \times \frac{S_{base,new}}{S_{base,old}} \times \left(\frac{V_{base,old}}{V_{base,new}}\right)^2$ | Change of base | pu |
| 5 | $I_f^{(3)} = \frac{E_g}{X_{1,eq}} = \frac{V_f}{Z_{1,th}}$ | 3-Phase Symmetrical Fault Current | pu |
| 6 | $I_{f1}^{(L-G)} = \frac{3 \times E_a}{X_1 + X_2 + X_0}$ | SLG Fault Positive Sequence Current | pu |
| 7 | $I_f^{(L-G)} = 3 \times I_{f1}^{(L-G)}$ | SLG Fault Total Current | pu |
| 8 | $I_{f1}^{(L-L)} = \frac{E_a}{X_1 + X_2}$ | L-L Fault Positive Sequence Current | pu |
| 9 | $I_f^{(L-L)} = \sqrt{3} \times I_{f1}^{(L-L)}$ | L-L Fault Total Current | pu |
| 10 | $I_{f1}^{(L-L-G)} = \frac{E_a}{X_1 + (X_2 \parallel X_0)}$ | LLG Fault Positive Sequence Current | pu |
| 11 | $V_{a1} = V_{a2} = V_{a0} = \frac{E_a \times (X_2 + X_0)}{X_1 + X_2 + X_0}$ | SLG Fault Sequence Voltages | pu |
| 12 | $V_{a1} = E_a - I_{a1} X_1$ | Positive Sequence Voltage during fault | pu |

## 2.4 Sequence Network Interconnections (KTU Critical Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THE GOLDEN RULE**: The interconnection of sequence networks depends **entirely on the type of fault**.

| Fault Type | Sequence Network Connection | Boundary Conditions |
|------------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| **3-Phase (L-L-L)** | Only Positive Sequence Network | $V_a = 0$, $I_b = I_c = 0$ |
| **Single Line-to-Ground (L-G)** | All Three in **Series** | $V_a = 0$, $I_b = I_c = 0$ |
| **Line-to-Line (L-L)** | Positive & Negative **Parallel** | $V_a = V_b$, $I_a = 0$, $I_c = -I_b$ |
| **Double Line-to-Ground (L-L-G)** | All Three **Parallel** | $V_b = V_c = 0$, $I_a = 0$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power System Protection Engineers**: Use these calculations to coordinate overcurrent relays
- **Substation Design**: Determines required **breaking capacity** of circuit breakers
- **Equipment Manufacturing**: Generators, transformers must withstand **short circuit MVA** (SCMVA = $\sqrt{3} \times V_{base} \times I_{f,sc}$)
- **Grid Stability Studies**: Predicts post-fault voltage recovery
- **Arc Flash Analysis**: Calculates incident energy for personnel safety

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Symmetrical 3-Phase Fault Analysis

**Problem Setup**: A synchronous generator with internal EMF $E_g$ behind subtransient reactance $X_d''$ feeds a fault bus through a system equivalent reactance $X_{sys}$. A bolted 3-phase fault occurs at the fault point F.

**Step 1**: Thevenin Equivalent at the fault point

The positive sequence network reduces to a single Thevenin source:
$$ E_{th} = V_{f(0)} = 1.0 \angle 0° \text{ pu (pre-fault voltage)} $$

$$ X_{1,th} = X_d'' + X_{sys} $$

**Step 2**: Apply Fault Boundary Condition

For a bolted 3-phase fault, the boundary condition is:
$$ V_a = V_b = V_c = 0 $$

This means all three phase voltages are zero, but the system remains balanced (symmetrical fault). Only the **positive sequence** network is involved.

**Step 3**: Calculate Fault Current

By Ohm's Law on the positive sequence network:
$$ I_{a1} = \frac{E_{a}}{X_{1,th}} = \frac{1.0 \angle 0°}{X_{1,th}} $$

Since the system is balanced, $I_b$ and $I_c$ have the same magnitude with 120° phase shift:
$$ I_b = a^2 I_a, \quad I_c = a I_a $$

**Step 4**: Actual Fault Current Magnitude

The total fault current in phase 'a' is:
$$ I_{f}^{(3)} = \frac{V_{base}}{X_{1,th}} \text{ (in Amperes)} $$

Or in per-unit:
$$ I_{f,pu}^{(3)} = \frac{1.0}{X_{1,th,pu}} $$

## 3.2 Derivation: Single Line-to-Ground (L-G) Fault

**Setup**: Phase 'a' is faulted to ground at bus F.

**Step 1: Boundary Conditions**

By definition of an L-G fault on phase 'a':
$$ V_a = 0, \quad I_b = 0, \quad I_c = 0 $$

**Step 2: Apply Symmetrical Component Transformation**

From the inverse transformation:
$$ V_a = V_{a0} + V_{a1} + V_{a2} = 0 $$

$$ I_a = I_{a0} + I_{a1} + I_{a2} $$

$$ I_b = I_{a0} + a^2 I_{a1} + a I_{a2} = 0 $$

$$ I_c = I_{a0} + a I_{a1} + a^2 I_{a2} = 0 $$

**Step 3: Solve for Sequence Currents**

Adding the last two equations:
$$ 2 I_{a0} + (a + a^2) I_{a1} + (a + a^2) I_{a2} = 0 $$

Since $a + a^2 = -1$:
$$ 2 I_{a0} - I_{a1} - I_{a2} = 0 $$

Subtracting the equations:
$$ (a^2 - a) I_{a1} + (a - a^2) I_{a2} = 0 $$

$$ I_{a1} = I_{a2} $$

Substituting back:
$$ I_{a0} = I_{a1} = I_{a2} $$

**Step 4: Determine Network Connection**

Since $I_{a0} = I_{a1} = I_{a2}$ and $V_{a0} + V_{a1} + V_{a2} = 0$, the three sequence networks must be connected in **SERIES**.

**Step 5: Calculate Positive Sequence Current**

Applying KVL across the series combination:
$$ E_a - I_{a1}(X_1 + X_2 + X_0) = 0 $$

$$ I_{a1} = \frac{E_a}{X_1 + X_2 + X_0} $$

**Step 6: Calculate Total Fault Current**

Since $I_a = 3 I_{a1}$:
$$ I_f^{(L-G)} = \frac{3 E_a}{X_1 + X_2 + X_0} $$

In actual units:
$$ I_f^{(L-G)} = \frac{3 V_{base}}{X_1 + X_2 + X_0} \text{ Amperes} $$

## 3.3 Derivation: Line-to-Line (L-L) Fault

**Setup**: Phases 'b' and 'c' are shorted at the fault point F (no ground connection).

**Step 1: Boundary Conditions**

$$ I_a = 0, \quad I_b + I_c = 0, \quad V_b = V_c $$

**Step 2: Convert to Sequence Components**

$$ I_a = I_{a0} + I_{a1} + I_{a2} = 0 $$

$$ V_b = V_c \implies V_{a0} + a^2 V_{a1} + a V_{a2} = V_{a0} + a V_{a1} + a^2 V_{a2} $$

Simplifying: $V_{a1}(a^2 - a) = V_{a2}(a^2 - a)$, hence $V_{a1} = V_{a2}$.

**Step 3: Determine Network Connection**

Since $V_{a1} = V_{a2}$ and no zero sequence current flows ($I_{a0} = 0$), the **positive and negative** sequence networks are connected in **PARALLEL**.

**Step 4: Calculate Fault Current**

$$ I_{a1} = \frac{E_a}{X_1 + X_2} $$

The phase 'b' and 'c' currents are:
$$ I_b = -I_c = -j\sqrt{3} I_{a1} $$

So the magnitude of the line-to-line fault current is:
$$ \vert I_f^{(L-L)} \vert = \sqrt{3} \times \vert I_{a1} \vert = \frac{\sqrt{3} \times E_a}{X_1 + X_2} $$

## 3.4 Derivation: Double Line-to-Ground (L-L-G) Fault

**Setup**: Phases 'b' and 'c' are shorted to ground at the fault point F.

**Step 1: Boundary Conditions**

$$ I_a = 0, \quad V_b = 0, \quad V_c = 0 $$

**Step 2: Convert to Sequence Components**

$$ I_{a0} + I_{a1} + I_{a2} = 0 $$

$$ V_b = V_{a0} + a^2 V_{a1} + a V_{a2} = 0 $$

$$ V_c = V_{a0} + a V_{a1} + a^2 V_{a2} = 0 $$

**Step 3: Solve for Sequence Voltages**

Adding the two voltage equations:
$$ 2V_{a0} + (a+a^2)(V_{a1} + V_{a2}) = 0 $$

$$ 2V_{a0} = V_{a1} + V_{a2} \implies V_{a0} = \frac{V_{a1} + V_{a2}}{2} $$

From either voltage equation: $V_{a1} = V_{a2} = V_{a0}$

**Step 4: Determine Network Connection**

Since $V_{a1} = V_{a2} = V_{a0}$ and $I_{a0} + I_{a1} + I_{a2} = 0$, all three sequence networks are connected in **PARALLEL**.

**Step 5: Calculate Fault Current**

$$ I_{a1} = \frac{E_a}{X_1 + (X_2 \parallel X_0)} = \frac{E_a (X_2 + X_0)}{X_1 X_2 + X_1 X_0 + X_2 X_0} $$

$$ I_{a2} = -I_{a1} \times \frac{X_0}{X_2 + X_0} $$

$$ I_{a0} = -I_{a1} \times \frac{X_2}{X_2 + X_0} $$

The phase 'b' current is:
$$ I_b = a^2 I_{a1} + a I_{a2} + I_{a0} $$

## 3.5 Comprehensive Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem**: A 50 MVA, 11 kV synchronous generator has $X_d'' = 0.15$ pu, $X_2 = 0.15$ pu, $X_0 = 0.05$ pu. The generator is connected through a transformer (X = 0.10 pu) to a fault bus. Calculate fault currents for all four fault types. Take $E_g = 1.0$ pu.

**Solution Setup**:

Base values: $S_{base} = 50$ MVA, $V_{base} = 11$ kV

$$ I_{base} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 11 \times 10^3} = 2624.3 \text{ A} $$

Sequence reactances (taking transformer into account):
$$ X_1 = 0.15 + 0.10 = 0.25 \text{ pu} $$

$$ X_2 = 0.15 + 0.10 = 0.25 \text{ pu} $$

$$ X_0 = 0.05 + 0.10 = 0.15 \text{ pu} $$

**Fault Current Calculations**:

**(a) 3-Phase Symmetrical Fault**:
$$ I_{f1}^{(3)} = \frac{1.0}{0.25} = 4.0 \text{ pu} = 4.0 \times 2624.3 = 10497.2 \text{ A} $$

**(b) Single Line-to-Ground Fault**:
$$ I_{f1}^{(L-G)} = \frac{3 \times 1.0}{0.25 + 0.25 + 0.15} = \frac{3.0}{0.65} = 4.615 \text{ pu} $$

$$ I_f^{(L-G)} = 4.615 \times 2624.3 = 12112.1 \text{ A} $$

**(c) Line-to-Line Fault**:
$$ I_{f1}^{(L-L)} = \frac{1.0}{0.25 + 0.25} = 2.0 \text{ pu} $$

$$ \vert I_f^{(L-L)} \vert = \sqrt{3} \times 2.0 = 3.464 \text{ pu} = 9090.6 \text{ A} $$

**(d) Double Line-to-Ground Fault**:
$$ I_{f1}^{(L-L-G)} = \frac{1.0}{0.25 + (0.25 \parallel 0.15)} = \frac{1.0}{0.25 + 0.09375} = \frac{1.0}{0.34375} = 2.909 \text{ pu} $$

The fault currents follow the typical KTU relationship:
$$ I_f^{(L-G)} > I_f^{(3)} > I_f^{(L-L-G)} > I_f^{(L-L)} $$
$$ 12112.1 > 10497.2 > 7634.5 > 9090.6 \text{ A} $$
