# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define symmetrical and unsymmetrical faults. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

A **symmetrical fault** is a fault that results in symmetrical (balanced) short circuit currents displaced 120° apart in all three phases, although they may be unequal in magnitude to the pre-fault condition. The system remains balanced even after the fault, so analysis can be performed on a single-phase basis.

**Example**: Three-phase to ground fault (L-L-L-G), Three-phase fault (L-L-L)

An **unsymmetrical fault** is a fault that results in unbalanced phase currents and voltages, requiring the use of symmetrical components for analysis.

**Examples**: Single line-to-ground fault (L-G), Line-to-line fault (L-L), Double line-to-ground fault (L-L-G)

*(Award 1 mark for each definition, 0.5 marks for each example — Total 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State Fortescue's theorem. What are the three symmetrical components?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

**Fortescue's Theorem (1918)**: Any set of three unbalanced phasors (of a three-phase system) can be resolved into three sets of balanced phasors, called symmetrical components.

The three symmetrical components are:
1. **Positive Sequence Components** ($I_{a1}, I_{b1}, I_{c1}$): Three phasors of equal magnitude, displaced by 120° in phase, having the same phase sequence as the original phasors (a-b-c).
2. **Negative Sequence Components** ($I_{a2}, I_{b2}, I_{c2}$): Three phasors of equal magnitude, displaced by 120°, with reverse phase sequence (a-c-b).
3. **Zero Sequence Components** ($I_{a0}, I_{b0}, I_{c0}$): Three phasors of equal magnitude, all in phase (zero phase displacement).

*(Award 1 mark for theorem, 2 marks for the three components — Total 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Modified Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat phasor diagram, derive the expressions for sequence components of an unsymmetrical three-phase system in terms of phase quantities.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 3-phase, 25 MVA, 11 kV generator has positive, negative and zero sequence reactances of 0.2, 0.2 and 0.08 pu respectively. A single line-to-ground fault occurs at the terminals of the generator. Determine the fault current and the line-to-line voltages during the fault. Assume the generator neutral is solidly grounded.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) - 7 Marks Solution:**

The phase currents $I_a, I_b, I_c$ can be expressed as the sum of their symmetrical components:

$$ I_a = I_{a0} + I_{a1} + I_{a2} $$

$$ I_b = I_{b0} + I_{b1} + I_{b2} $$

$$ I_c = I_{c0} + I_{c1} + I_{c2} $$

Where the phase 'b' components lag phase 'a' by 120° for positive sequence and lead by 120° for negative sequence:
$$ I_{b1} = a^2 I_{a1}, \quad I_{c1} = a I_{a1} $$
$$ I_{b2} = a I_{a2}, \quad I_{c2} = a^2 I_{a2} $$
$$ I_{a0} = I_{b0} = I_{c0} $$

Substituting and using the operator $a = 1\angle 120°$:

$$ I_a = I_{a0} + I_{a1} + I_{a2} $$
$$ I_b = I_{a0} + a^2 I_{a1} + a I_{a2} $$
$$ I_c = I_{a0} + a I_{a1} + a^2 I_{a2} $$

In matrix form:
$$ \begin{bmatrix} I_a \\ I_b \\ I_c \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & a^2 & a \\ 1 & a & a^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_{a0} \\ I_{a1} \\ I_{a2} \end{bmatrix} $$

Inverting the matrix (using $[A]^{-1} = \frac{1}{3}[A]^*$):
$$ I_{a0} = \frac{1}{3}(I_a + I_b + I_c) $$
$$ I_{a1} = \frac{1}{3}(I_a + a I_b + a^2 I_c) $$
$$ I_{a2} = \frac{1}{3}(I_a + a^2 I_b + a I_c) $$

**Part (b) - 7 Marks Solution:**

**Given Data**:
- $S = 25$ MVA, $V_L = 11$ kV
- $X_1 = 0.2$ pu, $X_2 = 0.2$ pu, $X_0 = 0.08$ pu
- $E_g = 1.0$ pu (assumed pre-fault voltage)
- Solidly grounded neutral → zero sequence path exists

**Step 1: Base Current**
$$ I_{base} = \frac{25 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 11 \times 10^3} = 1312.16 \text{ A} $$

**Step 2: Sequence Network Connection for L-G Fault**
For L-G fault, all three sequence networks are in series.

**Step 3: Positive Sequence Fault Current**
$$ I_{a1} = \frac{E_a}{X_1 + X_2 + X_0} = \frac{1.0}{0.2 + 0.2 + 0.08} = \frac{1.0}{0.48} = 2.0833 \text{ pu} $$

**Step 4: Total Fault Current**
$$ I_{a0} = I_{a1} = I_{a2} = 2.0833 \text{ pu} $$
$$ I_f = 3 I_{a1} = 3 \times 2.0833 = 6.25 \text{ pu} $$
$$ I_f = 6.25 \times 1312.16 = 8201 \text{ A} \approx 8.2 \text{ kA} $$

**Step 5: Sequence Voltages**
$$ V_{a1} = E_a - I_{a1} X_1 = 1.0 - 2.0833 \times 0.2 = 1.0 - 0.4167 = 0.5833 \text{ pu} $$
$$ V_{a2} = -I_{a2} X_2 = -2.0833 \times 0.2 = -0.4167 \text{ pu} $$
$$ V_{a0} = -I_{a0} X_0 = -2.0833 \times 0.08 = -0.1667 \text{ pu} $$

Verification: $V_{a1} + V_{a2} + V_{a0} = 0.5833 - 0.4167 - 0.1667 = 0.0001 \approx 0$ ✓

**Step 6: Line-to-Line Voltages**
$$ V_{ab} = V_{a1}(1 - a^2) + V_{a2}(1 - a) = j\sqrt{3}(V_{a1} - V_{a2}) $$
$$ \vert V_{ab} \vert = \sqrt{3} \times \vert V_{a1} - V_{a2} \vert = \sqrt{3} \times (0.5833 + 0.4167) = \sqrt{3} \times 1.0 = 1.732 \text{ pu} $$
$$ \vert V_{ab} \vert = 1.732 \times 11 = 19.05 \text{ kV} $$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** For a 3-phase, 50 MVA, 6.6 kV alternator with $X_1 = 0.2$ pu, $X_2 = 0.15$ pu, $X_0 = 0.05$ pu, calculate the fault currents for the following faults at the generator terminals:
(i) Three-phase fault
(ii) Line-to-line fault

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketch the sequence network connections for all four types of shunt faults in a power system.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) - 7 Marks Solution:**

**Given Data**:
- $S = 50$ MVA, $V_L = 6.6$ kV
- $X_1 = 0.2$ pu, $X_2 = 0.15$ pu, $X_0 = 0.05$ pu
- $E_g = 1.0$ pu

**Base Current**:
$$ I_{base} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 6.6 \times 10^3} = 4373.8 \text{ A} \approx 4.37 \text{ kA} $$

**(i) Three-Phase Fault Current**:
Only positive sequence network is involved:
$$ I_f^{(3)} = \frac{E_a}{X_1} = \frac{1.0}{0.2} = 5.0 \text{ pu} $$
$$ I_f^{(3)} = 5.0 \times 4373.8 = 21869 \text{ A} \approx 21.87 \text{ kA} $$

**(ii) Line-to-Line Fault Current**:
Positive and negative sequence networks in parallel:
$$ I_{a1} = \frac{E_a}{X_1 + X_2} = \frac{1.0}{0.2 + 0.15} = \frac{1.0}{0.35} = 2.857 \text{ pu} $$
$$ I_{a2} = -I_{a1} = -2.857 \text{ pu} $$
$$ I_{a0} = 0 $$

The line-to-line fault current magnitude:
$$ \vert I_f^{(L-L)} \vert = \sqrt{3} \times \vert I_{a1} \vert = \sqrt{3} \times 2.857 = 4.949 \text{ pu} $$
$$ I_f^{(L-L)} = 4.949 \times 4373.8 = 21644 \text{ A} \approx 21.64 \text{ kA} $$

**Part (b) - 7 Marks Solution:**

| Fault Type | Sketch Description | Connection Type |
|------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **3-Phase Fault** | Only positive sequence network with EMF $E_a$ in series with $X_1$, shorted at fault point | **Single Network** |
| **L-G Fault** | All three sequence networks ($X_1, X_2, X_0$) connected in **series** with $E_a$ across the combination, terminated at fault point | **Series Connection** |
| **L-L Fault** | Positive and negative sequence networks ($X_1$ and $X_2$) connected in **parallel**, with $E_a$ in positive sequence branch only | **Parallel (P-N)** |
| **L-L-G Fault** | All three sequence networks ($X_1, X_2, X_0$) connected in **parallel**, with $E_a$ only in positive sequence branch | **Parallel (All)** |

**Key Boundary Conditions Summary**:

$$ V_a = V_b = V_c = 0 \quad \text{(3-Phase)} $$

$$ V_a = 0, \quad I_b = I_c = 0 \quad \text{(L-G)} $$

$$ I_a = 0, \quad I_b = -I_c, \quad V_b = V_c \quad \text{(L-L)} $$

$$ I_a = 0, \quad V_b = V_c = 0 \quad \text{(L-L-G)} $$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS (KTU BOARD EXAMINATION PITFALLS)**
>
> **1. Per-Unit Conversion Errors (Most Frequent — loses 1-2 marks)**:
> - Forgetting to convert base MVA when equipment ratings differ from system base
> - Mixing single-phase and three-phase base values
> - **FIX**: Always use $S_{base}$ and $V_{base, L-L}$ consistently
>
> **2. Sequence Network Interconnection Mistakes (Critical — loses 3-4 marks)**:
> - Wrong connection for L-G vs L-L-G (SERIES vs PARALLEL confusion)
> - Forgetting to use all three networks for L-G and L-L-G
> - **FIX**: Memorize the boundary conditions and derive from them
>
> **3. Operator 'a' Arithmetic Errors**:
> - $a^2 = a^*$ (complex conjugate) is often confused
> - $1 + a + a^2 = 0$ property forgotten
> - **FIX**: $a = -0.5 + j0.866$, $a^2 = -0.5 - j0.866$
>
> **4. Transformer Zero Sequence Path Confusion (loses 2-3 marks)**:
> - Star-Star transformer with both neutrals grounded → $X_0$ appears
> - Delta winding → $X_0$ does NOT pass through (blocked)
> - **FIX**: Carefully check transformer winding connections for zero sequence paths
>
> **5. Final Answer Without Units**:
> - Always state final answer with units (kA, pu, MVA)
> - Show the conversion: $I_{actual} = I_{pu} \times I_{base}$

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Short Circuit Analysis**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ **Fault Classification**: Symmetrical (3-Phase) vs Unsymmetrical (L-G, L-L, L-L-G)
- ✅ **Fortescue's Theorem**: Foundation of symmetrical components
- ✅ **Per-Unit System**: Universal approach for multi-voltage networks
- ✅ **Thevenin Equivalent**: Foundation of fault current calculation

### High-Yield Formulas
- ✅ **3-Phase Fault**: $I_f = E/X_1$
- ✅ **L-G Fault**: $I_f = 3E/(X_1+X_2+X_0)$ — Networks in SERIES
- ✅ **L-L Fault**: $I_f = \sqrt{3}E/(X_1+X_2)$ — P-N Networks in PARALLEL
- ✅ **L-L-G Fault**: $I_f = \sqrt{3} \cdot E/[X_1+(X_2 \parallel X_0)]$ — All three in PARALLEL

### Critical Numerical Conversions
- ✅ $I_{base} = S_{base}/(\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{base})$
- ✅ $Z_{base} = V_{base}^2/S_{base}$
- ✅ Change of base formula must be applied when component MVA differs from system MVA

### Practical Engineering Insights
- ✅ **L-G faults are most common** (70% of all faults)
- ✅ **3-Phase faults are most severe** for circuit breaker rating
- ✅ **Zero sequence network depends on grounding** and transformer connections
- ✅ **Subtransient reactance** $X_d''$ is used for circuit breaker selection
- ✅ **Transient reactance** $X_d'$ is used for relay coordination timing

### Simulation Lab Notes
- ✅ Use **MATLAB Simulink** Simscape Electrical or **PSCAD** for fault simulation
- ✅ Set fault resistance = 0.001 Ω for bolted fault simulation
- ✅ Apply fault at t = 0.05 s and observe waveform for first 0.2 s
- ✅ Compare simulation results with analytical calculations (error < 5%)

### KTU Exam Strategy
- ✅ Always start with **base value calculation**
- ✅ Draw **sequence network** with proper labeling
- ✅ State **boundary conditions** before interconnection
- ✅ Show **step-by-step derivation** with units throughout
- ✅ Verify answer: $V_{a0} + V_{a1} + V_{a2} = V_a$ (should be 0 at faulted phase)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission Line Modelling (Basic Programming): ABCD Constants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL607 - ELECTRICAL SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Transmission Line Modelling (Basic Programming): ABCD Constants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generalized Circuit Constants (ABCD Parameters):**
> ABCD constants are the **generalized two-port network parameters** used to characterize a transmission line by relating the **sending-end voltage and current** to the **receiving-end voltage and current** through a set of four complex constants: $A$, $B$, $C$, and $D$. Mathematically, the line is modelled as a linear, passive, bilateral, two-port "black box" represented by the matrix equation:
> $$\begin{bmatrix} V_S \\ I_S \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_R \\ I_R \end{bmatrix}$$

The two governing scalar equations are:
$$V_S = A \cdot V_R + B \cdot I_R$$
$$I_S = C \cdot V_R + D \cdot I_R$$

Where:
- $V_S$ = Sending-end voltage (phase voltage, in Volts, V)
- $I_S$ = Sending-end current (phase current, in Amperes, A)
- $V_R$ = Receiving-end voltage (in Volts, V)
- $I_R$ = Receiving-end current (in Amperes, A)
- **$A$** = dimensionless voltage-ratio constant
- **$B$** = transfer impedance (in Ohms, $\Omega$)
- **$C$** = transfer admittance (in Siemens, S)
- **$D$** = dimensionless current-ratio constant

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Fundamental Properties of ABCD Constants (must be memorised):**
> 1. **Reciprocity Condition:** $AD - BC = 1$ — always valid for linear, passive, bilateral networks.
> 2. **Symmetry Condition:** $A = D$ — valid only for symmetrical networks (e.g., a uniform transmission line).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Universal Adaptor Pipe"

Imagine an **opaque water pipe** connecting a high-pressure water pump (sending end) to a city distribution tank (receiving end). You cannot see what is happening inside the pipe (it could be straight, bent, have a constriction, or a T-junction). However, the manufacturer has provided you with **four stamped numbers** $(A, B, C, D)$ on a nameplate.

- If you know the pressure ($V_R$) and flow ($I_R$) at the city tank, you can **predict** the exact pressure and flow required at the pump side by simply multiplying with these four numbers.
- The pipe works "backwards" too (tank to pump) because the **reciprocity condition** $AD - BC = 1$ holds for passive systems.
- A perfectly straight, uniform pipe of any length will always satisfy $A = D$ (symmetry).

These four numbers are the **ABCD constants** of the transmission line — they completely describe the line's external electrical behaviour, regardless of its internal physical construction (short, medium, long, T-model, or $\pi$-model).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Frequency** used in India for power transmission: $f = \mathbf{50 \text{ Hz}}$.
- **Angular frequency:** $\omega = 2\pi f = \mathbf{314.159 \text{ rad/s}}$.
- **Speed of light (vacuum):** $c \approx \mathbf{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}$.
- **Line classification (length-based):**
  - Short line: length $<\mathbf{80 \text{ km}}$
  - Medium line: $\mathbf{80 \text{ km} \le \text{length} \le 250 \text{ km}}$
  - Long line: length $>\mathbf{250 \text{ km}}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The classification of a transmission line dictates which model is used to compute its ABCD constants:

### Step 1 — Identify Line Length and Choose a Model

- **Short Line Model:** The shunt capacitance is negligible; the line is represented by a **lumped series impedance $Z = R + jX$** only.
- **Medium Line Model:** The shunt capacitance is **lumped** either at the centre (Nominal **T-model**) or split at both ends (Nominal **$\pi$-model**).
- **Long Line Model:** Parameters are **uniformly distributed** along the line, leading to **hyperbolic** (exponential) functions.

### Step 2 — Apply the Model's Circuit Equations

For each model, write the **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** and **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at the relevant nodes, and solve for the constants $A, B, C, D$ in terms of the line's total series impedance $Z$ and total shunt admittance $Y$.

### Step 3 — Verify the Reciprocity & Symmetry Conditions

After computing, always check that $AD - BC = 1$. For medium and long lines, $A = D$ is also expected because the network is symmetrical.

### Step 4 — Apply ABCD to Predict Sending-End Quantities

Once the matrix is known, the **Voltage Regulation**, **Transmission Efficiency**, and **Sending-End Power Factor** of the line can all be determined.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving any ABCD-related KTU problem. Memorise the entries marked in bold.

| **Line Model** | **Constant $A$** | **Constant $B$** | **Constant $C$** | **Constant $D$** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Short Line** | $\mathbf{1}$ | $\mathbf{Z}$ | $\mathbf{0}$ | $\mathbf{1}$ |
| **Medium Line (Nominal T)** | $\mathbf{1 + \tfrac{YZ}{2}}$ | $\mathbf{Z\!\left(1 + \tfrac{YZ}{4}\right)}$ | $\mathbf{Y}$ | $\mathbf{1 + \tfrac{YZ}{2}}$ |
| **Medium Line (Nominal $\pi$)** | $\mathbf{1 + \tfrac{YZ}{2}}$ | $\mathbf{Z}$ | $\mathbf{Y\!\left(1 + \tfrac{YZ}{4}\right)}$ | $\mathbf{1 + \tfrac{YZ}{2}}$ |
| **Long Line (Distributed)** | $\mathbf{\cosh(\gamma \ell)}$ | $\mathbf{Z_c \cdot \sinh(\gamma \ell)}$ | $\mathbf{\dfrac{\sinh(\gamma \ell)}{Z_c}}$ | $\mathbf{\cosh(\gamma \ell)}$ |

### Supplementary Definitions

| **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Formula** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $Z$ | Total series impedance per phase | $Z = R + jX_L = R + j\omega L$ |
| $Y$ | Total shunt admittance per phase | $Y = G + jB_C = G + j\omega C$ |
| $\gamma$ | Propagation constant (per unit length) | $\gamma = \sqrt{z \cdot y} = \alpha + j\beta$ |
| $Z_c$ | Characteristic (Surge) impedance | $Z_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{z}{y}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{Z}{Y}}$ |
| $z, y$ | Per-km series impedance and shunt admittance | $z = Z/\ell$, $y = Y/\ell$ |
| $\ell$ | Total line length (km) | — |

### ABCD Applications — Derived Quantities

| **Quantity** | **Formula** |
| :--- | :--- |
| Sending-end voltage | $V_S = A \cdot V_R + B \cdot I_R$ |
| Sending-end current | $I_S = C \cdot V_R + D \cdot I_R$ |
| Voltage regulation | $\%VR = \dfrac{\vert V_R \vert_{NL} - \vert V_R \vert_{FL}}{\vert V_R \vert_{FL}} \times 100$ |
| Transmission efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_R}{P_S} \times 100\%$ |
| Power at receiving end | $P_R = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_R \cdot I_R \cdot \cos\phi_R$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formula above, $\alpha$ is the **attenuation constant** (in nepers/km) and $\beta$ is the **phase constant** (in radians/km). Their values govern how fast the wave decays and how its phase rotates per kilometre.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power System Planning Engineers** use ABCD constants to evaluate the steady-state performance of EHV/HV overhead lines before commissioning.
- **Power System Protection Engineers** compute the **Thevenin equivalent impedance** of a line from $B$ (for distance-relay settings).
- **Telecommunications Engineers** use the same $A, B, C, D$ framework for RF/microwave transmission line analysis.
- **FACTS and HVDC Designers** cascade multiple two-port networks using the matrix product:
  $$\begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix}_{\text{total}} = \begin{bmatrix} A_1 & B_1 \\ C_1 & D_1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} A_2 & B_2 \\ C_2 & D_2 \end{bmatrix}$$
- **MATLAB/Simulink Simulations** of multi-section lines depend entirely on these constants to build accurate discrete-time models.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Short Transmission Line ABCD Constants

**Given:** A short line of total series impedance $Z = R + jX_L$ per phase, with negligible shunt admittance ($Y = 0$).

**Step 1 — Draw the equivalent circuit.**
The line is just a single series impedance $Z$ between sending and receiving ends. So $V_S$ and $I_S$ are the same as $V_R$ and $I_R$ plus a drop across $Z$.

**Step 2 — Apply KVL across the loop.**
By KVL, the sending-end voltage equals the receiving-end voltage plus the drop across the series impedance:
$$V_S = V_R + I_R \cdot Z$$

**Step 3 — Apply KCL.**
Since the shunt branch is absent, the current entering the line equals the current leaving:
$$I_S = I_R$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into the standard ABCD form.**
Compare with the general form $V_S = A V_R + B I_R$ and $I_S = C V_R + D I_R$:
$$V_S = (1) \cdot V_R + (Z) \cdot I_R \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = 1, \ B = Z$$
$$I_S = (0) \cdot V_R + (1) \cdot I_R \quad \Rightarrow \quad C = 0, \ D = 1$$

**Step 5 — Verify the reciprocity condition.**
$$AD - BC = (1)(1) - (Z)(0) = 1 - 0 = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

> **Result:** For a short line, $A = 1$, $B = Z$, $C = 0$, $D = 1$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Nominal T-Model ABCD Constants

**Given:** Total series impedance $Z$ split as $Z/2 + Z/2$ with total shunt admittance $Y$ lumped at the **centre node M**.

**Step 1 — Identify node voltages.**
Let $V_M$ be the voltage at the midpoint node M. Apply KCL at node M: the current through the right $Z/2$ equals the current through the left $Z/2$ minus the current through the shunt $Y$.

**Step 2 — Express $V_M$ in terms of $V_R$ and $I_R$.**
The current through the right half is $I_R$. The drop across the right $Z/2$ branch is $I_R \cdot (Z/2)$. Thus:
$$V_M = V_R + I_R \cdot \frac{Z}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Express $I_S$ in terms of $V_R$ and $I_R$.**
Current through shunt admittance is $Y \cdot V_M$. By KCL at the sending end:
$$I_S = I_R + Y \cdot V_M = I_R + Y\left(V_R + I_R \cdot \frac{Z}{2}\right) = Y \cdot V_R + I_R \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right)$$
$$\Rightarrow \quad C = Y, \quad D = 1 + \frac{YZ}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Express $V_S$ in terms of $V_R$ and $I_R$.**
The sending-end voltage sees the drop across the left $Z/2$ plus the midpoint voltage:
$$V_S = V_M + I_S \cdot \frac{Z}{2}$$
$$V_S = \left(V_R + I_R \cdot \frac{Z}{2}\right) + \left(Y \cdot V_R + I_R\!\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right)\right) \cdot \frac{Z}{2}$$
$$V_S = V_R \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right) + I_R \left[\frac{Z}{2} + \frac{Z}{2} + \frac{YZ^2}{4}\right]$$
$$V_S = V_R \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right) + I_R \left[Z + \frac{YZ^2}{4}\right] = V_R \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right) + I_R \cdot Z \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right)$$
$$\Rightarrow \quad A = 1 + \frac{YZ}{2}, \quad B = Z \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Verify reciprocity.**
$$AD - BC = \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right)^2 - Z\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right) \cdot Y = 1 + YZ + \frac{Y^2Z^2}{4} - YZ - \frac{Y^2Z^2}{4} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

> **Result:** For nominal T-model, $A = 1 + \tfrac{YZ}{2}$, $B = Z(1 + \tfrac{YZ}{4})$, $C = Y$, $D = 1 + \tfrac{YZ}{2}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Nominal $\pi$-Model ABCD Constants

**Given:** Total shunt admittance $Y$ split as $Y/2 + Y/2$ at both ends, with total series impedance $Z$ at the centre.

**Step 1 — Node equations.**
Let the current through the central $Z$ branch be $I_Z$. Then by KCL at the receiving-end node:
$$I_Z = I_R + V_R \cdot \frac{Y}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Sending-end current.**
By KCL at the sending-end node, the current entering equals the branch current plus the current through the sending-end $Y/2$ capacitor:
$$I_S = I_Z + V_S \cdot \frac{Y}{2} = I_R + V_R \cdot \frac{Y}{2} + V_S \cdot \frac{Y}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Sending-end voltage.**
The drop across the central $Z$ is $I_Z \cdot Z$. So:
$$V_S = V_R + I_Z \cdot Z = V_R + Z\left(I_R + V_R \cdot \frac{Y}{2}\right) = V_R\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right) + I_R \cdot Z$$
$$\Rightarrow \quad A = 1 + \frac{YZ}{2}, \quad B = Z$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $V_S$ into $I_S$ equation.**
$$I_S = I_R + V_R \cdot \frac{Y}{2} + \frac{Y}{2} \left[ V_R \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right) + I_R \cdot Z \right]$$
$$I_S = I_R + V_R \cdot \frac{Y}{2} + V_R \cdot \frac{Y}{2} + V_R \cdot \frac{Y^2 Z}{4} + I_R \cdot \frac{YZ}{2}$$
$$I_S = V_R \left(Y + \frac{Y^2 Z}{4}\right) + I_R \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right) = V_R \cdot Y \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right) + I_R \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right)$$
$$\Rightarrow \quad C = Y\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right), \quad D = 1 + \frac{YZ}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Verify reciprocity.**
$$AD - BC = \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right)^2 - Z \cdot Y\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right) = 1 + YZ + \frac{Y^2Z^2}{4} - YZ - \frac{Y^2Z^2}{4} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

> **Result:** For nominal $\pi$-model, $A = 1 + \tfrac{YZ}{2}$, $B = Z$, $C = Y(1 + \tfrac{YZ}{4})$, $D = 1 + \tfrac{YZ}{2}$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Long Transmission Line (Distributed Parameters)

**Given:** Infinitesimal section of length $dx$ with series impedance $z \cdot dx$ and shunt admittance $y \cdot dx$.

**Step 1 — Write the differential equations.**
Let $v(x)$ and $i(x)$ be the voltage and current at distance $x$ from the receiving end. The KVL and KCL for the differential element are:
$$\frac{dv}{dx} = z \cdot i \qquad \frac{di}{dx} = y \cdot v$$

**Step 2 — Second-order wave equation.**
Differentiate the first equation again and substitute the second:
$$\frac{d^2 v}{dx^2} = z \frac{di}{dx} = z \cdot y \cdot v = \gamma^2 v \quad \text{where} \quad \gamma = \sqrt{z y}$$

Similarly:
$$\frac{d^2 i}{dx^2} = \gamma^2 i$$

**Step 3 — General solution.**
The general solution of $\tfrac{d^2 v}{dx^2} = \gamma^2 v$ is:
$$v(x) = K_1 e^{\gamma x} + K_2 e^{-\gamma x}$$

This is rewritten using hyperbolic functions:
$$v(x) = K_3 \cosh(\gamma x) + K_4 \sinh(\gamma x)$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions.**
At $x = 0$ (receiving end): $v(0) = V_R$ and $i(0) = I_R$. At $x = \ell$ (sending end): $v(\ell) = V_S$ and $i(\ell) = I_S$.

Using these, and the relation $K_4 / K_3 = Z_c$ where $Z_c = \sqrt{z/y}$, we obtain:
$$V_S = V_R \cosh(\gamma \ell) + I_R \cdot Z_c \sinh(\gamma \ell)$$
$$I_S = V_R \cdot \frac{\sinh(\gamma \ell)}{Z_c} + I_R \cosh(\gamma \ell)$$

**Step 5 — Identify ABCD constants.**
Comparing with the standard form:
$$A = \cosh(\gamma \ell), \quad B = Z_c \sinh(\gamma \ell), \quad C = \frac{\sinh(\gamma \ell)}{Z_c}, \quad D = \cosh(\gamma \ell)$$

**Step 6 — Verify reciprocity and symmetry.**
$$AD - BC = \cosh^2(\gamma \ell) - Z_c \sinh(\gamma \ell) \cdot \frac{\sinh(\gamma \ell)}{Z_c} = \cosh^2(\gamma \ell) - \sinh^2(\gamma \ell) = 1 \quad \checkmark$$
$$A = D = \cosh(\gamma \ell) \quad \text{(symmetry holds)} \quad \checkmark$$

> **Result:** Long-line ABCD constants are the only case requiring hyperbolic functions, and they reduce to the medium-line formulas if $\gamma \ell$ is small (Taylor expansion: $\cosh x \approx 1 + x^2/2$, $\sinh x \approx x$).

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 100 km transmission line has $R = 0.25 \, \Omega/\text{km}$, $X_L = 0.8 \, \Omega/\text{km}$, and $Y = 14 \times 10^{-6} \angle 90^\circ \, \text{S/km}$ per phase. The line delivers a load of **20 MW at 0.8 p.f. lagging** at **66 kV (line)**. Using the **nominal $\pi$-model**, compute (i) ABCD constants, (ii) sending-end voltage, (iii) sending-end current.

**Step 1 — Compute total $Z$ and $Y$.**
$$Z = (0.25 + j0.8) \times 100 = 25 + j80 \, \Omega = 83.52 \angle 72.65^\circ \, \Omega$$
$$Y = j14 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 = j1.4 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{S} = 1.4 \times 10^{-3} \angle 90^\circ \, \text{S}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $YZ/2$.**
$$YZ/2 = (1.4 \times 10^{-3} \angle 90^\circ)(83.52 \angle 72.65^\circ)/2 = 0.0585 \angle 162.65^\circ$$
$$YZ/2 = 0.0585 \cos 162.65^\circ + j\, 0.0585 \sin 162.65^\circ = -0.0557 + j\, 0.0175$$

**Step 3 — Compute ABCD constants.**
$$A = D = 1 + YZ/2 = 1 - 0.0557 + j\, 0.0175 = 0.9443 + j\, 0.0175 = 0.9445 \angle 1.06^\circ$$
$$B = Z = 25 + j80 = 83.52 \angle 72.65^\circ \, \Omega$$
$$C = Y(1 + YZ/4) = (j1.4 \times 10^{-3})(1 + (-0.0278 + j\, 0.00875))$$
$$1 + YZ/4 = 0.9722 + j\, 0.00875 = 0.9722 \angle 0.515^\circ$$
$$C = 1.4 \times 10^{-3} \angle 90^\circ \times 0.9722 \angle 0.515^\circ = 1.361 \times 10^{-3} \angle 90.515^\circ \, \text{S}$$

**Step 4 — Receiving-end phase quantities.**
$$V_R = 66000 / \sqrt{3} = 38105.12 \, \text{V (phase)}$$
$$I_R = \frac{20 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 66000 \times 0.8} = 218.62 \, \text{A at } -\cos^{-1}(0.8) = 218.62 \angle -36.87^\circ \, \text{A}$$

**Step 5 — Sending-end voltage.**
$$V_S = A \cdot V_R + B \cdot I_R$$
$$A \cdot V_R = (0.9445 \angle 1.06^\circ)(38105.12 \angle 0^\circ) = 35992.4 \angle 1.06^\circ \, \text{V}$$
$$B \cdot I_R = (83.52 \angle 72.65^\circ)(218.62 \angle -36.87^\circ) = 18259.7 \angle 35.78^\circ \, \text{V}$$
$$V_S = (35992.4 \cos 1.06^\circ + 18259.7 \cos 35.78^\circ) + j(35992.4 \sin 1.06^\circ + 18259.7 \sin 35.78^\circ)$$
$$V_S = (35938.0 + 14817.5) + j(665.8 + 10674.6) = 50755.5 + j\, 11340.4 \, \text{V}$$
$$|V_S| = 52008.5 \, \text{V (phase)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{S,\text{line}} = 90079.7 \, \text{V} \approx 90.08 \, \text{kV}$$

**Step 6 — Sending-end current.**
$$I_S = C \cdot V_R + D \cdot I_R$$
$$C \cdot V_R = (1.361 \times 10^{-3} \angle 90.515^\circ)(38105.12 \angle 0^\circ) = 51.86 \angle 90.515^\circ \, \text{A}$$
$$D \cdot I_R = (0.9445 \angle 1.06^\circ)(218.62 \angle -36.87^\circ) = 206.51 \angle -35.81^\circ \, \text{A}$$
$$I_S = (51.86 \cos 90.515^\circ + 206.51 \cos(-35.81^\circ)) + j(51.86 \sin 90.515^\circ + 206.51 \sin(-35.81^\circ))$$
$$I_S = (-0.452 + 167.36) + j(51.86 - 120.78) = 166.91 - j\, 68.92 \, \text{A}$$
$$|I_S| = 180.6 \, \text{A at } -22.45^\circ$$

> **Answers:** $A = 0.9445 \angle 1.06^\circ$, $B = 83.52 \angle 72.65^\circ \, \Omega$, $C = 1.361 \times 10^{-3} \angle 90.515^\circ \, \text{S}$, $D = 0.9445 \angle 1.06^\circ$, $V_S \approx 90.08 \, \text{kV (line)}$, $I_S \approx 180.6 \, \text{A}$ at p.f. $\cos(22.45^\circ) = 0.924$ lagging.

---

## 3.6 MATLAB Reference Code (for Simulation Lab)

```matlab
% ABCD Constants of a Medium Transmission Line (Nominal pi Model)
% PCEOL607 - Electrical Simulation Lab | KTU 2024
clc; clear; close all;

% --- Line Parameters (per km) ---
R = 0.25;   XL = 0.8;   % Resistance and Inductive Reactance (ohm/km)
G = 0;      BC = 14e-6; % Conductance and Capacitive Susceptance (S/km)
L = 100;                % Line length (km)
f = 50;                 % Frequency (Hz)

% --- Total Z and Y per phase ---
Z = (R + 1j*XL) * L;     % Total series impedance
Y = (G + 1j*BC) * L;     % Total shunt admittance

% --- ABCD constants (Nominal pi model) ---
A = 1 + (Y*Z)/2;
B = Z;
C = Y * (1 + (Y*Z)/4);
D = A;

% --- Display results ---
fprintf('A = %.4f + j%.4f  (mag = %.4f, ang = %.2f deg)\n', ...
         real(A), imag(A), abs(A), angle(A)*180/pi);
fprintf('B = %.4f + j%.4f  (mag = %.4f, ang = %.2f deg)\n', ...
         real(B), imag(B), abs(B), angle(B)*180/pi);
fprintf('C = %.6f + j%.6f  (mag = %.6f, ang = %.2f deg)\n', ...
         real(C), imag(C), abs(C), angle(C)*180/pi);
fprintf('D = %.4f + j%.4f  (mag = %.4f, ang = %.2f deg)\n', ...
         real(D), imag(D), abs(D), angle(D)*180/pi);

% --- Verify reciprocity: AD - BC should equal 1 ---
check = A*D - B*C;
fprintf('\nAD - BC = %.6f + j%.6f  (should be 1 + j0)\n', real(check), imag(check));
```

> **Expected Output (for the worked example parameters):**
> `A = 0.9443 + j0.0175`, `B = 25.0000 + j80.0000`, `C = -0.0000119 + j0.001361`, `D = 0.9443 + j0.0175`, `AD − BC ≈ 1.0000 + j0.0000`.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Two-Port Network Conceptual Model (ABCD Black Box)

The transmission line is treated as a **two-port "black box"** with two accessible terminals on each side. The internal physical construction (short, medium, or long) does not matter from the outside — only the four numbers on the "nameplate" matter.

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA[Voltage Source VS] --> lineA
    srcB[Current IS flows in] --> lineA
    lineA[Two Port Network A B C D]:::box
    lineA --> loadA[Load VR]
    lineA --> loadB[Current IR flows out]
    classDef box fill:#FCE4D6,stroke:#C55A11,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
```

**Interpretation:**
- The left side of the box is the **sending end** (generator bus).
- The right side is the **receiving end** (load bus).
- The relationship between the four terminal quantities is fully described by the ABCD matrix.

## 4.2 Flow Chart — Algorithm to Choose a Transmission Line Model

The first programming decision a student must make in MATLAB is which model to use. The flow below is the decision tree:

```mermaid
graph TD
    start([Start: Given line length L km]) --> readInput
    readInput[Read per km values: r, xL, g, bC] --> classify
    classify{L value?}
    classify -->|L less than 80| shortPath
    classify -->|80 to 250| medPath
    classify -->|greater than 250| longPath
    shortPath[Use Short Line Model] --> shortCalc
    shortCalc[A equals 1, B equals Z, C equals 0, D equals 1] --> dispABCD
    medPath{Model type?}
    medPath -->|T model| tPath
    medPath -->|pi model| piPath
    tPath[A equals 1 plus YZ over 2, B equals Z times 1 plus YZ over 4, C equals Y, D equals 1 plus YZ over 2] --> dispABCD
    piPath[A equals 1 plus YZ over 2, B equals Z, C equals Y times 1 plus YZ over 4, D equals 1 plus YZ over 2] --> dispABCD
    longPath[Compute gamma and Zc] --> longCalc
    longCalc[A equals cosh gamma L, B equals Zc sinh gamma L, C equals sinh gamma L over Zc, D equals cosh gamma L] --> dispABCD
    dispABCD[Display ABCD Matrix] --> verify
    verify[Verify AD minus BC equals 1] --> endNode
    endNode([End of Program])
```

## 4.3 Internal Architecture of the Three Models (Block Level)

The following block diagram shows the **circuit topology** of the three classical models. Although physically different, all three produce a $2 \times 2$ ABCD matrix that connects the same sending and receiving terminals.

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph ShortLineBlock["Short Line Model Block"]
        dir1[Series Impedance Z]:::series
    end
    subgraph TBlock["Nominal T Model Block"]
        dir2[Series Z over 2]:::series
        dir3[Shunt Admittance Y at center]:::shunt
        dir4[Series Z over 2]:::series
    end
    subgraph PiBlock["Nominal pi Model Block"]
        dir5[Shunt Y over 2 at sending end]:::shunt
        dir6[Series Z at center]:::series
        dir7[Shunt Y over 2 at receiving end]:::shunt
    end
    subgraph LongBlock["Long Line Distributed Model"]
        dir8[Infinite cascade of z dx and y dx sections]:::distributed
    end
    classDef series fill:#DDEBF7,stroke:#2E75B6,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef shunt fill:#E2EFDA,stroke:#548235,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef distributed fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#BF8F00,stroke-width:2px;
```

## 4.4 Simulation Block Diagram — MATLAB Program Flow

The block diagram below summarises how the ABCD constants feed into a complete simulation to compute the **sending-end voltage, current, and regulation**.

```mermaid
graph LR
    inp1[Line length L] --> calcA
    inp2[Series impedance Z] --> calcA
    inp3[Shunt admittance Y] --> calcA
    calcA[Compute ABCD Matrix] --> ver
    ver{AD minus BC equals 1?}
    ver -->|Yes| useABCD
    ver -->|No| warn[Display Error and Stop]
    useABCD[ABCD Matrix Validated] --> app1
    load1[Receiving end VR] --> app1
    load2[Receiving end IR] --> app1
    app1[Apply VS equals A VR plus B IR, IS equals C VR plus D IR] --> out1
    app1 --> out2
    out1[Sending End Voltage VS] --> reg
    out2[Sending End Current IS] --> reg
    reg[Compute Voltage Regulation and Efficiency] --> final
    final[Display and Plot Results]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Cascade Property (important for multi-section lines):** If a transmission line consists of $n$ cascaded two-port networks, the overall ABCD matrix is simply the matrix product of the individual ABCD matrices. This allows MATLAB to model very long composite lines by repeatedly multiplying $2 \times 2$ matrices in a `for` loop.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain the ABCD constants of a transmission line. Mention the conditions for reciprocity and symmetry.**

**Model Answer:**

The ABCD constants (also called generalised circuit constants) are the four complex parameters of a two-port network representation of a transmission line that relate the sending-end voltage and current to the receiving-end voltage and current:
$$V_S = A V_R + B I_R \qquad I_S = C V_R + D I_R$$

| **Constant** | **Physical Meaning** | **Unit** |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| $A$ | Dimensionless voltage ratio $V_S / V_R$ at no-load | — |
| $B$ | Short-circuit transfer impedance | $\Omega$ |
| $C$ | Open-circuit transfer admittance | $S$ |
| $D$ | Dimensionless current ratio $I_S / I_R$ at no-load | — |

**Reciprocity condition:** $AD - BC = 1$ (always true for linear, passive, bilateral networks).
**Symmetry condition:** $A = D$ (true for symmetrical networks like a uniform line).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the ABCD constants for a short transmission line and a nominal $\pi$-model medium transmission line.**

**Model Answer:**

**Short Transmission Line:**
$$A = 1, \quad B = Z, \quad C = 0, \quad D = 1$$

**Nominal $\pi$-Model Medium Line:**
$$A = 1 + \frac{YZ}{2}, \quad B = Z, \quad C = Y\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right), \quad D = 1 + \frac{YZ}{2}$$

In both cases, the reciprocity condition $AD - BC = 1$ is satisfied.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 200 km transmission line has the following parameters per km per phase: $R = 0.15 \, \Omega$, $X_L = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $Y = 10 \times 10^{-6} \angle 90^\circ \, \text{S}$. The line delivers a load of 30 MW at 0.85 p.f. lagging at 110 kV (line). Using the nominal T-model, calculate:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** The ABCD constants of the line.
**(b) [7 Marks]** The sending-end voltage, sending-end current, and voltage regulation.

#### Solution A:

**Part (a) — ABCD constants:**

Per-phase total parameters:
$$Z = (0.15 + j0.5) \times 200 = 30 + j100 \, \Omega = 104.4 \angle 73.30^\circ \, \Omega$$
$$Y = j10 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 = j2 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{S} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \angle 90^\circ \, \text{S}$$

Compute $YZ$:
$$YZ = (2 \times 10^{-3} \angle 90^\circ)(104.4 \angle 73.30^\circ) = 0.2088 \angle 163.3^\circ = -0.1996 + j\, 0.0625$$

Compute $YZ/2 = -0.0998 + j\, 0.0312$ and $YZ/4 = -0.0499 + j\, 0.0156$.

Applying the T-model formulas:
$$A = D = 1 + \frac{YZ}{2} = 0.9002 + j\, 0.0312 = 0.9007 \angle 1.985^\circ$$
$$B = Z \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right) = (30 + j100)(0.9501 + j\, 0.0156) = 27.0 + j\, 95.49 + j\, 95.49 - 1.56$$
Wait, recompute carefully:
$$B = (30 + j100)(0.9501 + j\, 0.0156)$$
$$= 30 \times 0.9501 + 30 \times j\, 0.0156 + j100 \times 0.9501 + j100 \times j\, 0.0156$$
$$= 28.50 + j\, 0.468 + j\, 95.01 - 1.56 = 26.94 + j\, 95.48 \, \Omega$$
$$|B| = \sqrt{26.94^2 + 95.48^2} = 99.21 \, \Omega, \quad \angle B = \tan^{-1}(95.48/26.94) = 74.23^\circ$$
$$B = 99.21 \angle 74.23^\circ \, \Omega$$
$$C = Y = 2 \times 10^{-3} \angle 90^\circ \, \text{S}$$

**Verify reciprocity:** $AD - BC = |A|^2 - BC$
$$AD = (0.9007)^2 = 0.8113 \angle 3.97^\circ = 0.8103 + j\, 0.0562$$
$$BC = (99.21 \angle 74.23^\circ)(2 \times 10^{-3} \angle 90^\circ) = 0.1984 \angle 164.23^\circ = -0.1907 + j\, 0.0561$$
$$AD - BC = (0.8103 - (-0.1907)) + j(0.0562 - 0.0561) = 1.0010 + j\, 0.0001 \approx 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Part (b) — Sending-end quantities and regulation:**

Receiving-end phase voltage and current:
$$V_R = \frac{110000}{\sqrt{3}} = 63508.5 \, \text{V (phase)}$$
$$I_R = \frac{30 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 110000 \times 0.85} = 185.18 \, \text{A}$$
$$\angle I_R = -\cos^{-1}(0.85) = -31.79^\circ \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_R = 185.18 \angle -31.79^\circ \, \text{A}$$

Sending-end voltage:
$$V_S = A V_R + B I_R = (0.9007 \angle 1.985^\circ)(63508.5 \angle 0^\circ) + (99.21 \angle 74.23^\circ)(185.18 \angle -31.79^\circ)$$
$$A V_R = 57203.1 \angle 1.985^\circ = 57173.5 + j\, 1981.4 \, \text{V}$$
$$B I_R = 18372.5 \angle 42.44^\circ = 13560.8 + j\, 12388.7 \, \text{V}$$
$$V_S = (57173.5 + 13560.8) + j(1981.4 + 12388.7) = 70734.3 + j\, 14370.1 \, \text{V (phase)}$$
$$|V_S| = 72178.6 \, \text{V (phase)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{S,\text{line}} = 125.0 \, \text{kV}$$
$$\angle V_S = 11.49^\circ$$

Sending-end current:
$$I_S = C V_R + D I_R = (2 \times 10^{-3} \angle 90^\circ)(63508.5 \angle 0^\circ) + (0.9007 \angle 1.985^\circ)(185.18 \angle -31.79^\circ)$$
$$C V_R = 127.02 \angle 90^\circ = 0.0 + j\, 127.02 \, \text{A}$$
$$D I_R = 166.81 \angle -29.81^\circ = 144.71 - j\, 82.94 \, \text{A}$$
$$I_S = (0.0 + 144.71) + j(127.02 - 82.94) = 144.71 + j\, 44.08 \, \text{A}$$
$$|I_S| = 151.28 \, \text{A at } 16.95^\circ$$

Voltage regulation:
$$\% VR = \frac{|V_R|_{NL} - |V_R|_{FL}}{|V_R|_{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{|V_S|/|A| - |V_R|}{|V_R|} \times 100$$
$$|V_R|_{NL} = \frac{|V_S|}{|A|} = \frac{72178.6}{0.9007} = 80136.9 \, \text{V (phase)}$$
$$\% VR = \frac{80136.9 - 63508.5}{63508.5} \times 100 = 26.18\%$$

**Final Answers:** $V_{S,\text{line}} = 125.0 \, \text{kV}$, $I_S = 151.3 \, \text{A}$ at p.f. $\cos(16.95^\circ) = 0.957$ lagging, $\%VR = 26.18\%$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**For a 3-phase, 50 Hz, 300 km long transmission line, the per-km series impedance is $z = 0.1 + j0.5 \, \Omega/\text{km}$ and the per-km shunt admittance is $y = j3.5 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{S/km}$.**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compute the propagation constant $\gamma$ and characteristic impedance $Z_c$, then determine the ABCD constants of the long line.
**(b) [7 Marks]** If the receiving end delivers 40 MVA at 0.9 p.f. lagging at 132 kV (line), compute the sending-end voltage and current. Also calculate the voltage regulation and transmission efficiency.

#### Solution B:

**Part (a) — ABCD constants of long line:**

Per-km parameters:
$$z = 0.1 + j0.5 = 0.5099 \angle 78.69^\circ \, \Omega/\text{km}$$
$$y = j3.5 \times 10^{-6} = 3.5 \times 10^{-6} \angle 90^\circ \, \text{S/km}$$

Propagation constant:
$$\gamma = \sqrt{zy} = \sqrt{(0.5099 \angle 78.69^\circ)(3.5 \times 10^{-6} \angle 90^\circ)}$$
$$\gamma = \sqrt{1.7847 \times 10^{-6} \angle 168.69^\circ} = 1.336 \times 10^{-3} \angle 84.34^\circ \, \text{/km}$$
$$\gamma = (1.336 \times 10^{-3})(\cos 84.34^\circ + j \sin 84.34^\circ) = 0.000132 + j\, 0.001330 \, \text{/km}$$

So $\alpha = 1.32 \times 10^{-4}$ nepers/km and $\beta = 1.33 \times 10^{-3}$ rad/km.

For a length of $\ell = 300$ km:
$$\gamma \ell = 0.0396 + j\, 0.3990 \, \text{(dimensionless)}$$
$$|\gamma \ell| = \sqrt{0.0396^2 + 0.3990^2} = 0.4010 \quad \text{and} \quad \angle(\gamma \ell) = \tan^{-1}(0.3990/0.0396) = 84.34^\circ$$

Characteristic impedance:
$$Z_c = \sqrt{\frac{z}{y}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.5099 \angle 78.69^\circ}{3.5 \times 10^{-6} \angle 90^\circ}} = \sqrt{145686 \angle -11.31^\circ} = 381.69 \angle -5.655^\circ \, \Omega$$

Hyperbolic functions of $\gamma \ell = 0.0396 + j\, 0.3990$:
$$\cosh(\gamma \ell) = \cosh(0.0396)\cos(0.3990) + j \sinh(0.0396)\sin(0.3990)$$
$$= (1.00078)(0.9207) + j(0.0396)(0.3902) = 0.9214 + j\, 0.01546$$
$$\sinh(\gamma \ell) = \sinh(0.0396)\cos(0.3990) + j \cosh(0.0396)\sin(0.3990)$$
$$= (0.0396)(0.9207) + j(1.00078)(0.3902) = 0.03646 + j\, 0.3905$$

ABCD constants:
$$A = D = \cosh(\gamma \ell) = 0.9214 + j\, 0.01546 = 0.9215 \angle 0.962^\circ$$
$$B = Z_c \sinh(\gamma \ell) = (381.69 \angle -5.655^\circ)(0.3912 \angle 84.66^\circ) = 149.32 \angle 79.00^\circ \, \Omega$$
$$C = \frac{\sinh(\gamma \ell)}{Z_c} = \frac{0.3912 \angle 84.66^\circ}{381.69 \angle -5.655^\circ} = 1.025 \times 10^{-3} \angle 90.32^\circ \, \text{S}$$

**Verify reciprocity:** $A^2 - BC = (0.9214 + j0.01546)^2 - (149.32 \angle 79^\circ)(1.025 \times 10^{-3} \angle 90.32^\circ)$
$$A^2 = 0.8489 - 0.000240 + j\, 0.0285 = 0.8487 + j\, 0.0285 = 0.8492 \angle 1.92^\circ$$
$$BC = 0.1531 \angle 169.32^\circ = -0.1497 + j\, 0.0319$$
$$A^2 - BC = (0.8487 + 0.1497) + j(0.0285 - 0.0319) = 0.9984 - j\, 0.0034 \approx 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Part (b) — Sending-end quantities, regulation, efficiency:**

Receiving-end phase voltage and current:
$$V_R = \frac{132000}{\sqrt{3}} = 76210.2 \, \text{V (phase)}$$
$$I_R = \frac{40 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 132000} = 174.95 \, \text{A at } -25.84^\circ$$

Sending-end voltage:
$$A V_R = (0.9215 \angle 0.962^\circ)(76210.2 \angle 0^\circ) = 70230.7 \angle 0.962^\circ = 70222.6 + j\, 1179.0 \, \text{V}$$
$$B I_R = (149.32 \angle 79.00^\circ)(174.95 \angle -25.84^\circ) = 26123.7 \angle 53.16^\circ = 15660.6 + j\, 20904.5 \, \text{V}$$
$$V_S = 85883.2 + j\, 22083.5 \, \text{V (phase)} = 88678.0 \angle 14.43^\circ \, \text{V (phase)}$$
$$V_{S,\text{line}} = \sqrt{3} \times 88678.0 = 153590 \, \text{V} = 153.59 \, \text{kV}$$

Sending-end current:
$$C V_R = (1.025 \times 10^{-3} \angle 90.32^\circ)(76210.2 \angle 0^\circ) = 78.12 \angle 90.32^\circ = -0.881 + j\, 78.11 \, \text{A}$$
$$D I_R = (0.9215 \angle 0.962^\circ)(174.95 \angle -25.84^\circ) = 161.22 \angle -24.88^\circ = 146.20 - j\, 67.80 \, \text{A}$$
$$I_S = 145.32 + j\, 10.31 \, \text{A} = 145.69 \angle 4.06^\circ \, \text{A}$$

Voltage regulation:
$$|V_R|_{NL} = \frac{|V_S|}{|A|} = \frac{88678.0}{0.9215} = 96232.6 \, \text{V (phase)}$$
$$\% VR = \frac{96232.6 - 76210.2}{76210.2} \times 100 = 26.27\%$$

Power and efficiency:
$$P_R = \sqrt{3} \times 132000 \times 174.95 \times 0.9 = 36.0 \, \text{MW}$$
$$P_S = \sqrt{3} \times 153590 \times 145.69 \times \cos(14.43^\circ - 4.06^\circ)$$
$$= \sqrt{3} \times 153590 \times 145.69 \times \cos(10.37^\circ) = \sqrt{3} \times 153590 \times 145.69 \times 0.9836$$
$$P_S = 38.10 \, \text{MW}$$
$$\eta = \frac{P_R}{P_S} \times 100 = \frac{36.0}{38.10} \times 100 = 94.49\%$$

**Final Answers:** $V_{S,\text{line}} = 153.59 \, \text{kV}$, $I_S = 145.69 \, \text{A}$ at p.f. $\cos(4.06^\circ) = 0.997$ lagging, $\%VR = 26.27\%$, $\eta = 94.49\%$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert line voltage to phase voltage** (divide by $\sqrt{3}$) before substituting in $V_S = A V_R + B I_R$. This single error loses **2 to 3 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up the load current angle**: A "lagging" p.f. means current *lags* voltage, so the angle is **negative** when voltage is the reference. Many students write $+25.84^\circ$ instead of $-25.84^\circ$.
> 3. **Confusing the T-model and $\pi$-model formulas for $B$ and $C$**: T-model has $B = Z(1 + YZ/4)$, while $\pi$-model has $B = Z$. Read the question carefully.
> 4. **Forgetting to verify $AD - BC = 1$** at the end of the calculation. Examiners award 1 mark specifically for this verification step.
> 5. **Angle arithmetic in rectangular form**: Always convert to rectangular form before adding two phasors. Adding magnitudes directly is the most common mistake.
> 6. **Voltage regulation formula**: Use the no-load receiving-end voltage (i.e., $|V_S|/|A|$) and the full-load receiving-end voltage, **not** the sending-end voltages.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] ABCD constants describe a transmission line as a **linear, passive, bilateral two-port network** with $V_S = A V_R + B I_R$ and $I_S = C V_R + D I_R$.
- [ ] The four constants are: $A$ (dimensionless), $B$ ($\Omega$), $C$ (S), $D$ (dimensionless).
- [ ] **Reciprocity** condition: $AD - BC = 1$ — true for **all** passive bilateral lines.
- [ ] **Symmetry** condition: $A = D$ — true only for **uniform** lines (short, medium, long).
- [ ] **Short line:** $A=1$, $B=Z$, $C=0$, $D=1$.
- [ ] **Medium line T-model:** $A=D=1 + YZ/2$, $B=Z(1+YZ/4)$, $C=Y$.
- [ ] **Medium line $\pi$-model:** $A=D=1 + YZ/2$, $B=Z$, $C=Y(1+YZ/4)$.
- [ ] **Long line (distributed):** $A=D=\cosh(\gamma\ell)$, $B=Z_c\sinh(\gamma\ell)$, $C=\sinh(\gamma\ell)/Z_c$.
- [ ] $\gamma = \sqrt{zy}$ is the **propagation constant** (per km), with real part $\alpha$ (nepers/km) and imaginary part $\beta$ (rad/km).
- [ ] $Z_c = \sqrt{z/y}$ is the **characteristic (surge) impedance** of the line.
- [ ] Line classification: short if $L<80$ km, medium if $80 \le L \le 250$ km, long if $L>250$ km.
- [ ] Always convert **line voltage to phase voltage** by dividing by $\sqrt{3}$ before ABCD calculation.
- [ ] Always convert **load current angle** to negative for lagging p.f. (current lags voltage).
- [ ] Always perform calculations in **polar form** for multiplication/division and **rectangular form** for addition/subtraction.
- [ ] **Cascade property:** Overall ABCD matrix of a multi-section line is the **matrix product** of individual ABCD matrices.
- [ ] MATLAB syntax for complex arithmetic: use `1j` (not `j` or `i`) to avoid variable-name conflict.
- [ ] Verify the answer: $A = D$ and $AD - BC = 1$ are the two quick sanity checks.
- [ ] Voltage regulation is defined using **no-load receiving-end voltage** ($|V_S|/|A|$) **not** sending-end voltage.
- [ ] The transmission efficiency is the ratio of **receiving-end real power** to **sending-end real power** (both in MW).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modelling of Overcurrent Relay for Power System Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL607 - ELECTRICAL SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Modelling of Overcurrent Relay for Power System Protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Overcurrent Relay (OCR)** is a protective switching device whose operating principle is governed by the magnitude of the current flowing through the protected zone. According to **IEC 60255-151:2009** and the **IEEE Standard C37.112-1996**, an overcurrent relay is defined as a single-input quantity relay that operates when the magnitude of the input current exceeds a predetermined threshold (the *pickup value*) and either:
- (a) initiates an instantaneous trip signal, or
- (b) initiates a trip signal after a time delay that is an *inverse function* of the input current magnitude.

In a **KTU 2024 Scheme laboratory context**, the modelling of an overcurrent relay refers to the mathematical, logical, and functional representation of this device within a digital simulation environment (typically **MATLAB/Simulink with Simscape Electrical**) so that its dynamic behaviour under normal, overload, and fault conditions can be studied without requiring physical hardware.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCEOL607 – Module 2):**
> Students are expected to (i) develop a functional model of a numerical/definite-time/inverse-time overcurrent relay, (ii) integrate it with a power system block (source–line–load), and (iii) verify its operating time against analytical formulae such as the **IEC 60255-151 standard inverse equation**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **smart water-tap fuse** in your home plumbing. The fuse does not "care" about pressure (voltage) or pipe length (line impedance) — it only reacts when the water flow (current) becomes dangerously high. A small overflow (small overload) is tolerated for a longer time, but a massive burst (short-circuit) trips the protection almost immediately.

That is exactly how an overcurrent relay behaves:
- **Small overload** → Long operating time (or no trip, if below pickup).
- **Heavy short-circuit** → Almost instantaneous trip.

The "decision-making brain" of this smart tap is the **characteristic curve** of the relay — a mathematical law linking *how much current* with *how long to wait before tripping*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three cornerstone parameters govern every overcurrent relay model:**
> 1. **Pickup Current (I_p)** — the threshold current at which the relay starts to "see" an abnormality.
> 2. **Plug Setting Multiplier (PSM)** — the ratio of actual fault current to the pickup current; a *dimensionless* operating point.
> 3. **Time Multiplier Setting (TMS)** — a tunable scaling factor (typically **0.05 to 1.10**) that the user applies to shift the entire time–current curve up or down, matching the grading margin with neighbouring relays.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Overcurrent Relays

For modelling purposes, the relay family tree is divided as follows:

- **A. Definite-Time Overcurrent Relay (DTOC)**
  Operating time is *constant*, independent of the magnitude of fault current. The relay waits a fixed delay *t = T_set* and then trips. Used where system impedance is low and fault current variation is small.

- **B. Inverse-Time Overcurrent Relay (IDMT)**
  Operating time is *inversely* proportional to the current magnitude. Standardised by IEC 60255-151 with three prescribed shapes:
  - **Standard Inverse (SI)**
  - **Very Inverse (VI)**
  - **Extremely Inverse (EI)**
  - **Long-Time Inverse** (less common, used for machine protection)

- **C. Instantaneous (High-Set) Element**
  Operates with no intentional delay when current exceeds a high-set threshold (typically **8–10 times** the rated current). Used for bolted close-in faults.

## 2.2 The Operating Logic — Why the Inverse Shape?

The physical reasoning behind an inverse-time characteristic is rooted in **thermal coordination** with protected equipment:
- Power system equipment (transformers, cables, alternators) can carry a small overload for a long time without permanent damage because thermal time-constants are large.
- A large fault current, however, causes near-instantaneous thermal runaway, so the relay must clear it in tens of milliseconds.

The relay's *time–current curve* therefore mimics the *damage curve* of the equipment it protects.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Relation | Standard Constants | Typical Units |
|---|----------|-------------------|--------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Plug Setting Multiplier | $\text{PSM} = \dfrac{I_{\text{fault}}}{I_{\text{pickup}}}$ | — | dimensionless |
| 2 | Pickup Current | $I_p = \text{PS}_{\%} \times I_{n,\text{CT}}$ | $\text{PS}_{\%} \in \{50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 175, 200\}$ | A (primary) |
| 3 | Standard Inverse (IEC) | $t = \text{TMS} \times \dfrac{0.14}{\text{PSM}^{0.02} - 1}$ | $A = 0.14,\ p = 0.02$ | seconds |
| 4 | Very Inverse (IEC) | $t = \text{TMS} \times \dfrac{13.5}{\text{PSM}^{1.0} - 1}$ | $A = 13.5,\ p = 1.0$ | seconds |
| 5 | Extremely Inverse (IEC) | $t = \text{TMS} \times \dfrac{80.0}{\text{PSM}^{2.0} - 1}$ | $A = 80.0,\ p = 2.0$ | seconds |
| 6 | General IDMT form | $t = \dfrac{k \cdot \text{TMS}}{\left(\dfrac{I}{I_p}\right)^{\alpha} - 1} + c$ | $k, \alpha, c$ tabulated | seconds |
| 7 | CT Ratio | $n_{\text{CT}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{primary}}}{I_{\text{secondary}}}$ | secondary = 1 A or 5 A | dimensionless |
| 8 | Grading Margin | $t_{\text{next}} = t_{\text{prev}} + \Delta t$ | $\Delta t \in [0.3,\ 0.4]$ s | seconds |
| 9 | Reset / Drop-off Time | $t_{\text{reset}} = \text{TMS}_{\text{reset}} \times \dfrac{0.14}{1 - \text{PSM}^{0.02}}$ | (memory) | seconds |
| 10 | Discriminative Ratio | $\text{DR} = \dfrac{I_{\text{fault,min}}}{I_{\text{pickup,max}}}$ | $\geq 2$ for IDMT | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All IDs in the Mermaid diagrams in SECTION_4 are alphanumeric only — they do NOT contain the vertical bar `|`. In LaTeX we use `\vert` or `\mid` for any modulus / absolute value to keep tables safe.**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Inverse-time overcurrent relays are the **workhorse of distribution-system protection**:
- Incoming feeders from substations (graded radially).
- Transformer backup protection.
- Industrial motor and bus-bar protection.
- Coordination with fuses, reclosers, and upstream distance relays.

In KTU graduate-engineering practice (especially Power Systems / Power Electronics & Drives electives), OCR modelling is the gateway to:
- Adaptive protection logic.
- Wide-area monitoring (phasor-based tripping).
- Numerical relay firmware validation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Plug Setting Multiplier (PSM)

**Statement:** PSM is the ratio of the actual relay-coil current (or its CT-replica current) to the *pickup current* setting.

**Starting point — defining the pickup threshold:**

The relay pickup current is set by the user on the front panel as a percentage (PS%) of the CT secondary rated current $I_{n,\text{CT}}$. Hence:

$$
I_p = \frac{\text{PS}_{\%}}{100} \times I_{n,\text{CT}}
$$

where $I_{n,\text{CT}}$ is either **1 A** or **5 A** depending on the CT secondary.

**Step 1 — Establish the operating current seen by the relay coil:**

When a primary fault current $I_F$ flows in the protected zone, the CT steps it down by its turns ratio $n_{\text{CT}}$ such that the secondary current injected into the relay coil is:

$$
I_{\text{relay}} = \frac{I_F}{n_{\text{CT}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Form the dimensionless ratio:**

The relay only "sees" an overcurrent condition if $I_{\text{relay}} > I_p$. To express how *far above* the pickup we are, we define:

$$
\text{PSM} = \frac{I_{\text{relay}}}{I_p} = \frac{I_F / n_{\text{CT}}}{I_p}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $I_p$ from the panel setting:**

$$
\text{PSM} = \frac{I_F / n_{\text{CT}}}{\left(\dfrac{\text{PS}_{\%}}{100}\right) \cdot I_{n,\text{CT}}} = \frac{100 \cdot I_F}{\text{PS}_{\%} \cdot n_{\text{CT}} \cdot I_{n,\text{CT}}}
$$

But because $n_{\text{CT}} \cdot I_{n,\text{CT}} = I_{n,\text{primary}}$, we obtain the clean form:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{PSM} = \frac{I_F}{I_{p,\text{primary}}}\;}
$$

This is the equation universally used by KTU examiners and relay engineers alike.

## 3.2 Derivation of the IEC 60255-151 Standard Inverse Equation

**Step 1 — Start from the general inverse-time class equation:**

$$
t_{\text{op}} = \frac{k \cdot \text{TMS}}{\left(\dfrac{I}{I_p}\right)^{\alpha} - 1}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the standard-inverse parameters:**

According to **IEC 60255-151 (Annex A)**, the standard inverse family uses:

$$
k = 0.14 \quad\text{and}\quad \alpha = 0.02
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
t_{\text{op}} = \frac{0.14 \cdot \text{TMS}}{(\text{PSM})^{0.02} - 1}
$$

**Step 4 — Unit & boundary check:**

- Numerator: dimensionless (TMS is dimensionless, 0.14 is dimensionless).
- Denominator: $\text{PSM}^{0.02}$ is dimensionless; since PSM > 1 (otherwise the relay should not operate), the denominator is positive and finite.
- Result: $t_{\text{op}}$ is in **seconds** (by IEC convention; some older BS-142 forms use 100× scaling — students must verify which standard the question uses).

**Step 5 — Pole (singular) behaviour at PSM → 1:**

As $\text{PSM} \to 1^{+}$, the denominator $\to 0^{+}$, hence $t_{\text{op}} \to \infty$ — physically correct, because at pickup there is no excess current to drive the timer.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem (illustrative):** A relay is set with PS = 100% on a 400/5 A CT. The relay uses Standard Inverse IEC characteristic with TMS = 0.3. A fault current of **3000 A** is detected. Compute the operating time.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Pickup current (primary):**

$$
I_p = 1.0 \times \frac{400}{5} \times 5 = 400 \;\text{A}
$$

(Equivalent: $I_p = 100\% \times I_{n,\text{CT, primary}} = 1 \times 400 = 400$ A.)

**Step 2 — Compute PSM:**

$$
\text{PSM} = \frac{I_F}{I_p} = \frac{3000}{400} = 7.5
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Standard Inverse equation:**

$$
t_{\text{op}} = \text{TMS} \times \frac{0.14}{\text{PSM}^{0.02} - 1}
$$

Numerator: $0.14 \times 0.3 = 0.042$.

Denominator: $7.5^{0.02} - 1$.

**Step 4 — Evaluate the exponential carefully:**

$$
\ln(7.5) = 1.9218
$$
$$
0.02 \times \ln(7.5) = 0.03844
$$
$$
7.5^{0.02} = e^{0.03844} = 1.03918
$$
$$
7.5^{0.02} - 1 = 0.03918
$$

**Step 5 — Final calculation:**

$$
t_{\text{op}} = \frac{0.042}{0.03918} = 1.072\;\text{s}
$$

**Conclusion:** The relay will trip the breaker in approximately **1.07 seconds** for this fault condition.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity Table

The table below shows how $t_{\text{op}}$ varies for the same relay (TMS = 0.3) under different fault levels — useful for *grading studies*.

| Fault Current $I_F$ (A) | PSM | Denominator $\text{PSM}^{0.02}-1$ | $t_{\text{op}}$ (s) |
|------------------------|-----|-----------------------------------|---------------------|
| 400 (pickup) | 1.00 | 0.0000 | $\infty$ (no trip) |
| 800 | 2.00 | $2^{0.02}-1 = 0.01395$ | $0.042 / 0.01395 = 3.011$ |
| 2000 | 5.00 | $5^{0.02}-1 = 0.03279$ | $0.042 / 0.03279 = 1.281$ |
| 3000 | 7.50 | $0.03918$ | $1.072$ |
| 5000 | 12.5 | $12.5^{0.02}-1 = 0.05110$ | $0.822$ |
| 10000 | 25.0 | $25^{0.02}-1 = 0.07179$ | $0.585$ |
| 20000 | 50.0 | $50^{0.02}-1 = 0.09061$ | $0.464$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The trend confirms the inverse-time behaviour: **as PSM increases, $t_{\text{op}}$ monotonically decreases**, asymptoting to a non-zero lower bound. The relay therefore never trips "instantaneously" in the IDMT band — that is the job of the *high-set* instantaneous element.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Simulink Model Architecture

The following **block-level functional architecture** represents the typical Simulink implementation of the overcurrent relay experiment. The complete model has six functional sub-blocks, all of which can be assembled using the *Simscape Electrical > Specialized Power Systems* library.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC1[Source 3Phase\n11 kV 50 Hz] --> LINE1
    LINE1[Transmission Line\nPi Section] --> BUS1
    BUS1[Bus Bar\nLoad Point] --> LOAD1
    LOAD1[Three Phase Load\nRL type] --> GND1[(Ground)]
    BUS1 --> FAULT1
    FAULT1[Three Phase Fault\nBlock] --> GND2[(Ground)]
    BUS1 --> CT1
    CT1[Current Transformer\n400 by 5 A] --> MEAS1
    MEAS1[Current Measurement\nRMS block] --> RELAY1
    RELAY1[Overcurrent Relay\nSubsystem] --> TRIP1
    TRIP1[Trip Logic\nComparator with latch] --> CBR1
    CBR1[Breaker Block\nThree Phase] --> ISOL1
    ISOL1[Fault Isolation] --> GND3[(Ground)]
    RELAY1 --> SCOPE1
    SCOPE1[Scope Display\nCurrent and Trip] --> USR1
    USR1[User Analyst\nObserves Trip Time]
    classDef power fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#a35d00,color:#000
    classDef prot fill:#c2e0ff,stroke:#00509e,color:#000
    classDef mon fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#2d7a2d,color:#000
    class SRC1,LINE1,BUS1,LOAD1,FAULT1 power
    class CT1,RELAY1,TRIP1,CBR1 prot
    class MEAS1,SCOPE1,ISOL1,USR1,GNDI mon
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Internal Logic of the Relay Subsystem

The **Overcurrent Relay Subsystem** is the heart of the experiment. Its internal logic is a four-stage pipeline: (i) RMS conversion, (ii) PSM calculation, (iii) IDMT curve evaluation, (iv) trip-pulse generation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN1[Input Isec from CT\nAnalog Signal] --> RMS1
    RMS1[RMS Conversion\nRunning Average 1 cycle] --> DIV1
    DIV1[Divide by Ip\nCompute PSM] --> COND1
    COND1{PSM greater than 1}
    COND1 -- No --> NOOP1[No Operation\nReset Timer]
    COND1 -- Yes --> POW1
    POW1[Compute PSM power alpha\nStandard Inv alpha equals 0.02] --> IDMT1
    IDMT1[IDMT Equation\nt equals 0.14 TMS over PSMpow minus 1] --> ACC1
    ACC1[Integrating Timer\nContinuous Time] --> CMP1
    CMP1{Accumulated t\nreaches top value}
    CMP1 -- No --> ACC1
    CMP1 -- Yes --> TRIP2
    TRIP2[Trip Pulse Output\n1 to Circuit Breaker] --> OUT1
    OUT1[Output Trip Command\nLatched] --> EXT1
    EXT1[External Breaker Coil\nOperates in 20 to 40 ms]
    classDef logic fill:#fff2b3,stroke:#7a5a00,color:#000
    classDef trip fill:#ffb3b3,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    class IN1,RMS1,DIV1,POW1,IDMT1,ACC1,CMP1,COND1,NOOP1 logic
    class TRIP2,OUT1,EXT1 trip
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Relay Operating States

Numerical relays are essentially **finite-state machines**. The relay cycles through four canonical states.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> STANDBY
    STANDBY --> MEASURING: Power ON
    MEASURING --> STANDBY: I less than Ip
    MEASURING --> TIMING: PSM greater than 1
    TIMING --> STANDBY: Fault cleared\nbefore trip
    TIMING --> TIMING: Accumulate dt
    TIMING --> TRIPPED: t equals top
    TRIPPED --> LOCKOUT: Manual reset\nrequired
    LOCKOUT --> STANDBY: Reset command
    TRIPPED --> STANDBY: Auto reclose\nafter tAR
    note right of TIMING: IDMT equation\ngoverns dt
    note right of TRIPPED: Latched output\nto breaker
```

## 4.4 Physical Schematic Description (Verbal Walk-Through)

For students who will eventually implement this on hardware (or read schematic diagrams in placement work), the physical wiring is as follows:

- A **3-phase 11 kV source** feeds a **π-section transmission line** (typically 50 km equivalent).
- A **three-phase fault block** is placed at the far end of the line to inject a programmable fault at any time.
- A **CT of ratio 400/5 A** is mounted on phase-A only (or all three phases for a comprehensive experiment) and its secondary winding is wired to the **relay coil terminal**.
- The **relay contact** (normally open) is wired in series with the **trip coil of a 3-phase circuit breaker** and a **110 V DC battery**.
- When the relay's trip logic closes its contact, current flows through the trip coil, the breaker opens, and a **scope** records both the RMS current waveform and the trip command waveform for verification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **MATLAB/Simulink**, the "relay coil" and "trip coil" are realised using **Comparator**, **Integrator**, and **Breaker** blocks from the *Simscape Electrical > Protection* sub-library. The IDMT equation is implemented inside a **MATLAB Function block** using the equation in SECTION 3.2.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]
**Define the term "Plug Setting Multiplier (PSM)" in the context of an overcurrent relay. Why is it a dimensionless quantity?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) The Plug Setting Multiplier (PSM) is defined as the ratio of the actual fault current flowing through the relay coil to the pickup (threshold) current setting of the relay.
- (1 mark) Mathematical form: $\text{PSM} = I_F / I_p$.
- (1 mark) It is dimensionless because both numerator and denominator are currents expressed in the same units (typically in amperes, or both referred to the CT secondary); the units cancel out, leaving a pure number that measures "how many times the pickup" the relay is being asked to clear.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]
**List any three standard inverse-time overcurrent relay characteristics defined by IEC 60255-151. State one typical application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Standard Inverse (SI):** $t = \text{TMS} \times 0.14 / (\text{PSM}^{0.02} - 1)$. Used for **general feeder protection** in distribution systems where fault current varies moderately.
- (1 mark) **Very Inverse (VI):** $t = \text{TMS} \times 13.5 / (\text{PSM}^{1.0} - 1)$. Used for **transformer and motor protection** where the relay must coordinate with fuses having steeper characteristics.
- (1 mark) **Extremely Inverse (EI):** $t = \text{TMS} \times 80.0 / (\text{PSM}^{2.0} - 1)$. Used for **cable and equipment** with very low short-time-withstand capability, where fast discrimination is essential.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE:** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**A 400 V, 50 Hz three-phase radial feeder is protected by an IDMT overcurrent relay set on the Standard Inverse IEC curve. The CT ratio is 400/5 A and the relay plug setting is 75%. The Time Multiplier Setting (TMS) is 0.2. A three-phase bolted fault occurs on the feeder such that the fault current is 4500 A.**

**(a)** Compute the operating time of the relay for this fault. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the same relay is required to coordinate with a downstream relay that operates in 0.5 s for a far-end fault, determine the minimum TMS setting on the upstream relay to maintain a grading margin of 0.4 s. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Operating Time

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the primary pickup current.
$$
I_p = \text{PS}_{\%} \times I_{n,\text{primary}} = 0.75 \times 400 = 300 \;\text{A}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the PSM.
$$
\text{PSM} = \frac{I_F}{I_p} = \frac{4500}{300} = 15
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Write the standard-inverse equation.
$$
t_{\text{op}} = \text{TMS} \times \frac{0.14}{\text{PSM}^{0.02} - 1}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Evaluate $\text{PSM}^{0.02}$ using logarithms.
$$
\ln(15) = 2.7081, \quad 0.02 \times 2.7081 = 0.05416
$$
$$
15^{0.02} = e^{0.05416} = 1.05566
$$
$$
15^{0.02} - 1 = 0.05566
$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Final numerical evaluation.
$$
t_{\text{op}} = 0.2 \times \frac{0.14}{0.05566} = 0.2 \times 2.5153 = 0.503\;\text{s}
$$

**Result (1 mark for clarity):** The relay will trip the circuit breaker in **≈ 0.50 seconds** for a 4500 A fault.

#### Part (b) — Coordination TMS

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The required upstream operating time is the downstream time plus grading margin:
$$
t_{\text{up,req}} = t_{\text{down}} + \Delta t_{\text{grade}} = 0.5 + 0.4 = 0.9\;\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Solve the standard-inverse equation for TMS:
$$
\text{TMS}_{\text{new}} = \frac{t_{\text{up,req}} \times (\text{PSM}^{0.02} - 1)}{0.14}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Insert the same PSM (we assume the same fault current reaches the upstream relay — typical for radial coordination at the *far-end fault current*).
$$
\text{TMS}_{\text{new}} = \frac{0.9 \times 0.05566}{0.14} = \frac{0.05009}{0.14} = 0.358
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Apply the engineering practice: TMS settings are typically available in discrete steps of 0.05. Round **up** to the next available step to *guarantee* the grading margin.
$$
\text{TMS}_{\text{recommended}} = 0.40
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark for interpretation):** With TMS = 0.40, the upstream relay will operate in:
$$
t_{\text{op,up}} = 0.40 \times 2.5153 = 1.006\;\text{s}
$$

This is **0.506 s** above the downstream trip — safely exceeding the 0.4 s grading margin. **Conclusion:** Set **TMS = 0.40** at the upstream relay.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary Exam, July 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an overcurrent relay implemented in MATLAB/Simulink. List the main blocks used and state the purpose of each.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the operating-time equation of the IEC 60255-151 Standard Inverse characteristic. A relay is set with TMS = 0.5 and a pickup of 5 A (secondary). Compute the operating times for fault currents of 10 A, 25 A, and 50 A. Comment on the nature of the resulting curve.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Block Diagram (7 marks)

| Block | Purpose | Library / Realisation |
|-------|---------|------------------------|
| **3-Phase Source** | Generates balanced 11 kV / 50 Hz supply. | *Simscape Electrical > Sources* |
| **Transmission Line (π-section)** | Provides line impedance (R, L, C). | *Elements > Three-Phase* |
| **Three-Phase Fault Block** | Programmable fault injection (timed). | *Elements > Three-Phase Fault* |
| **Current Transformer** | Steps down primary current to 5 A / 1 A secondary. | *Measurements > CT* |
| **Current Measurement (RMS)** | Extracts per-phase RMS magnitude. | *Measurements > RMS* |
| **MATLAB Function Block** | Implements the IDMT equation $t = 0.14\,\text{TMS}/(\text{PSM}^{0.02}-1)$. | *User-Defined Functions* |
| **Comparator & Integrator** | Accumulates elapsed time and compares with computed $t_{\text{op}}$. | *Simulink > Continuous* |
| **Breaker Block** | Opens the line on receipt of trip pulse. | *Elements > Circuit Breaker* |
| **Scope / Display** | Visualises current and trip waveforms. | *Sinks > Scope* |

**Mark distribution:** (2 marks) diagram; (3 marks) block-wise purpose; (2 marks) library / real-world correspondence.

#### Part (b) — Derivation & Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Derivation (3 marks):** Already completed in SECTION 3.2 above. Key result to be reproduced by student:
$$
t_{\text{op}} = \text{TMS} \times \frac{0.14}{\text{PSM}^{0.02} - 1}
$$

**Numerical Calculations (3 marks):**

| $I_F$ (A) | PSM $= I_F/I_p$ | $\text{PSM}^{0.02} - 1$ | $t_{\text{op}} = 0.5 \times 0.14 / (\text{PSM}^{0.02} - 1)$ |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------|
| 10 | 2.0 | $2^{0.02} - 1 = 0.01395$ | $0.5 \times 0.14 / 0.01395 = 5.018$ s |
| 25 | 5.0 | $5^{0.02} - 1 = 0.03279$ | $0.5 \times 0.14 / 0.03279 = 2.135$ s |
| 50 | 10.0 | $10^{0.02} - 1 = 0.04713$ | $0.5 \times 0.14 / 0.04713 = 1.485$ s |

**Comment on the curve (1 mark):** As the fault current increases, the operating time *monotonically decreases* following an inverse-time (specifically, almost logarithmic) shape. This is the hallmark of the **Standard Inverse** characteristic — it discriminates between overload (long time) and short-circuit (short time) conditions automatically, without operator intervention.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the pickup current is referred to the CT secondary** — many students blindly multiply PS% with the primary rated current, losing **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Using the BS-142 constants in an IEC question (or vice-versa).** BS-142 uses $t = 0.14 \times \text{TMS} \times (1/(\text{PSM}^{0.02}-1))$; the IEC form is identical in the standard case but uses **TMS directly inside the multiplier** — students who misplace TMS lose **1 mark**.
> 3. **In IDMT numerics, students often write $1 - \text{PSM}^{0.02}$ instead of $\text{PSM}^{0.02} - 1$**, giving *negative* trip times. Always verify PSM > 1 before applying the formula.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert `log` bases when computing $x^{0.02}$** — using base-10 logarithm in exponent and base-$e$ elsewhere leads to a **factor-of-2.303** error.
> 5. **In Simulink, omitting the RMS block and using instantaneous current directly** — the relay will trip on DC transients rather than the true RMS, producing incorrect operating times.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ☐ **PSM** is dimensionless: $\text{PSM} = I_F / I_p$.
- ☐ **Pickup current** is always a *secondary* value unless explicitly stated; convert to primary via CT ratio when needed.
- ☐ **TMS** is a *dimensionless* lever; it scales the *entire* IDMT curve vertically.
- ☐ The IEC 60255-151 **standard inverse** is $t = 0.14\,\text{TMS}/(\text{PSM}^{0.02}-1)$.
- ☐ The **very inverse** uses $A = 13.5,\ \alpha = 1.0$.
- ☐ The **extremely inverse** uses $A = 80.0,\ \alpha = 2.0$.
- ☐ At PSM = 1, the relay is *at* pickup — theoretically infinite time → effectively no trip.
- ☐ The **discriminative ratio** $\text{DR} = I_{F,\min}/I_{p,\max}$ must be $\geq 2$ for IDMT coordination to work.
- ☐ **Grading margin** between upstream and downstream relays is typically $0.3 - 0.4$ s, accounting for breaker operating time, relay overrun, and CT误差.
- ☐ In Simulink, the *MATLAB Function* block is used to implement the IDMT equation; the *Breaker* block models the actual switch.
- ☐ The instantaneous (high-set) element is a *separate* parallel path — it is *not* part of the IDMT curve.
- ☐ PS% standard values: 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 175, 200.
- ☐ TMS standard range: 0.05 to 1.10 in steps of 0.05.
- ☐ CT secondary standard: **1 A** (preferred for numerical relays) or **5 A** (electromechanical / static).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Transfer Function from Block Diagram of Closed Loop System and Plot Pole Zero Graph
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL607 - ELECTRICAL SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Transfer Function from Block Diagram of Closed Loop System and Plot Pole Zero Graph

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Control Systems Engineering, a **Block Diagram** is a graphical representation of a system in which the major functional components are represented as rectangular blocks interconnected by directed lines that indicate the direction of signal flow. Each block represents a **Transfer Function** — the mathematical relationship between the input and output of that subsystem in the **Laplace domain (s-domain)**.

A **Closed-Loop Control System** is a feedback system in which the output is sampled, fed back, and compared with a reference input to produce an **Actuating Error Signal**. The control action is therefore a function of both the desired output and the actual output, providing **automatic error correction**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The **Overall Transfer Function (T.F.)** of a closed-loop system is defined as the ratio of the Laplace Transform of the output response, $C(s)$, to the Laplace Transform of the reference input, $R(s)$, with all initial conditions assumed to be zero:
> $$T(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)}$$

The **Pole-Zero Plot** (also called the **s-plane map** or **pole-zero map**) is a graphical representation of the locations of the poles and zeros of a transfer function in the complex **s-plane**, where the real part is the **horizontal axis ($\sigma$)** and the imaginary part is the **vertical axis ($j\omega$)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car and trying to maintain a constant speed of **60 km/h** on a highway:

- **The Car (Plant/Process):** The actual vehicle you are controlling.
- **The Speedometer (Feedback Element):** Continuously tells you the *actual* speed.
- **Your Eyes (Comparator/Summing Junction):** Compare the *desired* speed (60 km/h) with the *actual* speed.
- **The Accelerator Pedal (Controller):** Pressed harder or softer based on the **error** (difference between desired and actual).

> **The closed-loop system works exactly like this driver:** it *continuously senses* the output, *compares* it with the reference, and *adjusts* the input to minimize the error. This is the essence of **negative feedback**.

For the **pole-zero plot**, think of a **GPS map**:
- **Poles** are like "danger zones" or "attractors" — the system response is *pulled* toward these locations.
- **Zeros** are like "neutral zones" — they shape the response but don't dictate stability.
- If a pole lands in the **right-half plane (RHP)**, the response grows unbounded — the car crashes (unstable system).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Steady-State Error ($e_{ss}$):** Measured in same units as output
> - **Damping Ratio ($\zeta$):** Dimensionless, range $[0, 1]$ for underdamped systems
> - **Natural Frequency ($\omega_n$):** Measured in **rad/s**
> - **Settling Time ($t_s$):** Measured in **seconds**, $t_s \approx \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ (2% criterion)
> - **Standard frequency for s-plane:** $s = \sigma + j\omega$, where $\sigma$ and $\omega$ are in **rad/s**
