# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block Diagram Components — Structured Logic

A block diagram consists of four fundamental elements:

1. **Block:** Represents a transfer function $G(s)$. The output is the input multiplied by the transfer function: $Y(s) = G(s) \cdot X(s)$.

2. **Summing Junction (Comparator):** A circular element with input arrows and signs (+ or −). It algebraically sums incoming signals to produce the error signal: $E(s) = R(s) \pm B(s)$.

3. **Take-off Point (Branch Point):** A point from which a signal is taken and sent to multiple destinations without any modification.

4. **Arrow (Signal Flow):** A directed line indicating the unidirectional flow of signals from one point to another.

## 2.2 Block Diagram Reduction — Step-by-Step Logic

The **"Why"** behind block diagram reduction: A complex interconnected system becomes analytically tractable when expressed as a **single equivalent transfer function**.

**The "How"** — Canonical reduction rules:

- **Blocks in Cascade (Series):** $G_{eq}(s) = G_1(s) \cdot G_2(s) \cdot G_3(s)$
- **Blocks in Parallel:** $G_{eq}(s) = G_1(s) + G_2(s) + G_3(s)$
- **Feedback Loop (Closed-Loop):** 
  - **Negative Feedback:** $G_{eq}(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$
  - **Positive Feedback:** $G_{eq}(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 - G(s)H(s)}$
- **Moving a Take-off Point Ahead of a Block:** Divide by the block's transfer function
- **Moving a Take-off Point Behind a Block:** Multiply by the block's transfer function
- **Moving a Summing Junction Ahead of a Block:** Add a compensating block in parallel
- **Moving a Summing Junction Behind a Block:** Add a compensating block in series

## 2.3 Mason's Gain Formula (Signal Flow Graph Method)

For complex block diagrams that resist direct reduction, **Mason's Gain Formula** provides a systematic alternative:

$$T(s) = \frac{1}{\Delta} \sum_{k=1}^{N} P_k \Delta_k$$

Where:
- $P_k$ = Path gain of the $k^{th}$ forward path
- $\Delta$ = Determinant of the graph: $\Delta = 1 - \sum L_i + \sum L_i L_j - \sum L_i L_j L_k + \ldots$
- $\Delta_k$ = Cofactor of the $k^{th}$ path (determinant with loops touching $P_k$ removed)
- $L_i$ = Individual loop gain

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — MUST MEMORIZE FOR KTU EXAMS**

| **#** | **Configuration** | **Transfer Function** | **Key Condition** |
|:-----:|:------------------|:----------------------|:------------------|
| 1 | Unity Negative Feedback | $\dfrac{G(s)}{1+G(s)}$ | $H(s) = 1$ |
| 2 | Non-Unity Negative Feedback | $\dfrac{G(s)}{1+G(s)H(s)}$ | General closed loop |
| 3 | Unity Positive Feedback | $\dfrac{G(s)}{1-G(s)}$ | $H(s) = 1$ |
| 4 | Cascade of $n$ blocks | $\prod_{i=1}^{n} G_i(s)$ | No loading effect |
| 5 | Parallel sum | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} G_i(s)$ | Same input, outputs summed |
| 6 | Characteristic Equation | $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ | Roots = Closed-loop poles |
| 7 | Characteristic Polynomial (deg n) | $a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \ldots + a_n$ | Roots determine stability |
| 8 | Pole from factor $(s - p)$ | $s = p$ is a pole | System unstable if $\text{Re}(p) > 0$ |
| 9 | Zero from factor $(s - z)$ | $s = z$ is a zero | Zeros affect transient shape |
| 10 | Settling Time | $t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ | 2% tolerance band |
| 11 | Peak Overshoot | $M_p = e^{-\zeta\pi / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \times 100\%$ | Damped oscillation |
| 12 | Damped Frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$ | Imaginary part of pole |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TIP:** Always remember that for a closed-loop system, the **poles of the closed-loop system** are the **roots of the characteristic equation** $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$, **NOT** the poles of the open-loop $G(s)H(s)$. This is the single most common mistake students make in KTU board exams.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where this knowledge is applied:**
> - **Cruise Control Systems** in automobiles
> - **Servo Motor Positioning** in CNC machines and robotics
> - **Voltage Regulators** in power electronics (SMPS, AVRs)
> - **PID Controllers** in process industries (temperature, flow, pressure control)
> - **Aircraft Autopilot Systems** (roll, pitch, yaw stabilization)
> - **Active Suspension Systems** in luxury vehicles
> - **Inverted Pendulum Balancing** in robotics research

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Closed-Loop Transfer Function (Negative Feedback)

**Given:** A standard negative feedback loop with forward path transfer function $G(s)$ and feedback path transfer function $H(s)$.

**Reference Input:** $R(s)$ | **Output:** $C(s)$ | **Feedback Signal:** $B(s)$ | **Error Signal:** $E(s)$

### Step 1: Define the Error Signal

The output of the summing junction (comparator) is the difference between the reference input and the feedback signal. For negative feedback:

$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

### Step 2: Express the Output through Forward Path

The output is the error signal multiplied by the forward path transfer function:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

### Step 3: Express the Feedback Signal

The feedback signal is the output multiplied by the feedback transfer function:

$$B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

### Step 4: Substitute Equation 3 into Equation 1

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

### Step 5: Substitute Equation 4 into Equation 2

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot \left[ R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s) \right]$$

### Step 6: Expand the Right-Hand Side

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s) - G(s) H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

### Step 7: Collect $C(s)$ Terms on the Left

$$C(s) + G(s) H(s) \cdot C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

$$C(s) \cdot \left[ 1 + G(s) H(s) \right] = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

### Step 8: Solve for the Transfer Function

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

> **Final Result:** This is the canonical closed-loop transfer function for a **negative feedback** system with feedback element $H(s)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The denominator $1 + G(s)H(s)$ is called the **Return Difference**. When $G(s)H(s)$ is large in magnitude (i.e., $\vert G(s)H(s) \vert \gg 1$), the closed-loop transfer function approximates to $\dfrac{1}{H(s)}$, making the system behavior largely independent of the forward path — a property known as **robustness** in feedback theory.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Reduction of a Multi-Loop Block Diagram

**Problem:** Find the overall transfer function $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)}$ for the following system:
- $G_1 = \dfrac{1}{s+1}$, $G_2 = \dfrac{2}{s+2}$, $G_3 = \dfrac{1}{s}$, $H_1 = 1$, $H_2 = 2$

**System Architecture:** $G_1$ and $G_2$ are in cascade; this combination is in an inner feedback loop with $H_1$; output goes through $G_3$ and is fed back through $H_2$ to the input.

### Step 1: Combine $G_1$ and $G_2$ in Cascade

$$G_{12}(s) = G_1(s) \cdot G_2(s) = \frac{1}{s+1} \cdot \frac{2}{s+2} = \frac{2}{(s+1)(s+2)}$$

### Step 2: Reduce the Inner Feedback Loop (with $G_{12}$ and $H_1$)

$$G_{inner}(s) = \frac{G_{12}(s)}{1 + G_{12}(s) H_1(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{2}{(s+1)(s+2)}}{1 + \dfrac{2}{(s+1)(s+2)}}$$

### Step 3: Simplify the Denominator

$$G_{inner}(s) = \frac{2}{(s+1)(s+2) + 2} = \frac{2}{s^2 + 3s + 2 + 2} = \frac{2}{s^2 + 3s + 4}$$

### Step 4: Form the Outer Loop with $G_3$ and $H_2$

The forward path of the outer loop is $G_{inner}(s) \cdot G_3(s)$:

$$G_{forward}(s) = \frac{2}{s^2 + 3s + 4} \cdot \frac{1}{s} = \frac{2}{s(s^2 + 3s + 4)}$$

### Step 5: Apply the Negative Feedback Formula

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_{forward}(s)}{1 + G_{forward}(s) H_2(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{2}{s(s^2+3s+4)}}{1 + \dfrac{4}{s(s^2+3s+4)}}$$

### Step 6: Combine and Simplify

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{2}{s(s^2 + 3s + 4) + 4} = \frac{2}{s^3 + 3s^2 + 4s + 4}$$

### Step 7: Identify Poles and Zeros

**Numerator:** Constant 2 → **No finite zeros** (a zero at $s = \infty$)

**Denominator (Characteristic Equation):** $s^3 + 3s^2 + 4s + 4 = 0$

Using numerical methods (e.g., MATLAB `roots([1 3 4 4])`):
- Pole 1: $s_1 = -2.4567$
- Pole 2: $s_2 = -0.2717 + j\,1.1907$
- Pole 3: $s_3 = -0.2717 - j\,1.1907$

> **Stability Conclusion:** All three poles have **negative real parts** (left-half plane), so the system is **STABLE**. The complex conjugate pair indicates **underdamped oscillatory** response.

## 3.3 Pole-Zero Plot Interpretation Rules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam — Pole-Zero Plot Quick Reference:**

| **Pole Location** | **System Behavior** | **Stability** |
|:------------------|:--------------------|:--------------|
| LHP Real axis | Exponential decay | Stable |
| LHP Complex pair | Damped oscillation | Stable |
| Origin ($s = 0$) | Marginal / Integrator | Marginally stable |
| RHP Real axis | Exponential growth | **UNSTABLE** |
| RHP Complex pair | Growing oscillation | **UNSTABLE** |
| Repeated pole on $j\omega$ axis | Sustained oscillation | Marginally stable |
| Pole at $s = 0$ repeated | Ramp growth | **UNSTABLE** |

| **Zero Location** | **Effect on Response** |
|:------------------|:-----------------------|
| LHP Real axis | Reduces overshoot in step response |
| LHP Complex pair | Skewed transient shape (lead behavior) |
| RHP Real axis | **Non-minimum phase** — inverse response, undershoot |
| RHP Complex pair | **Non-minimum phase** — complex transient behavior |
| Zero at origin | Perfect derivative action |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Standard Closed-Loop Block Diagram (Mermaid Schematic)

The following diagram represents the canonical negative feedback closed-loop architecture, which is the foundational structure for KTU Module Experiment 2.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[REF R OF S] --> B[SUMMING JUNCTION NEG FB]
    B --> C[ERROR E OF S]
    C --> D[FORWARD G OF S]
    D --> E[OUTPUT C OF S]
    E --> F[FEEDBACK H OF S]
    F --> B
    E --> G[POLE ZERO PLOT GENERATOR]
    G --> H[MATLAB PZMAP TOOL]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The reference input $R(s)$ enters from the left.
> - The summing junction subtracts the feedback signal.
> - The error $E(s)$ drives the forward path $G(s)$.
> - The output $C(s)$ is tapped and passed through $H(s)$ to be subtracted from the reference.
> - The output is also fed to the pole-zero analysis tool.
