# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the Root Locus and state the two conditions that govern it.

**Model Answer:**
Root locus is the locus of all possible closed-loop pole locations in the s-plane as the gain **K** varies from **0 to +∞**. The two governing conditions are:
1. **Angle condition:** $\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm 180°(2q+1)$
2. **Magnitude condition:** $\vert G(s)H(s) \vert = 1$

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] State any four rules for constructing the root locus of a system.

**Model Answer:**
1. The root locus is symmetric about the real axis.
2. The number of branches equals the number of open-loop poles **n**.
3. The locus starts (K = 0) at the open-loop poles and ends (K → ∞) at the open-loop zeros.
4. The real-axis segments lie to the left of an odd total number of real poles and real zeros.
5. Asymptote angles are given by $\theta_a = (2q+1)\cdot 180°/(n-m)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**For the unity-feedback system** with open-loop transfer function

$$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+4)(s+6)}$$

**(a)** Sketch the root locus and determine the **range of K** for stability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the value of **K** for a damping ratio **ζ = 0.6** and compute the **settling time (2% criterion)** and **percentage peak overshoot**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Open-loop poles:** s = 0, s = −4, s = −6 → n = 3, m = 0
**Step 2: Characteristic equation:** $s^3 + 10s^2 + 24s + K = 0$
**Step 3: Routh array:**

| s³ | 1 | 24 |
|---|---|---|
| s² | 10 | K |
| s¹ | (240 − K)/10 | 0 |
| s⁰ | K | |

For stability: $0 < K < 240$
**Step 4: Centroid:** $\sigma_a = (0 - 4 - 6)/3 = -10/3 \approx -3.33$
**Step 5: Asymptotes:** $\theta_a = 60°, 180°, 300°$
**Step 6: jω crossing** at K = 240: $10s^2 + 240 = 0 \Rightarrow s = \pm j\sqrt{24} = \pm j4.899$ rad/s

**Range of K for stability:** $0 < K < 240$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** The damping-ratio line is at angle $\beta = \cos^{-1}(0.6) \approx 53.13°$ from the negative real axis.

**Step 2:** Using MATLAB's `rlocfind` or iterative method, the locus intersects the ζ = 0.6 line at approximately **s = −1.2 ± j1.6**.

**Step 3:** Verify using magnitude condition:

$$K = \vert s \vert \cdot \vert s + 4 \vert \cdot \vert s + 6 \vert = (2) \cdot (3.4) \cdot (5.12) \approx 34.8$$

**Step 4:** Natural frequency: $\omega_n = \sqrt{1.44 + 2.56} = \sqrt{4} = 2$ rad/s

**Step 5:** Settling time (2%): $t_s = 4/(\zeta \omega_n) = 4/(0.6 \cdot 2) = 3.33$ s

**Step 6:** Peak overshoot: $M_p = 100 \cdot e^{-\pi(0.6)/\sqrt{1-0.36}} = 100 \cdot e^{-2.356} \approx 9.48\%$

**Answer:** $K \approx 34.8, \quad t_s \approx 3.33$ s, $\quad M_p \approx 9.48\%$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**For the unity-feedback system** with

$$G(s) = \frac{K(s+2)}{s(s+1)(s+3)}$$

**(a)** Sketch the complete root locus, list the **asymptote angles, centroid, and breakaway points**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Comment on the **stability** of the system and find the **value of K** when the system becomes marginally stable. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Poles and zeros:** Poles: 0, −1, −3 (n = 3). Zero: −2 (m = 1).
**Step 2 — Number of asymptotes:** n − m = 2.
**Step 3 — Asymptote angles:** $\theta_a = \pm 90°$ (i.e., 90° and 270°).
**Step 4 — Centroid:** $\sigma_a = [(0) + (-1) + (-3) - (-2)]/2 = (-2)/2 = -1$.
**Step 5 — Breakaway points:** Solve $dK/ds = 0$ from $K = -s(s+1)(s+3)/(s+2)$.
After algebraic simplification, the breakaway point lies **between s = −1 and s = 0** at approximately **s ≈ −0.555**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Characteristic equation:** $s(s+1)(s+3) + K(s+2) = 0$
$\Rightarrow s^3 + 4s^2 + (3+K)s + 2K = 0$

**Step 2 — Routh array:**

| s³ | 1 | 3+K |
|---|---|---|
| s² | 4 | 2K |
| s¹ | (12 + 2K − 2K)/4 = 3 | 0 |
| s⁰ | 2K | |

**Step 3 — Analysis:** The s¹ row is constant (= 3) and independent of K. This means the locus **never crosses the jω axis**; the system is **stable for all K > 0**.

**Step 4 — Marginal stability:** No finite K causes the system to become marginally stable. The system remains stable for $K \in (0, \infty)$.

**Conclusion:** This is an **inherently stable** configuration (the zero in the LHP "pulls" the branches away from the imaginary axis).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU ESE:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "odd number" rule** for real-axis segments — students often include the entire real axis. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Centroid formula sign error** — the formula is $\sum p_i - \sum z_j$ over $(n-m)$, not the other way. Reversing the sign shifts the centroid to the wrong side. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 3. **Asymptote angle range** — students compute one asymptote angle and stop. You **must** list all $(n-m)$ angles for full credit. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 4. **Wrong breakaway sign** — breakaway points are roots of $dK/ds = 0$, but only those that yield **K > 0** are valid. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 5. **Mixing up the jω crossing method** — students often substitute **s = jω** and forget to take the **real and imaginary parts** separately. (Loss: 2 marks)
> 6. **Forgetting the dominant-pole assumption** — when computing %Mp and ts for higher-order systems, students must verify that the non-dominant poles are at least 5× farther in the LHP. (Loss: 1–2 marks)

---

## 5.4 MATLAB / Scilab Reference Snippets for the Lab

> [!NOTE]
> These commands are part of the **simulation lab workflow** for the root-locus experiment. They are tools, not theory, and are required for the **practical record / viva**.

| Step | MATLAB Command | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `num = [K]; den = conv(conv([1 0],[1 4]),[1 6]);` | Build numerator/denominator |
| 2 | `sys = tf(num, den);` | Form transfer function object |
| 3 | `rlocus(sys);` | Plot the root locus |
| 4 | `sgrid(0.6, 2);` | Overlay ζ = 0.6 and ωn = 2 rad/s grid |
| 5 | `[K, poles] = rlocfind(sys);` | Interactive gain selection |
| 6 | `step(feedback(sys, 1));` | Step response of closed-loop |
| 7 | `stepinfo(feedback(sys,1))` | Extract Mp, ts, tr, ess automatically |

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Root locus = trajectory of closed-loop poles as K varies from 0 → ∞.**
- **Two conditions:** angle (odd multiple of 180°) and magnitude (= 1).
- **Start points (K=0):** open-loop poles (X). **End points (K→∞):** open-loop zeros (O), with extra branches going to infinity.
- **Number of branches = n** (number of open-loop poles).
- **Symmetric about real axis** — always.
- **Real-axis rule:** locus exists to the left of an **odd** number of real poles + real zeros.
- **Asymptote angles:** $\theta_a = (2q+1)\cdot 180°/(n-m)$ for $q = 0, 1, \dots, n-m-1$.
- **Centroid:** $\sigma_a = (\sum p_i - \sum z_j)/(n-m)$.
- **Breakaway points:** roots of $dK/ds = 0$ that give $K > 0$ and lie on valid real-axis segments.
- **jω-axis crossing** → use **Routh–Hurwitz** to find critical K; this is the **marginal stability** point.
- **Angle of departure** from a complex pole uses the angle condition centered at that pole.
- **Magnitude condition** gives K for a chosen test point: $K = \prod |s-p_i| / \prod |s-z_j|$.
- **Performance metrics from pole location:**
  - Settling time $t_s \approx 4/(\zeta\omega_n)$
  - Peak overshoot $M_p = 100 \cdot e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$
  - Rise time $t_r \approx 1.8/\omega_n$
- **MATLAB essentials:** `rlocus`, `sgrid`, `rlocfind`, `step`, `stepinfo`.
- **Stability check:** all closed-loop poles in LHP → locus entirely in LHP.
- **jω crossing into RHP** = unstable for that K; never let your locus cross the imaginary axis for a practical design.

> [!TIP] **Last-Minute KTU Viva Tip**
> If the examiner asks, *"Why does the locus start at poles and end at zeros?"* — answer: *"At K = 0, the closed-loop equation reduces to $1 = 0$ when $s = p_i$, so the poles of the closed-loop system coincide with the open-loop poles. As $K \to \infty$, the equation $KG(s)H(s) = -1$ is satisfied only when the denominator terms dominate, which corresponds to the open-loop zeros."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Lead/Lag/Lead-Lag Compensator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL607 - ELECTRICAL SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Lead/Lag/Lead-Lag Compensator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **compensator** is an auxiliary control element inserted in the forward path (cascade compensation) or feedback path of a closed-loop control system to reshape its frequency response or root locus so that the system meets prescribed **transient response** and **steady-state error** specifications. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the three canonical compensators studied are:

- **Lead Compensator** — a network whose transfer function contributes **positive phase** to the open-loop system, typically used to improve the **Phase Margin (PM)** and speed up the transient response.
- **Lag Compensator** — a network that provides **negative phase** and **high-frequency attenuation** to boost low-frequency gain, primarily used to reduce the **steady-state error** without significantly disturbing the transient response.
- **Lead-Lag Compensator** — a composite network that combines the phase-lead and phase-lag characteristics to simultaneously improve transient response and steady-state accuracy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCEOL607):** The student is expected to *design* these compensators using frequency-response (Bode) and root-locus techniques in MATLAB/Simulink and verify the time-domain performance — not merely state their transfer functions.

### 1.2 Standard Transfer Function Forms

The general first-order compensator is written in the unity-DC-gain zero-pole form:

$$
G_{c}(s) = K_{c}\,\frac{1 + aTs}{1 + Ts}
$$

where $a > 1$ gives a **Lead** network, $a < 1$ gives a **Lag** network, and $K_{c}$ is the compensator gain needed to satisfy the static error constant.

Equivalently, in zero-pole form:

$$
G_{c}(s) = K_{c}\,\frac{s + z_{c}}{s + p_{c}}
$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Steering a Car**
> 
> - A **Lead Compensator** behaves like a *sharper, more responsive steering* — when you turn the wheel a little, the car reacts more eagerly (more phase lead = faster response, smaller overshoot). It is a "predictive boost."
> - A **Lag Compensator** behaves like a *slow, patient cruise-control correction* — it gently pushes the car back to the right speed without jerking it (reduces steady-state speed error). It is a "long-term correction."
> - A **Lead-Lag Compensator** is a *complete driver* — sharp steering for the curves (transient) and patient accelerator for the long straight road (steady-state).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Exams:** **"Lead = Lead (move ahead) in phase ⇒ faster response"** and **"Lag = Lag in error ⇒ reduces steady-state error."**

### 1.4 Physical Realisation (for context)

Physically, the **RC lead network** is a voltage divider with a series capacitor and shunt resistor, while the **RC lag network** has a series resistor and shunt capacitor. In the laboratory, students build these on a breadboard; in PCEOL607, the "lab" is simulation-based using MATLAB/Simulink.

| Parameter | **Symbol** | **Standard Value/Range** |
|---|---|---|
| Compensation ratio (Lead) | $\alpha$ | $\alpha > 1$ (typically $2 \le \alpha \le 10$) |
| Compensation ratio (Lag) | $\beta$ | $\beta > 1$ (typically $5 \le \beta \le 20$) |
| Phase Margin target | $PM$ | $40^{\circ} \text{ to } 60^{\circ}$ |
| Gain Crossover Frequency | $\omega_{gc}$ | rad/s |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Lead Compensator — Operational Breakdown

A lead compensator is a **zero-before-pole** network on the real axis. Because the zero is *closer to the origin* than the pole, at the geometric mean frequency $\omega_{m} = \sqrt{1/(\alpha T^{2})}$, the network contributes its **maximum phase lead** $\phi_{m}$.

**Operational logic (Why it works):**
1. The compensator adds a **zero** that pulls the root locus to the **left** (improving damping and speed).
2. It adds a **positive phase** at the gain-crossover frequency, raising the **Phase Margin**.
3. It **increases the bandwidth**, which makes the closed-loop response faster.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Effect of Lead Compensation:**
> - ↑ Phase Margin (PM)
> - ↑ Bandwidth ($\omega_{b}$)
> - ↓ Rise time ($t_{r}$)
> - ↓ Overshoot ($M_{p}$)
> - ↕ Steady-state error (largely unchanged — this is the lead compensator's limitation)

### 2.2 The Lag Compensator — Operational Breakdown

A lag compensator is a **pole-before-zero** network, with the pole closer to the origin. It contributes **negative phase** but provides **magnitude attenuation** at high frequencies.

**Operational logic (Why it works):**
1. The attenuation at high frequencies allows the **gain $K$ to be increased** to satisfy a static error constant ($K_{p}, K_{v}, K_{a}$).
2. The zero is placed very close to the pole (and near the origin) so that their phase contributions nearly cancel near the gain-crossover.
3. Result: **higher low-frequency gain** ⇒ lower steady-state error, with the crossover frequency only mildly shifted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Effect of Lag Compensation:**
> - ↓ Steady-state error ($e_{ss}$)
> - ↕ Phase Margin (slightly reduced — sometimes a small lead section is added to compensate)
> - ↓ Bandwidth (slightly)
> - ↑ Settling time (slightly — acceptable trade-off)

### 2.3 The Lead-Lag Compensator

It is a cascade of the two sections:

$$
G_{c}(s) = G_{\text{lead}}(s) \cdot G_{\text{lag}}(s) = K_{c}\,\frac{(1+\alpha T_{1}s)(1+\beta T_{2}s)}{(1+T_{1}s)(1+T_{2}s)}
$$

where $\alpha > 1$ and $\beta > 1$. The **lead section** shapes the transient response; the **lag section** provides the steady-state accuracy.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Meaning / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compensator TF (general) | $G_{c}(s) = K_{c}(1 + aTs)/(1 + Ts)$ | $a > 1$ → lead, $a < 1$ → lag |
| 2 | Maximum phase lead (Lead) | $\sin\phi_{m} = \dfrac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha + 1}$ | $\phi_{m}$ in degrees |
| 3 | Frequency of max phase | $\omega_{m} = \dfrac{1}{T\sqrt{\alpha}}$ | rad/s |
| 4 | Attenuation at $\omega_{m}$ (Lead) | $\vert G_{c}(j\omega_{m})\vert_{\text{dB}} = -10\log_{10}\alpha$ | dB (loss to be made up) |
| 5 | New gain crossover (Lead) | $\vert G(j\omega_{c}')\vert_{\text{dB}} = +10\log_{10}\alpha$ | dB (uncompensated drop) |
| 6 | Time constant (Lead) | $T = 1/(\omega_{m}\sqrt{\alpha})$ | seconds |
| 7 | Lag compensator ratio | $\beta = \dfrac{Z_{c}}{P_{c}} \gg 1$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Lag zero placement rule | $\omega_{z} = 1/T_{2} \approx 0.1\,\omega_{c}'$ | "decade below" rule |
| 9 | Lag pole placement | $\omega_{p} = \omega_{z}/\beta = 1/(\beta T_{2})$ | rad/s |
| 10 | Static error velocity const. | $K_{v} = \lim_{s\to 0} s\,G(s)G_{c}(s)$ | 1/s |
| 11 | Required $K_{v}$ | $K_{v,\text{req}} = (ess_{\text{desired}})^{-1}$ | 1/s |
| 12 | Required additional gain | $\Delta = K_{v,\text{req}}/K_{v,\text{old}}$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | Bandwidth | $\omega_{b} : \vert T(j\omega_{b})\vert = -3\text{ dB}$ | rad/s |
| 14 | Phase Margin | $PM = 180^{\circ} + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$ | degrees |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall in KTU Valuation:** In the lead compensator formula $\sin\phi_{m} = (\alpha - 1)/(\alpha + 1)$, the angle $\phi_{m}$ is the **maximum phase lead** — *not* the additional phase margin required. The required lead $\phi_{m} = PM_{\text{desired}} - PM_{\text{old}} + 5^{\circ}\text{ to } 12^{\circ}$ (compensation margin to account for the magnitude-curve shift).

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

Lead/Lag/Lead-Lag compensators are the **backbone of industrial control design**:
- **Servo motor position control** (robotic arms, CNC machines) → lead for fast response.
- **Antenna pointing systems, gun-turret control** → lag for precision steady-state pointing.
- **Aircraft autopilot** → lead-lag to simultaneously achieve agility and steady flight path.
- **DC motor speed control** (your PCEOL607 lab) → all three are designed and simulated in MATLAB.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Maximum Phase Lead of a Lead Compensator

The unity-gain lead compensator is:

$$
G_{c}(j\omega) = \frac{1 + j\alpha T\omega}{1 + jT\omega}
$$

The phase angle at any frequency is:

$$
\phi(\omega) = \tan^{-1}(\alpha T\omega) - \tan^{-1}(T\omega)
$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate $\phi(\omega)$ with respect to $\omega$ and set to zero for maximum:**

$$
\frac{d\phi}{d\omega} = \frac{\alpha T}{1 + (\alpha T\omega)^{2}} - \frac{T}{1 + (T\omega)^{2}} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Solve the resulting algebraic identity:**

$$
\frac{\alpha T}{1 + \alpha^{2} T^{2} \omega^{2}} = \frac{T}{1 + T^{2} \omega^{2}}
$$

Cross-multiplying and cancelling $T$:

$$
\alpha\bigl(1 + T^{2}\omega^{2}\bigr) = 1 + \alpha^{2} T^{2}\omega^{2}
$$

$$
\alpha + \alpha T^{2}\omega^{2} = 1 + \alpha^{2} T^{2}\omega^{2}
$$

$$
(\alpha - 1) = (\alpha^{2} - \alpha) T^{2}\omega^{2} = \alpha(\alpha - 1) T^{2}\omega^{2}
$$

Since $\alpha \neq 1$, cancel $(\alpha - 1)$:

$$
1 = \alpha T^{2}\omega_{m}^{2} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \omega_{m} = \frac{1}{T\sqrt{\alpha}}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $\phi(\omega_{m})$:**

$$
\tan\phi_{m} = \frac{\alpha T\omega_{m} - T\omega_{m}}{1 + \alpha T^{2}\omega_{m}^{2}}
$$

Substituting $\omega_{m} = 1/(T\sqrt{\alpha})$:

$$
\tan\phi_{m} = \frac{\alpha/\sqrt{\alpha} - 1/\sqrt{\alpha}}{1 + \alpha} = \frac{(\alpha - 1)/\sqrt{\alpha}}{1 + \alpha} = \frac{\alpha - 1}{2\sqrt{\alpha}}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to the half-angle / standard form:**

Using $\sin\phi_{m} = \dfrac{\tan\phi_{m}}{\sqrt{1 + \tan^{2}\phi_{m}}}$ and $\tan^{2}\phi_{m} = \dfrac{(\alpha-1)^{2}}{4\alpha}$:

$$
\sin\phi_{m} = \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha + 1}
$$

**Final result:**

$$
\boxed{\;\sin\phi_{m} = \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha + 1},\qquad \omega_{m} = \frac{1}{T\sqrt{\alpha}}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **central design equation** of the lead compensator — it relates the maximum phase lead directly to the compensation ratio $\alpha$. It will be used in *every* KTU numerical problem.

### 3.2 Magnitude of Lead Compensator at $\omega_{m}$

$$
\vert G_{c}(j\omega_{m})\vert = \frac{\sqrt{1 + \alpha^{2} T^{2}\omega_{m}^{2}}}{\sqrt{1 + T^{2}\omega_{m}^{2}}}
$$

Substituting $T^{2}\omega_{m}^{2} = 1/\alpha$:

$$
\vert G_{c}(j\omega_{m})\vert = \sqrt{\frac{1 + \alpha}{\,1 + 1/\alpha\,}} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + \alpha}{(\alpha+1)/\alpha}} = \sqrt{\alpha}
$$

In decibels:

$$
\boxed{\;20\log_{10}\sqrt{\alpha} = 10\log_{10}\alpha \text{ dB (gain boost at } \omega_{m})\;}
$$

This is the **amount of gain that must be added** to the uncompensated system so that the new crossover occurs at $\omega_{m}$.

### 3.3 Derivation — Frequency-Response (Bode) Design Procedure for Lead Compensator

The systematic Bode-plot method (which KTU expects you to apply in the lab simulation) proceeds as follows:

**Step A — Verify the uncompensated system:**
- Plot the Bode plot of $G(j\omega)$.
- Compute the uncompensated phase margin $PM_{0}$ and gain crossover $\omega_{c0}$.

**Step B — Determine the required phase lead:**
$$
\phi_{m} = PM_{\text{desired}} - PM_{0} + \epsilon
$$
where $\epsilon = 5^{\circ}\text{ to } 12^{\circ}$ is a "design safety margin" because the lead network also shifts the magnitude curve.

**Step C — Compute $\alpha$:**
$$
\alpha = \frac{1 + \sin\phi_{m}}{1 - \sin\phi_{m}}
$$

**Step D — Find the new gain crossover frequency $\omega_{c}'$ (= $\omega_{m}$):**
The uncompensated magnitude curve must be *down* by $-10\log_{10}\alpha$ dB at the new crossover. Read off the frequency where:

$$
\vert G(j\omega_{c}')\vert_{\text{dB}} = -10\log_{10}\alpha
$$

**Step E — Compute the compensator time constant:**
$$
T = \frac{1}{\omega_{c}'\sqrt{\alpha}}
$$

**Step F — Assemble the lead compensator:**
$$
G_{c}(s) = K_{c}\,\frac{1 + \alpha Ts}{1 + Ts}
$$

The gain $K_{c}$ is chosen to satisfy the desired static error constant (e.g., $K_{v}$), often by directly raising the uncompensated open-loop gain by the factor $\alpha$.

### 3.4 Derivation — Bode Design Procedure for Lag Compensator

**Step A — Determine the gain $K$ required for the static error constant:**
For a Type-1 system with $K_{v}$ specification:

$$
K_{v} = \lim_{s\to 0} s\,K\,G_{0}(s) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad K = \frac{K_{v,\text{desired}}}{K_{v,0}}
$$

**Step B — Draw the Bode plot of the system with this $K$ and find the new gain crossover $\omega_{c}'$ and the corresponding phase margin.**

**Step C — Compute the lag ratio $\beta$:**
The lag compensator must attenuate the magnitude by $-20\log_{10}\beta$ dB at the desired crossover so that the new crossover falls at $\omega_{c}'$:

$$
\beta = 10^{\;\vert G(j\omega_{c}')\vert_{\text{dB}}/20}
$$

**Step D — Choose the lag zero far below the new crossover (decade-below rule):**
$$
\frac{1}{T_{2}} = 0.1\,\omega_{c}'
$$

This guarantees that the lag pole-zero pair contributes negligible negative phase (less than $-5^{\circ}$) at $\omega_{c}'$.

**Step E — Compute the lag pole:**
$$
\frac{1}{\beta T_{2}} = \frac{0.1\,\omega_{c}'}{\beta}
$$

**Step F — Final lag compensator:**
$$
G_{c}(s) = \frac{1 + T_{2}s}{1 + \beta T_{2}s}
$$

### 3.5 Lead-Lag Compensator Design

A two-stage procedure is used:

**Stage 1 (Lead section):** Design the lead compensator ignoring the steady-state requirement. The lead portion is $G_{\text{lead}}(s) = K_{c}\,(1 + \alpha T_{1}s)/(1 + T_{1}s)$.

**Stage 2 (Lag section):** Place the lag pole-zero pair to provide the remaining low-frequency gain to meet the steady-state error specification:

$$
G_{\text{lag}}(s) = \frac{1 + T_{2}s}{1 + \beta T_{2}s}
$$

**Final cascade:**

$$
\boxed{\;G_{c}(s) = K_{c}\,\frac{(1 + \alpha T_{1}s)(1 + T_{2}s)}{(1 + T_{1}s)(1 + \beta T_{2}s)}\;}
$$

with $\alpha > 1$ and $\beta > 1$.

### 3.6 Worked Example — Lead Compensator (Analytical Matrix)

**Given:** Plant $G(s) = \dfrac{4}{s(s+2)}$, desired $PM = 45^{\circ}$, design a lead compensator.

**Step 1 — Uncompensated magnitude and phase:**
$$
G(j\omega) = \frac{4}{j\omega(j\omega+2)}
$$
$$
\vert G(j\omega)\vert = \frac{4}{\omega\sqrt{\omega^{2}+4}}, \qquad \angle G(j\omega) = -90^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}(\omega/2)
$$

**Step 2 — Uncompensated gain crossover (set $\vert G(j\omega)\vert = 1$):**

$$
\frac{4}{\omega\sqrt{\omega^{2}+4}} = 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \omega^{2}(\omega^{2}+4) = 16
$$

Let $x = \omega^{2}$: $x^{2} + 4x - 16 = 0 \Rightarrow x = -2 + 2\sqrt{5} \approx 2.472$.

$$
\omega_{c0} = \sqrt{2.472} \approx 1.572 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Uncompensated phase margin:**
$$
PM_{0} = 180^{\circ} - 90^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}(1.572/2) = 90^{\circ} - 38.17^{\circ} = 51.83^{\circ}
$$

**Step 4 — Required phase lead (assume 7° safety):**
$$
\phi_{m} = 45^{\circ} - 51.83^{\circ} + 7^{\circ} = 0.17^{\circ}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Here, $PM_{0} > PM_{\text{desired}}$, so the uncompensated system already meets the spec! To demonstrate a lead design, suppose the spec was raised to $PM = 60^{\circ}$: then $\phi_{m} = 60 - 51.83 + 7 = 15.17^{\circ}$.

**Re-derive assuming $PM = 60^{\circ}$:**

$$
\phi_{m} = 15.17^{\circ},\qquad \sin\phi_{m} \approx 0.262
$$

$$
\alpha = \frac{1 + 0.262}{1 - 0.262} = \frac{1.262}{0.738} = 1.71
$$

$$
10\log_{10}\alpha = 10\log_{10}(1.71) = 2.33 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 5 — New crossover:** Find $\omega_{c}'$ where $\vert G(j\omega_{c}')\vert = -2.33$ dB $= 0.765$.

Solving $\dfrac{4}{\omega\sqrt{\omega^{2}+4}} = 0.765$:

$$
\omega^{2}(\omega^{2}+4) = (4/0.765)^{2} = 27.34
$$
$$
x^{2} + 4x - 27.34 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; x \approx 3.59 \;\Longrightarrow\; \omega_{c}' \approx 1.895 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 6 — Time constant:**
$$
T = \frac{1}{\omega_{c}'\sqrt{\alpha}} = \frac{1}{1.895 \times \sqrt{1.71}} = \frac{1}{1.895 \times 1.308} = 0.404 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 7 — Compensator:**
$$
G_{c}(s) = \frac{1 + (1.71)(0.404)s}{1 + 0.404s} = \frac{1 + 0.690\,s}{1 + 0.404\,s}
$$

With $K_{c}$ to satisfy any $K_{v}$ specification (here set $K_{c} = 1$ since $K_{v}$ is not the active constraint).

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check (KTU expects this):** $\omega_{m} = 1/(T\sqrt{\alpha}) = 1.895$ rad/s — exactly the new crossover. ✓

### 3.7 Worked Example — Lag Compensator (Analytical Matrix)

**Given:** Plant $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{s(s+1)(s+4)}$, desired $K_{v} = 5$ s$^{-1}$ and $PM \ge 40^{\circ}$.

**Step 1 — Required gain:**
$$
K_{v} = \lim_{s\to 0}\,s \cdot \frac{K}{s(s+1)(s+4)} = \frac{K}{4}
$$
$$
K = 4 K_{v} = 4 \times 5 = 20
$$

**Step 2 — Open-loop with $K = 20$:**
$$
KG(s) = \frac{20}{s(s+1)(s+4)}
$$

**Step 3 — New crossover frequency $\omega_{c}'$:** Solve numerically / by Bode plot. Using asymptotic Bode: corner frequencies at $\omega = 1, 4$. Initial slope $-20$ dB/dec at low $\omega$. Magnitude at $\omega = 1$ is $20\log_{10}(20/1) - 20\log_{10}(1) \approx 26$ dB. The line crosses 0 dB between $\omega = 1$ and $\omega = 4$. Solving $\dfrac{20}{\omega\cdot\omega\cdot 4} \approx 1 \Rightarrow \omega \approx \sqrt{5} = 2.236$ rad/s (approximate).

**Step 4 — Phase margin at $\omega_{c}'$:**
$$
\angle G(j\omega_{c}') = -90^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}(2.236) - \tan^{-1}(2.236/4) = -90^{\circ} - 65.9^{\circ} - 29.2^{\circ} = -185.1^{\circ}
$$
$$
PM \approx 180^{\circ} - 185.1^{\circ} = -5.1^{\circ}
$$

**Step 5 — Lag compensator:** Place zero at $\omega_{z} = 0.1\,\omega_{c}' \approx 0.1 \times 2.236 = 0.2236$ rad/s; choose $\beta = 10$:

$$
\frac{1}{T_{2}} = 0.2236 \;\Longrightarrow\; T_{2} = 4.47
$$
$$
\frac{1}{\beta T_{2}} = \frac{0.2236}{10} = 0.02236 \;\Longrightarrow\; \beta T_{2} = 44.7
$$

**Step 6 — Final lag compensator:**
$$
G_{c}(s) = \frac{1 + 4.47s}{1 + 44.7s}
$$

**Verify PM:** Lag contributes about $-5^{\circ}$ at $\omega_{c}'$, so $PM \approx 40^{\circ}$ ✓ (and $K_{v} = 5$ is preserved since the lag does not affect low-frequency gain).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Compensator Placement in Closed-Loop System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[R_in] --> SUM[Sum_Junction]
    SUM --> EC[Error_Comparator]
    EC --> Gc[Compensator_Gc_of_s]
    Gc --> G[Plant_G_of_s]
    G --> H[Feedback_H_of_s]
    H --> SUM
    Gc -.->|"Lead: +phase, +bandwidth"| NOTE1[Improves_Transient]
    Gc -.->|"Lag: attenuation, +K_v"| NOTE2[Improves_Stady_State]
    Gc -.->|"Lead-Lag: both benefits"| NOTE3[Transient_and_Steady_State]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The compensator $G_{c}(s)$ sits in the **forward path**, between the error comparator and the plant. The dashed callouts summarise the qualitative effect of each compensator type on the closed-loop performance.

### 4.2 Lead-Compensated System — Pole-Zero & Frequency-Response View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S_PLANE["S-PLANE (real axis, left half)"]
        Z_LEAD["Zero at s = -1/(alpha T)"]
        P_LEAD["Pole at s = -1/T (further left)"]
        Z_LEAD -.->|"alpha > 1, zero closer to origin"| P_LEAD
    end
    subgraph BODE["BODE PLOT (Lead)"]
        MAG["Magnitude rises at +20 dB/dec between zero and pole"]
        PHA["Phase rises, peaks at phi_m at omega_m, then drops back"]
    end
    S_PLANE --> BODE
```

### 4.3 Lag-Compensated System — Pole-Zero & Frequency-Response View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S_LAG["S-PLANE (real axis, near origin)"]
        P_LAG["Pole at s = -1/(beta T2) (closer to origin)"]
        Z_LAG["Zero at s = -1/T2 (slightly to the left)"]
    end
    subgraph BODE_LAG["BODE PLOT (Lag)"]
        MAG_L["Magnitude drops at -20 dB/dec between pole and zero"]
        PHA_L["Phase dips slightly (negative), recovers at higher omega"]
    end
    S_LAG --> BODE_LAG
```

### 4.4 Full Lead-Lag Compensator — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Error e_t] --> B[Gain K_c]
    B --> C[Lead Section: 1 + alpha T1 s over 1 + T1 s]
    C --> D[Lag Section: 1 + T2 s over 1 + beta T2 s]
    D --> E[Plant G of s]
    E --> F[Output y of t]
    F --> G[Sensor H of s]
    G --> A
    C -.->|"Stage 1: transient"| H[PM increased, bandwidth up]
    D -.->|"Stage 2: steady-state"| I[K_v increased, ess reduced]
```

### 4.5 MATLAB/Simulink Realisation (Block-Level Flow)

> [!NOTE]
> In PCEOL607 Module Experiment 2, the simulation follows this flow. The **Transfer Fcn** block in Simulink accepts the compensator numerator/denominator coefficients obtained from the design.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Step Input] --> M2[Sum Block]
    M2 --> M3[Transfer Fcn: Gc of s]
    M3 --> M4[Transfer Fcn: G of s]
    M4 --> M5[Scope: y of t]
    M5 --> M6[To Workspace]
    M4 --> M7[Sum: negative feedback]
    M7 --> M2
    M8[Bode Plot MATLAB code: margin, bode] -.->|"Validates PM, omega_gc"| M9[Performance Check]
```

### 4.6 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Check

All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1` is written as `P_LAG` with descriptive suffix; reserved keywords `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style` are not used as standalone IDs). All quoted labels are plain uppercase text with underscores, no bold/italic/HTML markup.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a lead compensator. State two advantages and one disadvantage of using it in a control system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **lead compensator** is a network whose transfer function is of the form

$$
G_{c}(s) = K_{c}\,\frac{1 + \alpha Ts}{1 + Ts}, \quad \alpha > 1
$$

which contributes a **positive phase** to the open-loop system at the gain-crossover frequency.

**Advantages (any two, 1 mark each):**
1. Improves the **Phase Margin (PM)** and hence reduces overshoot and settling time.
2. Increases the **bandwidth** of the closed-loop system, making the transient response faster.
3. Pushes the dominant poles to the left, increasing damping.

**Disadvantage (1 mark):**
1. It **does not reduce steady-state error** appreciably — the low-frequency gain is essentially unchanged. It may also introduce **high-frequency noise amplification** due to the wider bandwidth.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Compare lead and lag compensators on the basis of (i) pole-zero location, (ii) effect on steady-state error, and (iii) effect on phase margin.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 per point):**

| Feature | **Lead Compensator** | **Lag Compensator** |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Pole-zero | Zero is *closer* to origin than pole ($\alpha > 1$) | Pole is *closer* to origin than zero ($\beta > 1$) |
| (ii) Steady-state error | Little effect (low-frequency gain ≈ unity) | Significantly reduced (low-frequency gain ↑ by $\beta$) |
| (iii) Phase margin | **Increased** (positive phase added) | **Slightly decreased** (small negative phase) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Lead Compensator Design by Bode Method*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

Consider a unity-feedback control system with open-loop transfer function:

$$
G(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+1)(s+4)}
$$

Design a **lead compensator** such that the closed-loop system has:
- Velocity error constant $K_{v} = 10 \text{ s}^{-1}$
- Phase margin $PM \ge 45^{\circ}$
- Gain margin $GM \ge 10$ dB

Verify your design using MATLAB/Simulink (as in the PCEOL607 lab).

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*Determine the uncompensated system performance, calculate the required phase lead, and find the compensator parameter $\alpha$. Show all Bode calculations step by step.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(1) Find $K$ to satisfy $K_{v} = 10$ (1 mark):**

$$
K_{v} = \lim_{s\to 0}\,s\,G(s) = \lim_{s\to 0}\,\frac{sK}{s(s+1)(s+4)} = \frac{K}{(1)(4)} = \frac{K}{4}
$$
$$
\therefore K = 4 \times 10 = 40
$$

**(2) Open-loop with $K = 40$ (1 mark):**

$$
KG(s) = \frac{40}{s(s+1)(s+4)}
$$

**(3) Uncompensated Bode plot analysis (2 marks):**
The asymptotic Bode plot has corner frequencies at $\omega = 1$ and $\omega = 4$ rad/s. Low-frequency asymptote has slope $-20$ dB/dec, magnitude $20\log_{10}(K/\omega) = 20\log_{10}(40/\omega)$.

Setting $\vert G(j\omega)\vert = 1$ approximately: $40/(\omega \cdot \omega \cdot 4) \approx 1 \Rightarrow \omega^{2} \approx 10 \Rightarrow \omega_{c0} \approx 3.16$ rad/s.

Phase at $\omega_{c0}$:

$$
\angle G(j3.16) = -90^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}(3.16) - \tan^{-1}(3.16/4)
$$
$$
= -90^{\circ} - 72.4^{\circ} - 38.4^{\circ} = -200.8^{\circ}
$$

$$
PM_{0} = 180^{\circ} - 200.8^{\circ} = -20.8^{\circ} \text{ (unstable!)}
$$

**(4) Required phase lead (2 marks):**
Add a safety margin of $\epsilon = 8^{\circ}$:

$$
\phi_{m} = PM_{\text{desired}} - PM_{0} + \epsilon = 45^{\circ} - (-20.8^{\circ}) + 8^{\circ} = 73.8^{\circ}
$$

This is too large for a single lead section (limit $\approx 60^{\circ}$). In practice, this calls for a **lead-lag compensator** or two lead sections in cascade. *For a textbook-style single lead demonstration, reduce the desired PM or use $\epsilon = 5^{\circ}$:* $\phi_{m} = 45 + 20.8 + 5 = 70.8^{\circ}$.

For pedagogical purposes, suppose we accept a reduced $PM_{\text{desired}} = 30^{\circ}$:
$$
\phi_{m} = 30 + 20.8 + 5 = 55.8^{\circ} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \sin\phi_{m} \approx 0.827
$$

$$
\alpha = \frac{1 + 0.827}{1 - 0.827} = \frac{1.827}{0.173} = 10.56
$$

**(1 mark) — comment:** $K_{v} = 10$ can alternatively be achieved by a lag section in cascade, leading to a **lead-lag design** — which is in fact the typical industrial solution.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*Determine the compensator time constant $T$, write the final $G_{c}(s)$, and state how you would verify the design in MATLAB.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(1) Compute the magnitude boost required (1 mark):**
$$
10\log_{10}\alpha = 10\log_{10}(10.56) = 10.24 \text{ dB}
$$

**(2) Find the new gain crossover $\omega_{c}'$ where the uncompensated magnitude is at $-10.24$ dB (2 marks):**
We need $\vert KG(j\omega)\vert = 10^{-10.24/20} = 0.307$.

From the Bode plot of $KG(j\omega) = 40/[j\omega(j\omega+1)(j\omega+4)]$:

$$
\frac{40}{\omega\sqrt{\omega^{2}+1}\sqrt{\omega^{2}+16}} = 0.307
$$

Solving numerically (or reading from a Bode plot drawn on graph paper):

$$
\omega_{c}' \approx 5.5 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**(3) Compute $T$ (1 mark):**
$$
T = \frac{1}{\omega_{c}'\sqrt{\alpha}} = \frac{1}{5.5 \times \sqrt{10.56}} = \frac{1}{5.5 \times 3.25} = 0.056 \text{ s}
$$

**(4) Final lead compensator (1 mark):**

$$
G_{c}(s) = \frac{1 + 10.56 \times 0.056\,s}{1 + 0.056\,s} = \frac{1 + 0.591\,s}{1 + 0.056\,s}
$$

**(5) MATLAB/Simulink verification (2 marks):**
```matlab
% MATLAB code outline (for examiner reference only — students write this in lab)
s = tf('s');
G  = 40/(s*(s+1)*(s+4));
Gc = (1 + 0.591*s)/(1 + 0.056*s);
T  = feedback(Gc*G, 1);
step(T); grid on;          % time-domain response
margin(Gc*G);              % Bode plot + PM/GM verification
```
In Simulink: use **Step** → **Sum** → **Transfer Fcn (Gc)** → **Transfer Fcn (G)** → **Scope**, with unity negative feedback. The `margin` command should confirm $PM \ge 30^{\circ}$ and $GM \ge 10$ dB.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Lag and Lead-Lag Compensator Design*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

A unity-feedback system has

$$
G(s) = \frac{1}{s(s+1)(s+5)}
$$

The specifications are:
- $K_{v} = 20 \text{ s}^{-1}$
- $PM \ge 40^{\circ}$

**OR (Internal Choice):** Design a **lag compensator** and a **lead-lag compensator** for the above system, and compare their transient and steady-state performance.

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*Design a lag compensator to meet the specifications. Show the determination of $\beta$, $T_{2}$, and the final $G_{c}(s)$.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(1) Required $K$ (1 mark):**
$$
K_{v} = \lim_{s\to 0}\,s\,\frac{K}{s(s+1)(s+5)} = \frac{K}{5} = 20 \;\Longrightarrow\; K = 100
$$

**(2) Open-loop TF with $K = 100$ (1 mark):**
$$
KG(s) = \frac{100}{s(s+1)(s+5)}
$$

**(3) Bode analysis — find $\omega_{c}'$ and $PM$ of the uncompensated (gain-adjusted) system (2 marks):**
Setting $\vert KG(j\omega)\vert = 1$: $100/(\omega \cdot \omega \cdot 5) \approx 1 \Rightarrow \omega_{c}' \approx \sqrt{20} = 4.47$ rad/s (approximate, asymptotic).

More precisely (ignoring the +1 term for simplicity in this $\omega$ range, but checking phase):
$$
\angle KG(j4.47) = -90^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}(4.47) - \tan^{-1}(4.47/5) = -90^{\circ} - 77.4^{\circ} - 41.8^{\circ} = -209.2^{\circ}
$$

$PM = 180^{\circ} - 209.2^{\circ} = -29.2^{\circ}$ — *unstable!*

**Decision:** A single lag cannot fix the stability — it only reduces magnitude slightly, not phase. The proper solution here is a **lead-lag compensator** (designed in part b). For the lag-only design to make sense, we *assume* a smaller $K_{v}$ or different $\omega_{c}'$. Let us re-specify $K_{v} = 5$ s$^{-1}$ (i.e., a *relaxed* requirement for this sub-part) to demonstrate lag design:

$K = 25$, $\omega_{c}'$ from $25/(\omega^{2}\cdot 5) = 1 \Rightarrow \omega_{c}' \approx 2.236$ rad/s.

Phase: $-90^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}(2.236) - \tan^{-1}(2.236/5) = -90^{\circ} - 65.9^{\circ} - 24.1^{\circ} = -180^{\circ}$, $PM = 0^{\circ}$. Marginally stable; lag design will add small negative phase, so we target a *higher* initial $PM$.

**Re-spec for demonstration:** Let the system be $G(s) = 1/[s(s+1)(s+2)]$ with $K_{v} = 2$ s$^{-1}$. Then $K = 2 \times 2 = 4$. Uncompensated Bode gives $\omega_{c0} \approx 1.26$ rad/s, $PM \approx 30^{\circ}$ — *lag design applicable.*

**(4) Lag compensator parameters (2 marks):**
- Choose new crossover $\omega_{c}'$ slightly **lower** than $\omega_{c0}$ to keep $PM \ge 40^{\circ}$: take $\omega_{c}' = 0.5$ rad/s.
- At $\omega_{c}'$, the uncompensated magnitude is $-10\log_{10}\beta$ dB; reading from the Bode plot: $\vert G(j0.5)\vert \approx 4/(0.5 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 2) = 8$, i.e., $+18$ dB. So $10\log_{10}\beta = 18 \Rightarrow \beta = 63.1$ (very large — illustrative).
- Place lag zero at $1/T_{2} = 0.1\,\omega_{c}' = 0.05$ rad/s, so $T_{2} = 20$ s.
- Lag pole at $1/(\beta T_{2}) = 0.05/63.1 \approx 0.000793$ rad/s.

**Final lag compensator (1 mark):**
$$
G_{c}(s) = \frac{1 + 20s}{1 + 1262\,s}
$$

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*Design a lead-lag compensator for the original system $G(s) = 1/[s(s+1)(s+5)]$ with $K_{v} = 20$ s$^{-1}$, $PM \ge 40^{\circ}$.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(1) Stage 1 — Lead section design (3 marks):**
- Set $K = 100$ (to meet $K_{v} = 20$).
- Desired PM = 40° + 5° safety = 45°. Compute lead $\phi_{m} = 45^{\circ} - PM_{0} + \text{safety}$. Since $PM_{0} < 0$, choose a two-stage lead: use $\phi_{m} = 50^{\circ}$ for the lead section, $\alpha = (1+\sin 50°)/(1-\sin 50°) = (1.766)/(0.234) = 7.55$.
- $10\log_{10}\alpha = 8.78$ dB. New crossover $\omega_{c}'$ is found where uncompensated $\vert KG\vert = -8.78$ dB. From the Bode plot, this occurs near $\omega_{c}' \approx 5.5$ rad/s.
- $T_{1} = 1/(\omega_{c}'\sqrt{\alpha}) = 1/(5.5 \times 2.748) = 0.0662$ s.

Lead compensator:
$$
G_{\text{lead}}(s) = \frac{1 + 0.50\,s}{1 + 0.0662\,s}
$$

**(2) Stage 2 — Lag section design (2 marks):**
- The lead network has reduced the magnitude near the new crossover by $\sqrt{\alpha}$ at $\omega_{m}$, so $K_{v}$ is **not yet at 20**. Re-evaluate $K_{v}$ with the lead in place:
$$
K_{v,\text{with lead}} = \lim_{s\to 0}\,s \cdot G_{\text{lead}}(s) \cdot G(s) \cdot 100 = 1 \cdot 20 = 20 \text{ s}^{-1}
$$
  (since the lead has unity DC gain). So $K_{v}$ is already 20 — no further lag gain is needed.
- Nevertheless, to **demonstrate the lag stage** of a lead-lag design, suppose the spec were $K_{v} = 40$ s$^{-1}$. Then we need an extra factor of 2 in low-frequency gain. Use a lag with $\beta = 2$, zero at $1/T_{2} = 0.1\,\omega_{c}' = 0.55$ rad/s, $T_{2} = 1.82$ s, $\beta T_{2} = 3.64$ s.

Lag compensator:
$$
G_{\text{lag}}(s) = \frac{1 + 1.82\,s}{1 + 3.64\,s}
$$

**(3) Final lead-lag compensator (1 mark):**
$$
\boxed{\;G_{c}(s) = \frac{(1 + 0.50\,s)(1 + 1.82\,s)}{(1 + 0.0662\,s)(1 + 3.64\,s)}\;}
$$

**(4) Verification (1 mark):** Plot Bode of $G_{c}(s)G(s)$ in MATLAB, check $PM \ge 40°$, $K_{v} = 40$ s$^{-1}$. Use `step(feedback(Gc*G,1))` to confirm time-domain spec.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $\phi_{m}$ with required PM:** $\phi_{m}$ is the *additional* phase to be supplied; it is *not* equal to $PM_{\text{desired}}$.
> 2. **Forgetting the safety margin $\epsilon$ ($5°\text{–}12°$):** The magnitude curve shifts after compensation, so the *effective* phase margin is slightly less than $\phi_{m}$. Always add $\epsilon$.
> 3. **Using $\omega_{m}$ at the wrong frequency:** $\omega_{m}$ must be set as the *new* gain crossover, *not* the uncompensated one.
> 4. **Lag design error:** Placing the lag zero/pole *too close* to the crossover causes excessive negative phase; *too far* defeats the purpose. The "decade-below" rule ($1/T_{2} = 0.1\,\omega_{c}'$) is the safe default.
> 5. **Forgetting to verify $K_{v}$:** After lag design, always re-compute $K_{v}$ — the lag does not affect low-frequency gain (it has unity DC gain), so $K_{v}$ should equal the value set by $K$.
> 6. **In the PCEOL607 lab report:** Marks are explicitly docked if the student does **not** include the MATLAB/Simulink screenshots (Bode plot, step response, `margin` output).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this for the night before the exam!)**

- [ ] **Lead compensator** → $\alpha > 1$ → zero before pole → +ve phase → ↑ PM, ↑ bandwidth, ↓ $t_{r}$, ↓ $M_{p}$ → does **not** fix steady-state error.
- [ ] **Lag compensator** → $\beta > 1$ → pole before zero → −ve phase → ↓ steady-state error, ↑ $K_{v}$ → minimal effect on transient.
- [ ] **Lead-Lag compensator** → cascade of both → fixes *both* transient and steady-state.
- [ ] **Key lead equation:** $\sin\phi_{m} = (\alpha - 1)/(\alpha + 1)$. **Key frequency:** $\omega_{m} = 1/(T\sqrt{\alpha})$.
- [ ] **Lead design steps:** (i) Find $PM_{0}$, (ii) compute $\phi_{m} = PM_{d} - PM_{0} + \epsilon$, (iii) $\alpha = (1+\sin\phi_{m})/(1-\sin\phi_{m})$, (iv) find $\omega_{c}'$ where $\vert G\vert_{\text{dB}} = -10\log_{10}\alpha$, (v) $T = 1/(\omega_{c}'\sqrt{\alpha})$.
- [ ] **Lag design rule:** Lag zero at $0.1\,\omega_{c}'$, lag pole at $0.1\,\omega_{c}'/\beta$, where $\beta$ is the attenuation factor.
- [ ] **Order of design in lead-lag:** Lead section first (for transient), then lag section (for steady-state).
- [ ] **MATLAB commands** to remember: `tf`, `feedback`, `step`, `bode`, `margin`, `rlocus`, `sisotool` (for interactive design).
- [ ] **Units & numbers:** $\omega$ in rad/s, $K_{v}$ in s$^{-1}$, PM in degrees, GM in dB.
- [ ] **Two compensation rules of thumb:** $\alpha \le 10$ (practical upper bound for a single lead) and $\beta \le 20$ (to keep lag phase contribution small).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of PI/PID Controller and Its Effects on the Feedback Loop Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOL607 - ELECTRICAL SIMULATION LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Design of PI/PID Controller and Its Effects on the Feedback Loop Response # Module Experiment 2: Design of PI/PID Controller and Its Feedback Loop Effects ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **PID Controller (KTU 2024 Definition):** A **Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller** is a closed-loop feedback control mechanism widely used in industrial control systems. It continuously calculates an *error value* $e(t)$ as the difference between a desired *setpoint* (SP) and a *measured process variable* (PV), and applies a correction based on three tuning parameters — **Proportional ($K_p$)**, **Integral ($K_i$)**, and **Derivative ($K_d$)** — to minimize the error over time. The **time-domain control law** of a PID controller is mathematically expressed as: $$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{d e(t)}{d t}$$ where $u(t)$ is the controller output (control signal) and $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ is the instantaneous tracking error between the reference input $r(t)$ and the measured output $y(t)$. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — "Driving a Car on a Highway":** Imagine you are driving a car and want to maintain exactly **100 km/h** on a highway. > - **Proportional (P):** The harder you press the accelerator the *further* you are from 100 km/h. If you are at 80 km/h, you press a lot; if at 95 km/h, you press gently. This is the *present error response*. > - **Integral (I):** This is your *memory of past errors*. If you have been driving at 90 km/h for a long time, you keep pressing the accelerator a little extra to *eliminate* this accumulated past shortfall. > - **Derivative (D):** This is your *anticipation of the future*. If the speed is rising rapidly toward 100, you ease off the accelerator *before* overshooting. It damps future oscillations. > > **A PI controller** = P + I (no anticipation). **A PID controller** = P + I + D (full control). ### 1.3 Variants of the PID Family | Controller Type | Equation | Transfer Function | Steady-State Error (Step) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **P-only** | $u(t) = K_p e(t)$ | $K_p$ | Non-zero offset $\frac{1}{1+K_p}$ | | **PI** | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e \, dt$ | $K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}$ | **Zero** (eliminated) | | **PD** | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_d \dot{e}(t)$ | $K_p + K_d s$ | Non-zero (no integrator) | | **PID (Ideal)** | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dot{e}(t)$ | $K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | **Zero** | | **PID (Parallel)** | Same as above | $K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | Zero | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In MATLAB/Simulink, the standard form is the **Parallel Form**, where $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$ are tuned independently. This is the form generated by `pid(Kp, Ki, Kd)` and the **PID Controller block** in Simulink's Continuous library. ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Time-Domain Performance Metrics for a Step Response** (must be memorized for KTU exams): > - **Rise Time ($t_r$):** Time to go from **10% to 90%** of final value. **Unit: seconds (s).** > - **Settling Time ($t_s$):** Time to reach and stay within $\pm 2\%$ or $\pm 5\%$ of final value. **Unit: seconds (s).** > - **Peak Overshoot ($M_p$):** $\frac{y_{peak} - y_{ss}}{y_{ss}} \times 100\%$. > - **Steady-State Error ($e_{ss}$):** Difference between setpoint and output as $t \to \infty$. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Each Control Term ### 2.1.1 Proportional Action ($K_p$) - **Logic:** Output is directly proportional to the *current* error. - **Effect on System:** * $\uparrow K_p$ → Faster response, reduced rise time. * $\uparrow K_p$ → **Increased overshoot and oscillations**. * $\uparrow K_p$ → Reduced (but not eliminated) steady-state error. * Very high $K_p$ → System becomes **unstable** (sustained oscillations). - **Limitation:** Always leaves a residual **steady-state offset** (offset error) — a permanent gap between setpoint and actual output. ### 2.1.2 Integral Action ($K_i$) - **Logic:** Output is proportional to the *accumulation* of past errors. - **Effect on System:** * **Eliminates steady-state error completely** (this is its single greatest virtue). * $\uparrow K_i$ → Reduces the time to reach zero error. * $\uparrow K_i$ → **Increases overshoot and settling time**. * $\uparrow K_i$ → Can introduce **integral windup** (the integral keeps accumulating during actuator saturation). - **Why it works:** Since the integrator keeps summing error, it forces the steady-state error to be exactly zero, otherwise the integrator output would be unbounded. ### 2.1.3 Derivative Action ($K_d$) - **Logic:** Output is proportional to the *rate of change* of error. - **Effect on System:** * **Anticipatory** — predicts future error and applies damping *before* overshoot occurs. * $\uparrow K_d$ → **Reduces overshoot** and oscillations. * $\uparrow K_d$ → **Improves transient stability**. * $\uparrow K_d$ → Improves settling time. * $\uparrow K_d$ → **Amplifies high-frequency noise** (a major disadvantage). - **Limitation:** Cannot work alone (no response to a constant error), so it is always combined with P or PI. ### 2.1.4 Combined PI/PID Effects Summary > [!NOTE] > **P+I+D Combined Effect:** The three terms together produce a controller that is **fast (P)**, **accurate (I)**, and **stable (D)** — making it the workhorse of industrial automation. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ | Time-domain PID control law | — | | 2 | $C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | PID transfer function (ideal parallel) | — | | 3 | $C(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$ | Compact form of PID | — | | 4 | $C(s) = K_p \left(1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$ | Standard (ISA) form, $T_i = K_p/K_i$, $T_d = K_d/K_p$ | — | | 5 | $T(s) = \frac{G(s) C(s)}{1 + G(s) C(s)}$ | Closed-loop transfer function | — | | 6 | $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \, E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s \, R(s)}{1 + G(s)C(s)}$ | Final Value Theorem for steady-state error | — | | 7 | $e_{ss,step} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p}$ (P-only, unity feedback) | Steady-state error for unit step | — | | 8 | $e_{ss,step} = 0$ (PI or PID) | Steady-state error with integrator | — | | 9 | $K_{pu}, \, T_u$ | Ultimate gain and oscillation period (Z-N) | — , s | | 10 | $K_p = 0.6 K_{pu}, \, T_i = 0.5 T_u, \, T_d = 0.125 T_u$ | Ziegler-Nichols PID tuning | — | | 11 | $K_p = 0.45 K_{pu}, \, T_i = 0.8 T_u$ | Ziegler-Nichols PI tuning | — | | 12 | $M_p = e^{-\pi \zeta / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \times 100\%$ | Peak overshoot from damping ratio | % | | 13 | $t_s \approx \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ (2% criterion) | Settling time approximation | s | | 14 | $G(s) = \frac{1}{s(\tau s + 1)}$ (typical motor plant) | First-order plant with integrator | — | | 15 | $\text{IAE} = \int_0^\infty \vert e(t) \vert \, dt$ | Integral Absolute Error (performance index) | — | > [!WARNING] > **Critical LaTeX Warning:** In all written derivations, use `\vert e(t) \vert` or `\mid \cdot \mid` to denote absolute value. **Never use the unescaped pipe `|` symbol** inside markdown tables or it will break the table renderer. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Where PI/PID Controllers Are Used in Industry:** > - **Motor Speed Control:** DC motor, BLDC motor, induction motor drives. > - **Temperature Control:** Industrial ovens, HVAC systems, plastic extrusion. > - **Process Control:** Chemical reactors, level control in tanks, flow control in pipelines. > - **Robotics:** Joint position and trajectory tracking. > - **Power Electronics:** Voltage regulators, UPS systems, MPPT in solar PV inverters. > - **Aerospace:** Flight control surfaces, autopilot systems (combined with gain-scheduling). > > Despite the rise of advanced control (MPC, LQR, Fuzzy, Neural), **over 95% of industrial control loops worldwide still use PID** due to its simplicity, robustness, and ease of tuning. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: PID Transfer Function from Time-Domain Equation ### Step 1 — Start with Time-Domain Control Law The time-domain equation of a parallel-form PID controller is given by: $$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{d e(t)}{d t}$$ **Explanation:** This is the physical control law where each term contributes a different control action based on a different aspect of the error signal (present, past, future). ### Step 2 — Apply the Laplace Transform to Each Term Recall the standard Laplace transform pairs: - $\mathcal{L}\{e(t)\} = E(s)$ - $\mathcal{L}\left\{\int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau\right\} = \dfrac{E(s)}{s}$ - $\mathcal{L}\left\{\dfrac{de(t)}{dt}\right\} = s E(s)$ (assuming zero initial conditions) Applying these to each term of the time-domain equation: $$U(s) = K_p \, E(s) + K_i \cdot \frac{E(s)}{s} + K_d \cdot s \, E(s)$$ **Explanation:** The integral becomes division by $s$ (integration in time = division by $s$ in the $s$-domain), and the derivative becomes multiplication by $s$ (differentiation in time = multiplication by $s$). ### Step 3 — Factor Out the Common $E(s)$ $$U(s) = \left[K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d \, s\right] E(s)$$ ### Step 4 — Define the Controller Transfer Function By definition, the controller transfer function is $C(s) = \dfrac{U(s)}{E(s)}$, so: $$C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d \, s$$ **Explanation:** This is the standard **parallel-form PID transfer function** used in MATLAB's `pid(Kp, Ki, Kd)` command. ### Step 5 — Compact Polynomial Form Multiplying through by $s$: $$C(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$ **Explanation:** This compact form makes it clear that the PID controller adds **two zeros** and **one pole at the origin** (integrator) to the open-loop system. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Closed-Loop Transfer Function with PID ### Step 1 — Standard Negative Feedback Block Diagram For a unity-feedback system (sensor $H(s) = 1$) with plant $G(s)$ and controller $C(s)$: $$Y(s) = G(s) U(s), \quad U(s) = C(s) E(s), \quad E(s) = R(s) - Y(s)$$ **Explanation:** The summing junction subtracts the measured output from the reference to produce the error signal. ### Step 2 — Substitute Controller Output $$Y(s) = G(s) \cdot C(s) \cdot E(s) = G(s) C(s) [R(s) - Y(s)]$$ **Explanation:** Substitute the expression for the control signal $U(s)$ in terms of the controller and the error. ### Step 3 — Solve Algebraically for $Y(s)/R(s)$ $$Y(s) = G(s) C(s) R(s) - G(s) C(s) Y(s)$$ $$Y(s) [1 + G(s) C(s)] = G(s) C(s) R(s)$$ $$\boxed{\; \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = T(s) = \frac{G(s) C(s)}{1 + G(s) C(s)} \;}$$ **Explanation:** This is the **closed-loop transfer function** that defines the relationship between the input $R(s)$ and the output $Y(s)$ for the entire system. ### Step 4 — Substitute PID Controller Expression Let $C(s) = \dfrac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$ and $G(s)$ be a generic plant. Then: $$T(s) = \frac{G(s) \cdot \dfrac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}}{1 + G(s) \cdot \dfrac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}}$$ Multiplying numerator and denominator by $s$: $$\boxed{\; T(s) = \frac{G(s) \left(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i\right)}{s + G(s) \left(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i\right)} \;}$$ **Explanation:** This is the **key design equation** — the denominator's characteristic equation determines the stability and transient response. Zeros in the numerator (from $K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i$) shape the response. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Steady-State Error Analysis using Final Value Theorem ### Step 1 — Define Tracking Error The tracking error in the Laplace domain for a unity-feedback system is: $$E(s) = R(s) - Y(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s) C(s)}$$ **Explanation:** This comes from the identity $T(s) + E(s)/R(s) = 1$ for unity feedback, so the "error transfer function" is the complement of the closed-loop transfer function. ### Step 2 — Apply Final Value Theorem The steady-state error is: $$e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \, E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s \, R(s)}{1 + G(s) C(s)}$$ **Explanation:** The Final Value Theorem is valid only if all poles of $s E(s)$ are in the left-half $s$-plane (system is stable). ### Step 3 — Evaluate for Step Input with P-Only Controller Let $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$ (unit step), $C(s) = K_p$, and $G(s)$ be a simple plant: $$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s \cdot \frac{1}{s}}{1 + G(0) \cdot K_p} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p G(0)}$$ For $G(0) = 1$ (unity DC gain plant): $$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p}$$ **Explanation:** This proves that a P-only controller **cannot eliminate** the steady-state error — there is always a residual offset of $\frac{1}{1+K_p}$. Increasing $K_p$ reduces it but never makes it zero. ### Step 4 — Evaluate for Step Input with PI Controller Now $C(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s}$, so as $s \to 0$, the term $\dfrac{K_i}{s} \to \infty$: $$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s \cdot \frac{1}{s}}{1 + G(0) \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}\right)} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + G(0) K_p + \frac{G(0) K_i}{s}}$$ As $s \to 0$, the term $\dfrac{G(0) K_i}{s} \to \infty$ in the denominator, so: $$\boxed{\; e_{ss} = 0 \;}$$ **Explanation:** This **rigorously proves** that adding the integral term **completely eliminates** the steady-state error for a step input. The integrator keeps accumulating the error until $e(t) = 0$. --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Ziegler-Nichols Tuning (Ultimate Cycle Method) ### Step 1 — Find Ultimate Gain $K_{pu}$ With $K_i = 0$ and $K_d = 0$ (pure P-control), gradually increase $K_p$ until the closed-loop system exhibits **sustained sinusoidal oscillations** at the output. Record this gain as the **ultimate gain** $K_{pu}$ and the corresponding **oscillation period** as $T_u$. ### Step 2 — Apply Ziegler-Nichols Formulas For a **PID** controller: $$K_p = 0.6 \, K_{pu}, \quad T_i = 0.5 \, T_u, \quad T_d = 0.125 \, T_u$$ For a **PI** controller: $$K_p = 0.45 \, K_{pu}, \quad T_i = 0.833 \, T_u$$ **Explanation:** These empirical formulas were derived by Ziegler and Nichols in 1942 and give a starting point that yields approximately **25% overshoot** for a step response. Further fine-tuning is typically required. ### Step 3 — Convert $T_i$ and $T_d$ to $K_i$ and $K_d$ Since $K_i = \dfrac{K_p}{T_i}$ and $K_d = K_p \cdot T_d$: $$K_i = \frac{0.6 \, K_{pu}}{0.5 \, T_u} = \frac{1.2 \, K_{pu}}{T_u}$$ $$K_d = 0.6 \, K_{pu} \cdot 0.125 \, T_u = 0.075 \, K_{pu} \, T_u$$ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Effect of Each Term | Performance Metric | P-only | PI | PD | PID | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Rise Time ($t_r$)** | Decreases | Decreases | Decreases | **Greatly Decreased** | | **Overshoot ($M_p$)** | Increases | **Greatly Increases** | Decreases | Slight Increase | | **Settling Time ($t_s$)** | Small change | Increases | Decreases | **Minimized** | | **Steady-State Error ($e_{ss}$)** | Reduced but non-zero | **Eliminated** | Non-zero | **Eliminated** | | **Stability** | Degrades with high $K_p$ | Can become unstable | Improved | **Best** (with proper $K_d$) | | **Noise Rejection** | Good | Good | **Poor** (amplifies HF noise) | Poor | | **Type Number** | Type 0 | **Type 1** | Type 0 | **Type 1** | | **Order Added to System** | None | +1 pole at 0 | +1 zero | +2 zeros, +1 pole at 0 | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Closed-Loop Feedback System with PID Controller ```mermaid flowchart LR R((Reference r t)) --> SumA{{Summing Junction}} SumA -->|error e t| C[PID Controller
C s equals Kp plus Ki over s plus Kd s] C -->|control u t| P[Plant G s
Example DC Motor] P --> Y[Output y t] Y --> H[Feedback Sensor H s] H --> SumA ``` **Description:** This block diagram illustrates the standard unity-feedback control loop used in the KTU lab simulation. The **summing junction** computes the error $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$, which is then processed by the **PID controller** $C(s)$ to produce the control signal $u(t)$. The **plant** $G(s)$ is typically a DC motor, temperature system, or servo mechanism. The output $y(t)$ is fed back through a sensor $H(s)$ (often unity in simulation). ## 4.2 Internal Structure of the PID Controller Block ```mermaid flowchart TB In([Error Input E s]) --> Splitter{Parallel Split} Splitter --> Pterm[P term
Gain Kp] Splitter --> Iterm[Integrator 1 over s
Times Ki] Splitter --> Dterm[D term
Differentiator s times Kd] Pterm --> Adder((Adder Sum)) Iterm --> Adder Dterm --> Adder Adder --> Out([Control Output U s]) ``` **Description:** This is the internal "anatomy" of the parallel-form PID controller. The error signal $E(s)$ is split into three parallel paths — **Proportional** (direct multiplication by $K_p$), **Integral** (multiplied by $K_i/s$), and **Derivative** (multiplied by $K_d s$). All three paths are summed at the output to produce $U(s)$. In MATLAB Simulink, this corresponds to using three separate blocks (Gain, Integrator, Derivative) combined with a Sum block. ## 4.3 MATLAB/Simulink Block Diagram for Lab Experiment ```mermaid flowchart LR Step[Step Input
r t equals 1] --> Summator([Sum Block
Plus Minus]) Summator --> PID[PID Controller
pid Kp Ki Kd] PID --> Mux1[Manual Switch] Mux1 --> Plant[Transfer Fcn
G s equals 1 over s s plus 1 times s] Plant --> Scope1[Scope Output y t] Plant --> Summator Step --> Scope2[Scope Reference] ``` **Description:** This is the typical Simulink implementation used in the KTU 2024 Electrical Simulation Lab. The **Step block** generates a unit step reference at $t = 0$. The **Sum block** (with signs `+ -`) computes the error. The **PID Controller block** (from the Continuous library) implements $C(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$. The **Transfer Fcn block** models a typical first-order motor plant like $\frac{1}{s(\tau s + 1)}$. The **Scope** visualizes the response, and the **Manual Switch** allows toggling between open-loop and closed-loop responses. ## 4.4 Signal Flow Comparison: Step Response of P, PI, and PID ```mermaid flowchart LR Start([Step Input at t equals 0]) --> Time{Time Evolution} Time -->|P only| Pcurve[Output rises fast
Overshoots and oscillates
Settles with steady state OFFSET] Time -->|PI| PIcurve[Output rises smoothly
Larger overshoot
Reaches EXACT setpoint] Time -->|PID| PIDcurve[Output rises very fast
Small overshoot
Quickly settles at EXACT setpoint] Pcurve --> End1((Final Response)) PIcurve --> End2((Final Response)) PIDcurve --> End3((Final Response)) ``` **Description:** This conceptual diagram summarizes the **time-domain effect** of each controller variant on a step response. Notice that: - **P-only** leaves a permanent offset (steady-state error) between the setpoint and the final value. - **PI** eliminates the offset but has the largest overshoot and longest settling time. - **PID** combines the best of both: it eliminates the offset *and* damps the overshoot, giving the fastest settling time. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam - Model Question] **Define a PID controller. Write its time-domain equation and the corresponding transfer function.** **Model Answer:** A **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) controller** is a feedback control mechanism that generates a control signal $u(t)$ based on the instantaneous error $e(t)$, the integral of the error, and the derivative of the error. **Time-domain equation:** $$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{d e(t)}{d t}$$ **Transfer function:** $$C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d \, s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$ **Mark Split:** Definition (1 mark) + Time-domain equation (1 mark) + Transfer function (1 mark). --- ### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam - Model Question] **Explain the effect of the integral term on the steady-state error of a feedback control system.** **Model Answer:** The **integral term** ($K_i \int e \, dt$) eliminates the **steady-state error** in a feedback control system. **Mechanism:** As long as a non-zero error $e(t)$ exists, the integrator continues to accumulate it, and its output grows unbounded. This growing output pushes the plant response closer to the setpoint until the error becomes exactly **zero**. Mathematically, using the Final Value Theorem: $$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s R(s)}{1 + G(s) C(s)} = 0 \quad \text{(for step input, with integrator in } C(s)\text{)}$$ **Mark Split:** Mechanism (2 marks) + Final value expression (1 mark). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Model Question] **(a)** For a unity-feedback system with plant $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$, design a PI controller using the Ziegler-Nichols ultimate cycle method. The ultimate gain is $K_{pu} = 4$ and the ultimate period is $T_u = 3$ s. Calculate $K_p$ and $K_i$ and write the controller transfer function. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the effect of P, PI, and PID controllers on the following time-domain specifications: (i) Rise time, (ii) Steady-state error, (iii) Overshoot, and (iv) Stability. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to (a): **Step 1 — Recall Z-N Formulas for PI Controller:** $$K_p = 0.45 \, K_{pu}, \quad T_i = 0.833 \, T_u, \quad K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $K_p$:** $$K_p = 0.45 \times 4 = 1.8$$ **Step 3 — Compute $K_i$:** $$T_i = 0.833 \times 3 = 2.499 \text{ s}$$ $$K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{1.8}{2.499} \approx 0.7203 \text{ s}^{-1}$$ **Step 4 — Write the Controller Transfer Function:** $$C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} = 1.8 + \frac{0.7203}{s} = \frac{1.8 s + 0.7203}{s}$$ **Final Answer:** $$\boxed{\; K_p = 1.8, \quad K_i = 0.7203 \;}$$ $$\boxed{\; C(s) = \frac{1.8 s + 0.7203}{s} \;}$$ --- #### Model Solution to (b): | Specification | P-Only | PI | PID | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **(i) Rise Time** | Decreases with $\uparrow K_p$ | Decreases further | **Greatly decreased** | | **(ii) Steady-State Error** | Reduced but **non-zero**: $e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1+K_p G(0)}$ | **Eliminated** ($e_{ss} = 0$) | **Eliminated** ($e_{ss} = 0$) | | **(iii) Overshoot** | **Increases** with $K_p$ | **Greatly increases** (integrator wind-up) | Slightly increased, **damped by $K_d$** | | **(iv) Stability** | Degrades for high $K_p$ | Can be unstable without $K_d$ | **Best stability** (with tuned $K_d$) | **Explanation (4 marks for content + 3 marks for tabular comparison):** - P-only gives fast response but always leaves a residual offset. - PI removes the offset but can cause large overshoot and slow settling. - PID combines P's speed, I's accuracy, and D's damping — the **best overall performance**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Model Question] **(a)** Derive the closed-loop transfer function $\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)}$ of a unity-feedback system with plant $G(s)$ and PID controller $C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$. Show that the integral term makes the system **Type-1** and explain the implication on steady-state error. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A DC motor has transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{s(0.5s+1)}$. Using Simulink-style block operations, design a PID controller with $K_p = 2$, $K_i = 1.5$, $K_d = 0.3$ and find (i) the closed-loop characteristic equation, and (ii) determine the **system type** and the steady-state error for a unit ramp input. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to (a): **Step 1 — Standard Closed-Loop Formula:** For unity feedback, $\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s) C(s)}{1 + G(s) C(s)}$. **Step 2 — Substitute $C(s)$:** $$C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$ $$G(s) C(s) = \frac{G(s) (K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)}{s}$$ **Step 3 — Closed-Loop Transfer Function:** $$\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s) (K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)}{s + G(s) (K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)}$$ **Step 4 — Determine System Type:** The **system type** is the number of pure integrators (poles at $s = 0$) in the **open-loop transfer function** $G_{OL}(s) = G(s) C(s)$. Since the PID controller has a $\frac{1}{s}$ term, the open-loop has at least one pole at the origin → **Type-1 system**. **Step 5 — Implication for Steady-State Error:** For a Type-1 system: - **Step input** $R(s) = 1/s$: $e_{ss} = 0$ - **Ramp input** $R(s) = 1/s^2$: $e_{ss} = \frac{1}{K_v}$ (finite), where $K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s G(s) C(s)$ - **Parabolic input**: $e_{ss} = \infty$ --- #### Model Solution to (b): **Step 1 — Open-Loop Transfer Function:** $$G_{OL}(s) = G(s) C(s) = \frac{1}{s(0.5s+1)} \cdot \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right)$$ Substituting $K_p = 2$, $K_i = 1.5$, $K_d = 0.3$: $$C(s) = 2 + \frac{1.5}{s} + 0.3 s = \frac{0.3 s^2 + 2 s + 1.5}{s}$$ $$G_{OL}(s) = \frac{0.3 s^2 + 2 s + 1.5}{s^2 (0.5 s + 1)}$$ **(i) Closed-Loop Characteristic Equation:** $$1 + G_{OL}(s) = 0 \implies s^2 (0.5 s + 1) + (0.3 s^2 + 2 s + 1.5) = 0$$ Expanding: $$0.5 s^3 + s^2 + 0.3 s^2 + 2 s + 1.5 = 0$$ $$0.5 s^3 + 1.3 s^2 + 2 s + 1.5 = 0$$ Multiplying by 2 to clear decimals: $$\boxed{\; s^3 + 2.6 s^2 + 4 s + 3 = 0 \;}$$ **(ii) System Type and Steady-State Error for Ramp:** - **System Type:** The open-loop $G_{OL}(s) = \dfrac{0.3 s^2 + 2 s + 1.5}{s^2 (0.5s+1)}$ has **two poles at the origin** $\Rightarrow$ **Type-2 system**. - **Velocity Error Constant:** $$K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s G_{OL}(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s \cdot (0.3 s^2 + 2 s + 1.5)}{s^2 (0.5s+1)} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{0.3 s^2 + 2 s + 1.5}{s(0.5s+1)} = \infty$$ - **Steady-state error for unit ramp:** $$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{K_v} = \frac{1}{\infty} = 0$$ **Conclusion:** A Type-2 system tracks a ramp input with **zero steady-state error**. (However, practical systems always have finite gain, so the result is "near-zero" in reality.) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the integrator in the PID transfer function.** The most common error is writing $C(s) = K_p + K_i + K_d s$ instead of $C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$. The integral is a **division by $s$**, not a constant. > 2. **Confusing parallel form with standard (ISA) form.** In ISA form, $C(s) = K_c\left(1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$ — the $K_c$ multiplies **all three terms**, not just $K_p$. Always clarify which form the question is using. > 3. **Ignoring units of $K_i$ and $K_d$.** $K_p$ is dimensionless, $K_i$ has units of $\text{s}^{-1}$, and $K_d$ has units of seconds. Writing units in the answer earns a half-mark bonus. > 4. **Mishandling the Final Value Theorem.** It is only valid if all poles of $sE(s)$ are in the left-half $s$-plane. If the system is unstable, the theorem gives a **wrong** answer — always check stability first. > 5. **Z-N tuning misconception.** The Z-N formulas give a starting point that yields ~25% overshoot, **not** an optimal response. Students who claim "Z-N gives the best tuning" lose marks. > 6. **Sign error in summing junction.** In Simulink, the Sum block is configured as `|+-|` (positive for reference, negative for feedback). Reversing these signs creates a **positive feedback** system that diverges. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:** - **PID Equation:** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dot{e}$ - **PID Transfer Function:** $C(s) = \dfrac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$ - **Three terms:** **P** = present, **I** = past, **D** = future. - **Effect of $K_p$:** Faster response, more overshoot, reduces (but doesn't eliminate) steady-state error. - **Effect of $K_i$:** Eliminates steady-state error, increases overshoot and settling time, can cause integral windup. - **Effect of $K_d$:** Reduces overshoot, damps oscillations, amplifies noise, improves transient stability. - **PI controller** is the most widely used industrial variant (no derivative, so no noise issue). - **PID Transfer Function adds:** 2 zeros + 1 pole at origin to the open-loop system. - **System Type:** P-only → Type 0 (in general), PI/PID → **Type 1** (in general). - **Ziegler-Nichols PI Tuning:** $K_p = 0.45 K_{pu}$, $T_i = 0.833 T_u$. - **Ziegler-Nichols PID Tuning:** $K_p = 0.6 K_{pu}$, $T_i = 0.5 T_u$, $T_d = 0.125 T_u$. - **Steady-state error for step input** is **0** for PI/PID; **non-zero** for P-only. - **Steady-state error for ramp input** is $\frac{1}{K_v}$ for Type-1; **0** for Type-2. - **Final Value Theorem:** $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \dfrac{sR(s)}{1 + G(s)C(s)}$ — only valid for **stable** systems. - **MATLAB Commands:** `pid(Kp, Ki, Kd)`, `pidtool(G)`, `step(feedback(G*C, 1))`, `bode(G*C)`. - **Simulink Block:** *Continuous Library → PID Controller* (double-click to set $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$). - **Always** start with $K_i = K_d = 0$ (P-only), increase $K_p$ until response is acceptable, then add $K_i$ to remove offset, then add $K_d$ to damp overshoot. - **Anti-windup:** Use `pid(Kp, Ki, Kd, 1/Tt)` in MATLAB to enable back-calculation anti-windup (essential for actuator saturation).
